# Thread: I was doing great until....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-04-09T09:01:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-12925>

I was doing great until we went on vacation....and now we're back, there's always some reason to veer off the reset rules....once you go off its so hard to tighten up the reins...it's the hardest thing! Today's easter... there's a reason to eat. I cooked breakfast for my son and granddaughter....so had to have muffins (glutenfree, but...!) Then to dinner with friends....wine, dessert, help me.......helllp!

I know almost all of you have been here at one time or another. You've been on a diet or a "plan" and then fallen off, way off, and going back is almost impossible.... I am going to have to find out what makes it possible to go back on plan. Just venting but if anyone has any insights, tips, or encouragement, please chime in.

### Petra — 2012-04-09T11:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-12928>

Just relax, take a deep breath.

As soon as you can, sit all by yourself for at least 30 minutes. No interruptions and think, think WHY you want this to work.

There has to be something deep down inside of you that want this to work.

Find it.

Love and care,

Petra

### Glamorama — 2012-04-09T12:46:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-12931>

Hi! It's normal... I had some Lindt milk chocolate yesterday and felt horrible after. The pure physical discomfort I was in was enough to get me back on track. Just wake up, start your brand new day with a really big BAB and up the fat if you have to... The cue is to get as full as possible on healthy foods, and cravings for carbs will go soon enough.

### caroline — 2012-04-09T13:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-12934>

don't beat yourself up- life happens. tomorrow will be a new day

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-09T16:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-12961>

Start by going back to a BAB. Eat a big protein/fat breakfast. Salad for dinner.

Get up the next day, eat a big protein/fat breakfast and salad for dinner.

Day three, repeat. Usually by this time, things start to sort themselves out. I do not know why, but 72 hours seems to be the magic point for me.

### janagram — 2012-04-09T21:11:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-13088>

I can't thank you guys enough! Each of you has said something that adds support to my aim. Oops, there I've said the word aim! Now I've gotta do it. If the wrd aim hadn't leapt out of my fingers I could still waffle, but "aim" is serious! Thank you all! Maybe I'll use this thread to follow up.

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-14T20:20:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-15097>

Janagram maybe this will help.

I am a carb sugar pastry cake cookie LOVER. Hard to think of breakfast without toast. If, 7 weeks ago, pre-leptin reset me would have looked into my food log that I've eaten the last few weeks, I'd not have wanted to go on the program! Go to a ball game without peanuts and hot dogs and soda and ice cream? Never! Not enjoy food to soothe my blue moods? Unthinkable! Have a bday without a cake ...unheard of!

But see, that is focusing on what you CANNOT have. Doing that will keep you from getting on the wagon.

Someone else posted, look inside and think what you DO want to have. For me it was just not feeling crappy,, like I was stuffed into a sausage casing, not winded at the top of a flight of stairs. The weight loss to me is just a by product of healing. So I focus on what I want to have - and I focus on what I CAN EAT. Bacon! Eggs! Steaks smothered in onions and mushrooms cooked in pastured butter!

At first I just focused on the BAB. And I found that it, with heaping helpings of CO (I melt it in a shot glass and drink it like a shot!), I started to crave, yes crave, meat and veggies. And was supremely happy eating what I was eating. And felt a sense of empowerment going to lunch with friends, because I can find something I can eat EVERYWHERE... Even if it is a burger without a bun. My friends think they know what I'm doing - but over time they learned no dairy, no nuts, no legumes, no grains... Not just low carb? You're crazy! (go ahead and think it but it's working!). They are 'proud' of how no matter if we go to a Mexican place, I get a salad with chicken and salsa and guacamole and don't touch the basket of chips. You couldn't pay me to eat them though...

I guess what I'm saying is eating that BAB will check your cravings and get you on track, and if you act as though you are determined to find a way to eat paleo even in any circumstance, you will be able to.

Whatever you are looking for, you will find it. I went to a baby shower. I made sure to have my BAB before (which usually gets me through til dinner!) and I was mentally prepared to have nothing but ice water the whole time. If I'd been looking for a reason why I could not eat paleo, I'd have found one. But I wasnt. They had tea there.. Fine. They had little cute bundt individual cakes that looked delicious..... They had a spinach crust less quiche. It had some Parmesan cheese in it but all else was ok. So I had a square of that, some water, some tea. I left there feeling awesome about my choices (and excited for my friends baby, of course!)

The moral of the story is - when faced with a menu look for what you CAN have and be determined to find it, even if you have to ask the server for a side salad with oil and vinegar instead of rice or potatoes....if you are determined to stay on, you will find a way! If you're looking for a reason to slide, you'll find that too.

As a good friend of mie always says -if you argue for your limitations, you get to keep them! So I argue for what I want, not for why I can't do it.

Hope that helps... You gotta wanna, you gotta believe. I have read just about every one of jacks blog posts and all the comments, some more than once. I find a forum like this, immersing myself in the science, helps my belief in 1) that it WILL WORK -and 2) that I can do it, with a little boost from the cheer squad now and then... And last but not least, try to find new recipes and fix 1-3 new things every week to put into your meal rotation to keep things interesting.

I swear if I read pastry recipes all day I'd stumble. I read recipes for things I can eat... And get inspired. jacks cookbook is great for that!

### chipmeister — 2012-08-21T01:27:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-45940>

Wow...what a great thread. I agree whole heartedly with all that has been said prior but one thing for me which keeps me on track is this: You must read and understand how eating the "bad" stuff is actually destroying you...it isn't just bad for your diet, it is doing nothing but providing you a small level of satisfaction for a short period of time while destroying your body from the inside.

That is what helps to motivate me when making my decisions. I want to live a long and healthy life. I don't want to be one of those guys wondering if I brought enough meds on my vacation, or trying to remember to bring my morning meds to breakfast and evening meds to dinner. I don't want to have to worry about any pills other than vitamins.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-21T01:50:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-45944>

One though I had when re-reading this is. . .part of this is a "fake it till you make it thing". By just DOING IT, over and over again, even when you falter (get back up and BAB damnit!) that what you WANT actually changes! Very few never fail. . .expect to fail. But I bet what happens, even without planning or desire is each time it's a little longer between fails. . .and each time that wheat-yummy tastes a little less awesome. . .and pretty soon you go weeks without even thinking about those things that used to bring you pleasure.

I'm not one for "tricking" myself or playing behavior games. . . and believe me I had a love affair with delish foods - but if you keep getting back up and starting over when you fail - YOU change.

### bigknitwit — 2012-08-21T01:57:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-45948>

I do what Jack said somewhere. In short it was something like, "embrace the discomfort." Really feel your level of discomfort and experience it. Remind yourself of previous discomforts that you got past and beyond (I think of my first several weeks on this diet), and in particular remember the discomfort you felt then. I find that when I do this, I find I can move forward in a better state of acceptance, rather than constantly fighting with it.

### popcornsally — 2012-08-23T05:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-46482>

+1 on the old me thinking, "No way can/will I do that," giving up pastries and other junk food. I have also been a serious cheese lover, but now since my chronic sinus headaches are gone, I am not even tempted by dairy. Not worth it!

Enough time goes by and you brain starts working again. Then it's not that hard to say no to food that messes you up for days.

### Rosekelly — 2012-08-23T18:35:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-46545>

I attended a discussion group last night where they always have snacks laid out. This time someone brought a beautiful cake. I didn't feel tempted by the cake, but I realized that I missed the social bonding over food. That's really more important than the actual food. Eating is communal, and refusing to eat when you've been offered a delectable treat can feel as unsociable as refusing to laugh at a really good joke. And we all want to fit in with our friends.

But my friends understand. I'm just the one that doesn't eat the cake, or the chocolates, or the crackers, etc. And last night we actually got talking about this, and I opened up more about my reasons, and damned if they didn't all have some of the same doubts about all this sugar. One has really been wanting to cut back but feels addicted. She told me afterwards she didn't even like the cake.

So maybe we quietly help our friends and families by not eating the cake with them. Just food for thought.

### Lyndra — 2012-08-23T19:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-46555>

Starfish. You're having a positive influence. So good your friends understand and are interested in hearing new ideas.

### caroline — 2012-08-24T00:49:11+0300

>
>
> So maybe we quietly help our friends and families by not eating the cake with them. Just food for thought.

I relate to what you are saying so much - I too am the friend that doesn't eat the cake or crackers or chocolates. That is nice to think that maybe I am having an impact in a subtle way. Thanks

### vlynnb — 2012-08-24T06:17:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-46668>

Rosekelly and Caroline, I will think of you at my monthly book club when they bring out the goodies. And Jana, I really do relate and hope you are still trying. Most of my problems have come in the last few months frequent visitors, then an illness and then death of a long time friend, more visitors coming to the funeral, having to care for lots of grandchildren for one reason or another ... always something. Actually I haven't been too non-compliant, I am just a little more than a little disorganized and live far out in the country so sometimes if someone eats my planned protein its a long way to the store and I have a hard time getting my BAB and that is when I have trouble, That's when I succumb to just a tiny little bite of something one of my gourmet chef children have come up with. But I agree with others that the discomfort and sinus problems afterward are making it easier and easier to say no. And my tastes and cravings for their creations have changed.

My children are harder to deal with than my friends at book club. The children think I am a little nuts, if the friends do, they are too polite to say so. Well, most are. And after several months of turning the friends down two of them have joined me at least in trying to get rid of yeast in their systems. The others in the group are now trying to make sure there is at least something we can eat.

In fact the book club has kept me on target for another reason. I heard one lady who is not always gracious whisper to another that I would never succeed at keeping the weight off after the Hcg diet (she and her husband didn't), So, I have to succeed! Besides, I really like her husband and his health is starting to deteriorate so I want to be a good influence for him.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-25T20:38:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-was-doing-great-until.738/post-47079>

For me, it goes in cycles on my thoughts regarding this whole situation.

Sometimes no matter what I feel so good and I am so confident, I don't care what people try to offer me. To state, I NEVER actually have any 'cravings' at all. However, the social stigma is REALLY hard to overcome. To be the 'weird' one...or sometimes can't help but think about the people that do EVERYTHING wrong yet still look/feel (better) than you do...what the hell?

So I guess I always just hope for the days I feel confident and strong...b/c while I can resist, the emotional grief I feel for those cyclical times is damned hard.

Thanks.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-26T03:54:06+0300

hehe I'm the same way. . .someone says "you can't do this" and by god I'm going to Fing doing it!

### caroline — 2012-08-26T04:30:02+0300

I am now at a really decent weight for me \[5'5" and 59K\] - but someone is always saying - are you sure you aren't getting too thin??? I am definitely not too thin but it seems to disturb people for some reason.

### janagram — 2012-08-28T20:16:53+0300

# Thread: T3 and reverse T3?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T14:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12941>

Hi all,

Years ago (1989), I had an overactive thyroid. My doctor offered 2 treatments. One was to use radioactive iodine to kill of the entire thyroid, making me forever dependent on pills to get my T3. The other was a partial thyroid kill off, using limited amounts of radioactive iodine to 'normalize' the T3 levels by not killing off the entire thyroid.

Naturally, I wanted to selectively kill off the thyroid, so that I wouldn't need pills to have a normal thyroid activity. But, the doctor said this was not really a good solution as I'd have to test for T3 levels constantly and would probably have to take some small amount of synthetic T3 pills anyway.

So, I caved in and allowed the entire thyroid to be killed off, and have taken 175 mcg of synthroid (synthetic T3) ever since 1990.

I understand the basic operation of the thyroid system (T3, TSH (also called T4)). I find it interesting that D. J's leptin reset addresses an abnormal hypothalamus response, because the hypothalamus is part of the thyroid system.

But, **_what is reverse T3???? I read some online reverse T3 websites, but I'm still not getting it.....why is reverse T3 so important_** and why do lab work discussions on this list seem to downplay the importance of T3 levels and accentuate the significance of reverse T3 levels????

TY.

Art

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-09T16:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12958>

Hi Art, the hypothalamus is not part of the thyroid system, but they are both part of the endocrine system. Minor point. Normally, the body will convert T4 into the bio active hormone T3. T3 is the one that is used at the cell level. When the body is under stress, physical or mental, some of the T4 is converted to reverseT3 (rT3). To your cells, the rT3 looks like T3, and the rT3 acts like a placeholder taking up a position and that doesn't doesn't allow the real T3 to fit into the slot. So the rT3 is taking up room but not doing any of the work, and it's blocking the real deal from doing the work too.

That's a bit simplified, but I hope it makes sense.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-04-09T16:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12960>

hi, you have some pretty basic info backwards here

TSH = Thyroid stimulating hormone this is issued by the pituitary in response to signals from the body. TSH causes the thyroid to issue T4 hormone, T4 hormone is further down regulated into T3 (active hormone) or reverse T3 (RT3, inactive, supposedly) hormone.

synthroid is synthetic **T4 the storage hormone

cytomel, cynomel, Liothyronine are all versions of synthetic T3** the active hormone

taking synthroid requires the ability to convert T4 to T3, conversion happens in the liver amongst other places, if you can\`t convert T4 to T3 you will continue to feel hypo on synthetic T4 medication a better choice is natural dessicated thyroid which is a combo of T4, T3, T2 and T1 and mimics the ratios that are naturally by the human body (this is also brand named armour, erfa, naturethyroid amongst others)

when you body wants to slow your metabolism down it will tell the T4 to produce more RT3 instead of T3 thus the greater amount of RT3 you produce the more likely it is you are Leptin Resistant and thyroid resistant

Please read [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/) to learn more about RT3 and thyroid in general

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-04-09T17:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12991>

> **LisaAPB;13027 wrote:** So the rT3 is taking up room but not doing any of the work, and it's blocking the real deal from doing the work too.

This is also why a blood test of free T3 could indicate you have plenty of bio-available thyroid hormone, but you could still be hypothyroid at the cellular level.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T17:29:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-12998>

> **ColleenM;13029 wrote:**
>
>
>
> taking synthroid requires the ability to convert T4 to T3, conversion happens in the liver amongst other places, if you can\`t convert T4 to T3 you will continue to feel hypo on synthetic T4 medication a better choice is natural dessicated thyroid which is a combo of T4, T3, T2 and T1 and mimics the ratios that are naturally by the human body (this is also brand named armour, erfa, naturethyroid amongst others)
>
>
>
> when you body wants to slow your metabolism down it will tell the T4 to produce more RT3 instead of T3 thus the greater amount of RT3 you produce the more likely it is you are Leptin Resistant and thyroid resistant
>
>
>
> Please read [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/reverse-t3/) to learn more about RT3 and thyroid in general

Thanks to the both of you for the enlightenment.

It seems my reverse T3 is even more significant than the T3 levels, I'll make sure my doctor tests me for it when I need another synthroid test-my T3 was low at last test, and I'm now taking more synthroid, so another test will be needed soon.

I am absolutely LR and I'm sure my liver is clogged beyond being functional....especially since I have no gallbladder and I haven't been taking ox bile (have some on order). I'm of the opinion that I need to begin burning fat and that i desperately need to eliminate all sugar as it is toxic, in any amount. So, liver detox should help me immensely.

I'm doing LR now, and am moving towards Paleo-it's gonna be a bumpy uphill ride for me. But, I have begun the journey.

Again, ty to you both for replying, it helps allot!!!!

Art

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-09T18:07:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-and-reverse-t3.740/post-13012>

Another point.....there are two thyroid axis.....one that controls the brains use of thyroid hormone alone and then there is the peripheral system. In CT we rely on the brain only system. In warm adaptation we rely on both........huge difference when you are trying to understand why.

### primat — 2012-04-19T10:41:31+0300

# Thread: Night Sweats??? Anybody else or is it just me...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vkiernan — 2012-04-09T14:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-12942>

Ok, I'm confused, what is the chance CT is causing night sweats? I have had them for the past week & 1/2 during a really bad TOM and now even after Tom almost every night. What is up with this? The only other changes since starting CT over 7 weeks ago is that I am taking supps for adrenal fatigue: DHEA, pregnenolone and Isocort (now switched to HC but that was only mid last week). It is either menapause starting really fast, the new supps or CT. I'm thinking a call in to my doc is warranted. Something has changed for sure.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-09T15:46:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-12951>

I haven't had night sweats - but I have had periods where I'm so freaking HOT I CANT STAND IT.. I get all flushed, I'm walking around wearing tank tops, shorts, dresses to work. b/c I've been getting too hot...

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-09T16:05:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-12955>

### vkiernan — 2012-04-09T20:22:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13056>

> **Shijin13;13020 wrote:** I haven't had night sweats - but I have had periods where I'm so freaking HOT I CANT STAND IT.. I get all flushed, I'm walking around wearing tank tops, shorts, dresses to work. b/c I've been getting too hot...

It sounds like you are either LS or becoming cold-adapted or maybe both. Woohoo! Has dr. K confirmed you are LS at this point or do you know for sure?

I would be excited to be hot all the time. Although, I am definitely warmer than I have been. When I crawl into bed at night I have noticed that I warm up really quick. I only sleep in a light tank and undies and when my legs hit the cold sheets it is usually miserable; but lately they warm right up and I don't worry about climbing into a cold bed anymore. I also get a little warm feeling in my legs in the CT bath. That has got to be a little bit of cold adaptation wouldn't you think????

I also followed you with drinking cold things all the time. That was good advice. Rarely do I drink hot things and now if I do, they are too hot! Bring on the cold baby!

### vkiernan — 2012-04-09T21:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13087>

> **oliviascotland;13150 wrote:** I've had a couple of nights of horrible nightsweats, which were also combined with intense nausea. Don't know whether it's from the CT, though?

Sounds like it. We all sure have had our share of detoxing going on. I have to blame it all on CT cuz this isn't normal. But, I did ask my doc today about the hot flashes and she thinks I am taking too much HC in the afternoon. I am cutting back today by 1/3 and we'll see what tonight brings.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-09T21:42:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13106>

I would guess by taking the HC and DHEA and pregn that you are starting to "feel" some hormonal changes going on and manifesting as night sweats. Most docs say that you need estrogen but we know that is not the case. I think you have just stirred things up and with CT and the detoxing it's going to take a while for your body to find it's sweet spot.

I'm having a couple times during the night where I get warm but not even to take the one light blanket off. I think the hormones in my body are getting stirred up too but not quite as much as yours are. It might be the HC but I don't know. It's really the only thing I see that you are on that I am not on and our situations are eerily similar.

### Sherry — 2012-04-10T00:40:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13148>

I've been heating up for years. I'm ready to be done with it. Not even BHRT helped in the long run. Since starting CT things have been more intense and I can only hope that it is all the detoxing going on, the hormones readjusting themselves, etc. It's not linear either. The first few weeks were tough and then things leveled out in that area but now in the last few days they are intense again. I've even had hot flashes in the 48 degree tub!

Has anyone noticed if the hot flashes/night sweats are preceded by a fear or anxiety-type of feeling? One thing that has been happening to me is that I can lay down to sleep and initially fall asleep with no problem. Within minutes I am wide awake inside a 450 degree oven with a feeling of panic/slight unknown fear accompanying it. And then I lay there forever trying to get back to sleep.

Geez will I be glad when I'm detoxed and over the hump.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-10T00:47:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13150>

My TSH went from 2.1 to 4.8 My free t3 came up and my RT3 value went down.

### N~Doro — 2012-04-10T16:57:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13333>

> **Sherry;13217 wrote:** I've been heating up for years. I'm ready to be done with it. Not even BHRT helped in the long run. Since starting CT things have been more intense and I can only hope that it is all the detoxing going on, the hormones readjusting themselves, etc. It's not linear either. The first few weeks were tough and then things leveled out in that area but now in the last few days they are intense again. I've even had hot flashes in the 48 degree tub!
>
>
>
> Has anyone noticed if the hot flashes/night sweats are preceded by a fear or anxiety-type of feeling? One thing that has been happening to me is that I can lay down to sleep and initially fall asleep with no problem. Within minutes I am wide awake inside a 450 degree oven with a feeling of panic/slight unknown fear accompanying it. And then I lay there forever trying to get back to sleep.
>
>
>
> Geez will I be glad when I'm detoxed and over the hump.

I've been having hot flashes since the beginning of the year so I can't blame CT detox.

From what I have read, hot flashes can have as much to do with blood sugar regulation and adrenal problems as estrogen. The anxiety you are feeling is mostly due to a big release of adrenalin when your blood sugar drops. I usually have pounding heartbeat and racing mind for about an hour and a half or two hours before I can get back to sleep.

My lovely husband brilliantly put together the concept of the relaxed feeling following the dive reflex with my nightly frustration trying to get back to sleep. When I wake up with the inevitable hot flash, I get up and splash cold water on my face or go for an all out face dunk. Sounds totally counter-intuitive, but it has been easier to get back to sleep.

FYI on the hormone or other treatments for hot flashes, it is my understanding that they only work about 50% of the time because unless you are constantly testing estrogen levels, it is hard to tell if you are on an up or down swing. It only works for one of those conditions ( I think it is when e is dropping). During Peri-menopause estrogen charts look pretty much like a major earthquake event on a seismograph, hence all the fun times.

### Sherry — 2012-04-10T20:38:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13421>

> **N~Doro;13402 wrote:** I've been having hot flashes since the beginning of the year so I can't blame CT detox.
>
>
>
> From what I have read, hot flashes can have as much to do with blood sugar regulation and adrenal problems as estrogen. The anxiety you are feeling is mostly due to a big release of adrenalin when your blood sugar drops. I usually have pounding heartbeat and racing mind for about an hour and a half or two hours before I can get back to sleep.
>

>
> My lovely husband brilliantly put together the concept of the relaxed feeling following the dive reflex with my nightly frustration trying to get back to sleep. When I wake up with the inevitable hot flash, I get up and splash cold water on my face or go for an all out face dunk. Sounds totally counter-intuitive, but it has been easier to get back to sleep.
>
>
>
> FYI on the hormone or other treatments for hot flashes, it is my understanding that they only work about 50% of the time because unless you are constantly testing estrogen levels, it is hard to tell if you are on an up or down swing. It only works for one of those conditions ( I think it is when e is dropping). During Peri-menopause estrogen charts look pretty much like a major earthquake event on a seismograph, hence all the fun times.

This is helpful. I do know that the adrenals are involved although I have been regularly testing my four salivary cortisol levels and finally, in the last year they are in the proper ranges. Of course, I do know that there are a bunch of other ranges to test that I haven't done. Right now I'm laying off the testing for a while since CT can throw them all over the place. For years and years I tested my estrogens every 6 weeks and you're right they were like a seismograph.

Blood sugar could be an issue. Do you think it is a good idea to test FBG daily? It seems counter-intuitive to me because I'm not going to stop doing this keto Paleo and CT and for me I find the stress of the tests to not be helpful. But, if someone gave me a good reason to do it I would think about it.

I like the face dunk idea. I'll take that one on board and see how it works. I only have this problem right after falling asleep - not in the middle of the night so it seems like it would be easier to talk myself into getting up and doing it then rather than if it were in the wee hours. Thanks.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-10T20:38:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13422>

> **Shijin13;13219 wrote:** My TSH went from 2.1 to 4.8 My free t3 came up and my RT3 value went down.

So glad to hear it. You are definitely going in the right direction. I was making the same sort of progress before the new doc took me off t3 only and put me on armour. Now, my t3 has actually come up a little and my rt3 is going bad again.

I am seriously thinking about going to Tenn. to see Dr. K. When Dr. K gives me little messages sometimes after I post things, he has made me realize I am dealing with major health issues that shouldn't be taken lightly. I am so grateful everytime he clues me in. I don't know of a doc who can treat the whole like he will. If my insurance would at least cover some of the labs it would be very doable. At some point in one's life you have take the leap to get you above just treating the symptoms. I want my health back and am very willing to pay for it one way or another. I will never understand why when one has insurance that it matters what dr. you see. You are paying for something very limited and that is very frustrating.

### Sherry — 2012-04-10T22:37:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats-anybody-else-or-is-it-just-me.741/post-13468>

>

And then when you do use a bonafide doctor it turns out it isn't one they want to pay for. # Thread: zero sugar or just low carb?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T15:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12948>

I understand our bodies need some sugar.

But, does the body make enough sugar to meet its needs if we don't consume any sugar at all...consuming only carbs (which the bodies own metabolism converts to sugar internally)?

I'm diabetic and have tracked the diabetic diets official recommendations for years. It hasn't changed. Currently, the official diabetic diet recommends 45 to 60 (net) carbs per meal, 3 meals a day (no skipping meals) and allows an additional 30 carbs per day in snacks-snacks are encouraged to fight hunger).

But, Paleo and Dr. J seem to think 30 to 60 g of carbs per day is ok-a radical shift from the (current) official diabetes diet protocol.

I've been on 60 carbs per day since starting the leptin reset, and I think my body tolerates the lower carb consumption just fine. I think 60 carbs per day might be enough carbs for the human body unless large amounts of physical activity demand more fuel. But, should these carbs come from sugar free foods or is fruit (that contains actual sugar) ok as long as the carbs are limited to 60 g per day?

In other words.....**_if we consume 60 g of carbs per day without consuming any sugar(s), will the body make enough sugar to satisfy its needs?_**

TY.

Art

### Adria — 2012-04-09T16:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12957>

They'll keep recommending carbs for diabetics and the drug companies will continue to make their profits.

I've been keto diet for 20+yrs, meaning less than 20carbs total daily, no sugars or starches.

### ealachan — 2012-04-09T16:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12972>

> In other words.....if we consume 60 g of carbs per day without consuming any sugar(s), will the body make enough sugar to satisfy its needs?

Part of what the reset is doing is forcing your body to figure out that yes, it can run just fine on lower carbs. Until your body learns how to handle it, it can feel kind of crap-tastic (ie: the dreaded "carb flu")...but once your system catches on, it's great!

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T16:55:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-12974>

We have all been taught that we need carbs. Resist the brain washing.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T22:53:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13131>

> **shilohman;13043 wrote: We have all been taught that we need carbs. Resist the brain washing.**

**

I'm here because I have an open mind. Those who don't will enjoy their pasta and wheat bread because it's easier to remain the same than it is to CHANGE.......

But, don't we need a small amount of carbs because we need some glucose for proper cell function??? I'm not brainwashed now, _I'm trying to understand whether we need a small amount of carbs to make small amounts of blood glucose._**

And, if we need some glucose, can we get all the glucose we need from carbs, or do we need to eat small amounts of sugar (like we get in fruits)? I do understand that the fiber in fruit and the antioxidants are benefits and that it might not be wise to eliminate fruits entirely.

If Dr. J is monitoring, I'd appreciate having his input on this.

Art

### penguinmom718@yahoo.com — 2012-04-10T00:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13152>

> **ineedanLR;13200 wrote:
>
> But, don't we need a small amount of carbs because we need some glucose for proper cell function??? I'm not brainwashed now, _I'm trying to understand whether we need a small amount of carbs to make small amounts of blood glucose._**

Short answer, no. If your liver is functioning properly, it can make all the glucose you need through Gluconeogenesis.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T02:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13192>

The liver makes the needed carbohydrates from proteins, powered by fats for the conversion. The liver, however, needs to be in shape for this, and if it is constantly repairing itself from onslaughts of fructose, alcohol, or acetaminophen, it's abilities are hindered.

Your understanding is a product of mis-information. You do not need ANY sugar. Your gut uses some of the carbohydrates from vegetables to support the good bacteria and supress the bad, so some veggie carbs are good for you. Other carbs (fruit, sugar, starchy vegetables) are not needed at all. Depending on your level of health, you may be able to tolerate them fine. The low carbs approach is for T2D.

There is a ton of info on here about Diabetes, I think you can search the blog posts for a bunch of reading on it. Also, Jack has been able to rid people of the diabetes with his protocol.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-10T15:12:11+0300

TY Caveman. My formal diabetes education is almost 20 years old. I haven't gotten a formal refresher....so I'm bound to be a little 'off' with regard to the latest diabetes diet and treatment(s).

However, last week I paid for a session with a dietitian associated with the diabetes clinic at the local hospital. She is also a certified diabetes educator. But, the only diabetes diet she knew about was the standard diet, the one I learned about 20 years ago when I was first diagnosed with diabetes.

She know nothing of Paleo, Dr J and his work, leaky gut, Leptin/Leptin Reset. I didn't even menton the gaps diet protocol to her (out of fear of overload). She also was skeptical when I tried to explain the importance of minimizing our intake of foods known to cause inflamatory and autoimmune responses.

I am a techie (electrical engineer, retired), so I understand science and the roll it plays in our lives.

My guess is that this forum will explode with new members when the news of Dr. J's work becomes more mainstream...and, as other professional medical type people come on board. I hope the management is already working on expansion plans.

GL to all.

Art

This is one of my ongoing frustrations. It's very VERY difficult, when faced with a professional who tut-tut's you and acts like you are "one of those annoying people believing everything they think on the internet". It's the reality of our world though, so I think the idea that you just have to do it yourself - then let the CW see your n=1 and get curious.

What I am absolutely sure about is, looking to SUCCESSFUL examples as a "north star" so to speak. For instance, how many people are getting cured from their diabetes with that diet and medical intervention? I don't know the answer, but I bet very very few. On the other hand - there are examples down the paleo>gaps>kruse>etc path that can put forth some examples.

I guess in my mind, after seeing all the extremely poor studies being put forth as scientific "proof" and then carried over to CW - I personally put zero stock in a study unless I can actually find the whole thing and know what they did and can see exactly the outcomes. Too many unknown confounders IMO in almost all the medical research going on. Not to mention, much research is about finding drugs - not about helping humanity.

Anyway, I know it's so hard to just ignore that well trained pro telling you the opposite of what you think is correct. For me, that means I just don't go see them, but I'm a wimp that way. I don't feel the need to educate my doctor that is unreceptive.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-11T18:19:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-13771>

> **Cavemam;13400 wrote: how many people are getting cured from their diabetes with that diet and medical intervention? I don't know the answer, but I bet very very few. On the other hand - there are examples down the paleo>gaps>kruse>etc path that can put forth some examples**

**

Hi Caveman,

You are SO CORRECT!!!!

Yesterday, I spent all day (literally) researching the issue of what's the best treatment for high blood sugar and diabetes.

What I found was a disgusting misinformation campaign headed by the US government (the food pyramid chart, which has remained unchanged since 1950)! The chart is the basis or foundation of what we SHOULD BE eating to be healthy. The ADA refuses to recommend a lower carb diet, despite some of their own studies that show blood sugar drops tremendously once the patients dump the 180 to 210 carb per day limit that they currently support. Many who were insulin dependent have been able to return thier HBA1C levels and their fasting blood sugar levels to normalcy, by just reducing their carbs per day down to the 20 to 60 g range.

Sugar can be totally eliminated from a diet that is intended to be healthy and that it is quite harmful (see video called Sugar: The Bitter Truth, by a distinguished pediatric endocrinologist for the Univ of California). Yet, the FDA continues to list processed sugar on their 'gras' list (generally regarded as safe), which means it can be used in unlimited amounts for food and rink intended for human consumption.

It sure seems to me like we're being sold out by these entities that are failing to recommend proper diet, despite massive evidence that the current standards and recommendations are just plain DEAD wrong. Clearly the food manufacturers are determining what information is released to the public with regard to what's healthy and 'normal'.

_Anyway, my eyes have been opened._**

Regards,

Art

### differentstory — 2012-04-13T21:54:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-14829>

Funny I saw this thread today as I have having a terrible time. My mood and energy is at an all-time low. I cut out all carbs about 6 weeks ago - I only get carbs from a small amount of blueberries each day and green vegetables (and the occasional piece of chocolate). I'm probably hovering at 20g a day.

And I have been feeling like COMPLETE CRAP! It has gotten so extreme that this week I can't even go to work. I sit in a lethargic state at home and if I start to feel better, I get up to do something and 5 minutes later I am completely exhausted and have to sit down again. And I keep having these weird phases where I am just sitting on the couch staring into space and I am enjoying it!! (not good) It's like my body is demanding this hibernation state where I am not thinking, moving, nothing.

I felt great in the beginning of Paleo (first week or 2) and then this started. And it is getting worse.

So last night, out of desperation, I ate a sweet potato. And, wouldn't you know it, 2 hours later, it was like someone turned on the lights. I was up buzzing around the house like my usual self. I haven't been like that in probably 2 weeks! So, this morning, I wake up feeling good and decide to do the same thing - eat a small amount of carbs for breakfast (along with my BAB) and get ready to go to work (that's a first this week!). I felt great getting out of bed and even woke up early, but by the time I am driving to work, I can feel the exhaustion coming on. But, I am determined to get to work and research this issue.

(I should note that roughly 2 hours after eating breakfast, I am back to my usual self and feeling good again just like last night.)

So, today in researching all of this, I find this article and it is eerily similar to what I am going through:

[http://drcate.com/going-low-carb-too-fast-may-trigger-thyroid-troubles-and-hormone-imbalance/](http://http://drcate.com/going-low-carb-too-fast-may-trigger-thyroid-troubles-and-hormone-imbalance/)

So, just wanted to throw that out there for you. I have been bunking in the "no carb" camp for quite awhile and have been so impressed with my self for the past 6 weeks. This is the first time in YEARS that I have been able to eliminate (virtually) all carbs. Sadly, it's not working for me though.

Side note: in reading the 100+ comments, it doesn't seem that "slowly removing carbs" worked for anyone that posted and seems to be just a hypothesis so I'm skeptical of that as the solution, but it's nice to know I'm not alone and I'm not sure if removing all carbs is always the answer?

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-14T19:45:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-15085>

Add CT to the reset to help fix hormones. If you've been eating the carb laden standard American diet your hormone are out of whack for sure, and feeling crappy on lower carbs is a sign of that really... It should just tell you that you've got a ways to go. Jack comments on questions like yours in the blog somewhere, if I find it I will post here... You may get worse before you get better on paleo, but, if you continue to eat the SAD you will get worse and stay there!!!

### Owl — 2012-04-15T08:25:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-15256>

Art, I'm also terribly disappointed with the medical community's recommendations for diabetics.

My 73 year old dad has T2D, and last year, his kidney function had been getting worse. It was down to 18%, and his docs were telling him to get ready for a transplant when it got to 10%. They made appointments for him to get him pre qualified for it and get on the list. He told me this, and I asked him about his diet (I live on the other side of the country, so don't much see what he eats). He mostly ate the normal SAD but kept his BG in line enough to please his doctor. He said he had never been told what to eat by his doc or a dietician. I couldn't believe it. I went there on vacation shortly afterward, and has a long talk with him about what a transplant would mean for his health and lifestyle. I explained to him how blood sugar and insulin worked, and how going low carb would help his kidneys and give them a break. There's a doctor in Florida who is reversing kidney damage by putting patients on a ketogenic diet. I tried to get my dad to go there and try it. He said he would think about it, but I wasn't hopeful.

A few months later, I asked him how his kidneys were, and he told me he had cut way back on the carbs. And that his kidney function had gone up to 26%!!! And it has stayed there, so he doesn't need the transplant now!

It's really infuriating how doctors are not only allowing, but telling diabetics to eat the wrong things. Why is the relationship between carbs, blood sugar, and diabetes so hard for them to understand?

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-17T02:13:01+0300

@owl I hear ya, my mom and big sis are t2d and all the 'diabetic cookbooks' just use fake sugar and don't really control carbs in any way. They are taught to 'manage' their blood sugar and some I know who inject just figure if they eat sweets, they can always do a bigger injection to cover it. SCARY!

Even my doc told me about dr Bernstein's (berensteins?) diet book which iirc advocates VLC to turn diabetes around. And that was just to help me not need meds (I'm borderline). I'm hopeful she'll read wolfs book and jacks blog and help me to optimal.

### chocolate — 2012-04-24T17:49:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-18372>

Big regulations are awful against individual freedoms. Even if you have the land, you need to have an "in" with the packager, processor, or slaughterhouse. Those are often isolated to one location, or just two or three for the entire nation. To transport a cow, you need a health cert from a vet. A vet's house call is 300 to 400, their rigs are loaded and large and if you only have one steer to sell, you're negative a few hundred. There used to be a Stainless Steel Slaughter House on wheels that would come to your farm and slaughter your flock and package it for a piece of the action. It was very promising for small farmers. The industrial farmers got that shut down. You can't be cracking into the market. There are only a few poultry packing houses in the Central Valley and you bet your bippy they are factory owned.

### Dali Dula — 2012-04-25T14:44:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-18664>

Art, the answer is in the quilt and the blog. If you are interested in taking charge of your health you should read it all. My answer to your query from my experience as a t2d who has been off meds for 11 mo. and maintains a A1c of 5.6 is you should eat as little carbs as possible 30 gr a day. Once you are keto your body only needs carbs for red blood cell function and in the absence of dietary carbs your body can manufacture what it needs. I eat no fruit, sugar, grains, legumes or potatoes. If you eat some green veggies you will get all the carbs you need. And you will feel a whole lot better. You have found a website that can truly save your life. Love it.

A vet's house call is 300 to 400, their rigs are loaded and large and if you only have one steer to sell, you're negative a few hundred. There used to be a Stainless Steel Slaughter House on wheels that would come to your farm and slaughter your flock and package it for a piece of the action. It was very promising for small farmers. The industrial farmers got that shut down. You can't be cracking into the market. There are only a few poultry packing houses in the Central Valley and you bet your bippy they are factory owned.

There's still hope. My husband and his family still operate the butcher shop on wheels, which enables folks to enjoy home-raised, farm-fresh meats. It's local ag at its finest.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-25T16:34:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-18683>

> **differentstory;14898 wrote:** Funny I saw this thread today as I have having a terrible time. My mood and energy is at an all-time low. I cut out all carbs about 6 weeks ago - I only get carbs from a small amount of blueberries each day and green vegetables (and the occasional piece of chocolate). I'm probably hovering at 20g a day.
>
>
>
> And I have been feeling like COMPLETE CRAP! It has gotten so extreme that this week I can't even go to work. I sit in a lethargic state at home and if I start to feel better, I get up to do something and 5 minutes later I am completely exhausted and have to sit down again. And I keep having these weird phases where I am just sitting on the couch staring into space and I am enjoying it!! (not good) It's like my body is demanding this hibernation state where I am not thinking, moving, nothing.
>
>
>
> I felt great in the beginning of Paleo (first week or 2) and then this started. And it is getting worse.
>
>
>
> So last night, out of desperation, I ate a sweet potato. And, wouldn't you know it, 2 hours later, it was like someone turned on the lights. I was up buzzing around the house like my usual self. I haven't been like that in probably 2 weeks! So, this morning, I wake up feeling good and decide to do the same thing - eat a small amount of carbs for breakfast (along with my BAB) and get ready to go to work (that's a first this week!). I felt great getting out of bed and even woke up early, but by the time I am driving to work, I can feel the exhaustion coming on. But, I am determined to get to work and research this issue.
>
>
>
> (I should note that roughly 2 hours after eating breakfast, I am back to my usual self and feeling good again just like last night.)
>
>
>
> So, today in researching all of this, I find this article and it is eerily similar to what I am going through:
>
> [http://drcate.com/going-low-carb-too-fast-may-trigger-thyroid-troubles-and-hormone-imbalance/](http://http://drcate.com/going-low-carb-too-fast-may-trigger-thyroid-troubles-and-hormone-imbalance/)
>
>
>
> So, just wanted to throw that out there for you. I have been bunking in the "no carb" camp for quite awhile and have been so impressed with my self for the past 6 weeks. This is the first time in YEARS that I have been able to eliminate (virtually) all carbs. Sadly, it's not working for me though.
>
>
>
> Side note: in reading the 100+ comments, it doesn't seem that "slowly removing carbs" worked for anyone that posted and seems to be just a hypothesis so I'm skeptical of that as the solution, but it's nice to know I'm not alone and I'm not sure if removing all carbs is always the answer?

its not.....some people cant handle cold turkey drop of carbs.....and that is not how mammals face it in the wild either and neither should we......we slow down and ramp up based upon circadian cycles......that is what I have been saying for a long time now.......other people put words in my mouth. They are not my words.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-04-25T20:32:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zero-sugar-or-just-low-carb.742/post-18789>

> I've been on 60 carbs per day since starting the leptin reset, and I think my body tolerates the lower carb consumption just fine. I think 60 carbs per day might be enough carbs for the human body unless large amounts of physical activity demand more fuel. But, should these carbs come from sugar free foods or is fruit (that contains actual sugar) ok as long as the carbs are limited to 60 g per day?
>
>
>
> In other words.....if we consume 60 g of carbs per day without consuming any sugar(s), will the body make enough sugar to satisfy its needs?
>
>
>
> TY.
>
>
>
> Art

As a recently converted "paleo doc", I can tell you that just about everyone can do fine on around 50 grams of carbs a day. I don't "treat" T2D anymore, I try to reverse it. In the past 6 months I've seen a bunch of my patients do just that. It's not rocket science. The excessive carbs are the problem. By just dropping sugar and grain, you will see results. Some people can drop their a1c 3-4 full percentage points in 3 months, but most take longer, depending on how insulin resistant you are, you're overall health, and general hormone status etc.

Don't be content with a C+, i.e. keeping your a1c below 7 and taking 5 or 6 meds to do it.

Go for A+ with strict paleo diet, some cold therapy, lifting weights, and sleeping. It's totally doable.

The dieticians and conventional wisdom docs are behind the times or locked into dogma.

Of course we all need experts to help us out, but the bottom line is that you are in charge of your health. You have taken the 1st steps into a better, bigger, funner world. Please keep us abreast of your progress.

# Thread: Paleo strict enough??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ineedanLR — 2012-04-09T15:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/post-12952>

Hi All,

I notice the Paleo diet allows carrots (very high in starch) and peas and green beans (legumes). It allows allows some nightshade family foods, such as green pepper, red pepper, hot peppers, tomato...all of which invoke inflammatory responses within the body.

I happen to believe very strongly that inflammatory responses explain many of our various sicknesses and illnesses and that the medical community in the recent past has not given the elimination of foods that cause inflammatory responses the negative attention that they deserve with respect to our diets. I think the medial community will change their tune soon, but old habits are hard to break.

Dr. J is a pioneer, and should probably deserves some serious formal recognition for his revolutionary work in leptin reset and leaky gut research. Maybe a Nobel Prize in medicine?!

Getting back to the original topic however....**_is it possible/likely that the Paleo diet is indeed excessively lax with regard to some of the foods it allows?_**

Art

### ealachan — 2012-04-09T16:55:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/post-12973>

The Paleo diet is not necessarily made to be low-carb, in general. Many people don't need to be Very Low Carb, at least not year-round.

### Cron23 — 2012-04-09T17:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/post-12979>

I think it depends which Paleo diet you follow. Cordain is stricter than Wolf who is stricter than Sisson. I just read Cordain's 2012 The Paleo answer and he breaks down nightshades and autoimmune diseases, dairy, vitamins, etc. He says no peas, legumes etc, no dairy not even chia seeds and no nightshades for autoimmune. I may cut out nightshades this summer which means my friends and neighbors will be reaping the benefits of all these veggies from my organic garden.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-04-09T17:11:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/post-12987>

I think in general every food "kills" our body somehow. It's the only explanation for every food having a downside. We need to get to a point of optimization on what we never eat, what we eat on occasion, and what we treat as staples that are the least offensive and most nutrient rich for our bodies. It's nice to have a set of rules but none can apply to all and response needs to be considered. I see the mixed signals on coffee as a good example here (appropriateness based on cortisol issues, timing, quality of coffee). This is why CT leading to lower calorie eating might extend longevity - not the weight loss but the smaller food consumption. A summary document without writing another book on this (maybe and e-document) would be nice.

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T17:20:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/post-12993>

If you are doing the leptin reset, then you need to be vlc. If you are not fixing a problem like losing weight or eradicating an ai disease , then you can eat whatever. Dr K says he eats whatever he wants. But you have to realize everything has to be done in moderation .

### cantweight — 2012-04-11T20:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-strict-enough.743/post-13841>

I absolutely believe that "regular" paleo is not enough for those with a certain set of circumstances. In my case perhaps that was leptin resistance or chronic illness, it took a ketogenic approach to paleo to change my life.

I have done HCG which is very low calorie, I omitted the allowed grain/gluten but still had fruit-no good. I have been vegan-no good. I have done months of paleo that included fruit and sweet potato-better but not great.

Cutting carbs to under 30 daily, no grain or legume, and omitting fruit almost entirely, (I had berries twice in a month with whipped heavy cream on them) has been like the holy grail of dieting for me.

Like every other program I have tried through the years....I am waiting for this success to end....but until then I'm keeping it cold and keto

# Thread: Foods/Supplements that increase UCP1 and BAT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-09T17:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-12980>

Besides Green Tea and Bitter Melon - are there other foods or supplements we can take to increase the CT effect/transition?

Bitter Melon has a noticeable effect on me - it really ramps up the heat production.

Looking though the literature, I found a few things that seemed suggestive:

Red peppers (and maybe isothiocyanates?)

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3244046](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3244046)

[http://www.jappl.org/content/95/6/2408.full](http://http://www.jappl.org/content/95/6/2408.full)

EV Olive Oil

[http://www.jnutbio.com/article/S0955-2863(07)00013-7/abstract](http://http://www.jnutbio.com/article/S0955-2863\(07\)00013-7/abstract)

Garlic

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/129/2/336.short](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/129/2/336.short)

I realize that we don't want to mess with the Leptin Reset diet folks and that we want to eat high fat/protein - but what do you think? Would it help to add some hot sauce, garlic, etc?

Is there anything to this?

### Glamorama — 2012-04-10T01:11:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-13160>

Yes to the garlic. I don't know what it is, but it seriously cranks up my inner heater! Cayenne pepper is also very good for increasing metabolism.

### AKMan — 2012-04-10T01:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-13171>

I read an article with credible references a while back that said the thermogenic effect of peppers wasn't from the capsaicin, but another chemical that's not hot, so even peppers that lack heat are thermogenic. I couldn't find it, but did find this you may find interesting.

From Livestrong.com

Thermogenic foods may help increase metabolism and calorie burning by enhancing thermogenesis, a process in which the body burns calories to utilize the foods you have just eaten, converting those calories to heat. Your body mainly burns calories consumed through maintaining its regular metabolic functions and through physical activity, however a small percentage, about 10 percent, of calories are burned through diet-induced thermogenesis, according to the Mayo Clinic. The following is a list of foods that may enhance thermogenesis:

Spices

Consuming foods with spices such as red hot peppers or black pepper, increases thermogenesis and can potentially have a significant effect on feelings of satiety and fat oxidation, according to Dr. Margriet Westerterp-Plantenga and colleagues at the Department of Human Biology at Maastricht University in The Netherlands in a review published August 2006 in the journal Physiology & Behavior.

Capsaicin, the substance that gives hot peppers their pungent flavor, has been reported to increase thermogenesis, and in studies in Japanese men and women who consumed a meal containing capsaicin, the participants experienced an increase in energy expenditure immediately following their spicy meal, according to Dr. Westerterp-Plantenga.

Similarly, black pepper contains piperine, a substance that has been shown to influence thermogenesis through stimulating the nervous system, according to Dr. Westerterp-Plantenga and colleagues.

Green Tea

Green tea contains two substances--caffeine and polyphenols called catechins--that have been shown to boost thermogensis, and may enhance each other's effects. according to the August 2006 review by Dr. Westerterp-Plantenga and colleagues published in the journal Physiology & Behavior.

Catechins in green tea may increase thermogenesis through inhibiting a particular enzyme, and green tea contains high amounts of a certain catechin, called epigallocatechin gallate, that is "probably the most pharmacologically active," according to Dr. Westerterp-Plantenga and colleagues.

Coconut Oil

Coconut oil contains mainly medium-chain fatty acids, fats that, when consumed, have been shown to inhibit fat deposition through increased thermogenesis and fat burning, in studies conducted in animals and in humans, according to a review by Dr. Koji Nagao and colleagues at the Laboratory of Nutrition Biochemistry, Department of Applied Biochemistry and Food Science, at Saga University in Saga, Japan, that was published in March 2010 in the journal Pharmocological Research.

Protein

Diet-induced thermogenesis can also be affected by the proportion of macronutrients consumed--carbs, fats, and proteins--with high-protein diets being the most thermogenic compared to high-carb or high-fat diets, according to researcher Helen H. Hermsdorff at Universidad de Navarra, in Pamplona, Spain, and colleagues in a study published in the journal Archivos Latinoamericanos de Nutricion in March 2007. Sources of protein include lean cuts of red meat, poultry, eggs, and nuts.

Read more: [http://www.livestrong.com/article/198798-thermogenic-foods-list/#ixzz1raLhIMjC](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/198798-thermogenic-foods-list/#ixzz1raLhIMjC)

### Jim — 2012-04-10T01:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-13176>

> **Anna K.;13222 wrote:** HI, how much bitter melon do you take? thanks.

I take 500mg twice a day. Here is what Dr K does.

Found this in the Cold Thermogenesis protocol post

Mr. D. Says:

February 12th, 2012 at 1:48 pm

February 12th, 2012 at 2:49 pm

### freesia — 2012-04-10T02:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-13189>

A good way to get black pepper, is to put a half to a whole Tablespoon of black peppercorns in with the bones when making bone broth. The long slow cooking seems to release something warming in more than a "spicy hot" way.

I'm enjoying garlic too.

Early in LR (10 days in), so won't try the others yet but thanks for the useful list-to-look-forward-to.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-10T15:07:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-13308>

> **AKMan;13240 wrote:** I read an article with credible references a while back that said the thermogenic effect of peppers wasn't from the capsaicin, but another chemical that's not hot, so even peppers that lack heat are thermogenic. I couldn't find it, but did find this you may find interesting.

I believe capsaicin has the effect, but there are analogs that are not spicy that have the same effect.

They dubbed them nonpungent analogs aka capsinoids. I guess they are found in all peppers but aren't hot.

### chocolate — 2012-04-15T03:20:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-15190>

Dietary capsaicin reduces obesity-induced insulin resistance and hepatic steatosis in obese mice fed a high-fat diet.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19798065](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19798065)

I thought I would look to see if other thermogenic foods reduced insulin resistance. Probiotics seem to be anti-inflammatories that do this.

### Dextery — 2012-04-15T04:01:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-15200>

Just remember that all peppers are in the nightshade class of veggies and are a gut irritant. You can eat them. The question is: Should you?

### nonchalant — 2012-04-15T04:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foods-supplements-that-increase-ucp1-and-bat.746/post-15209>

Haha, I like your signature line, Dextery!

### optimus prime — 2012-04-09T17:25:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-results-im-lost-without-you.747/post-12995>

\*I posted this in a different thread before I saw this dedicated optimal labs forum. Sorry for the redundancy if you've read this already.

I just got some blood work done and I'd like some help deciphering the results. I have no idea what many of these values mean and I want to be well prepared when I go back for my follow up on Wednesday. I haven't had any hormones tested yet. I'll be doing that Wednesday as well. For now I just have the results of my "General Health Panel". Below are my thyroid, lipids and few other things. I posted the rest of the results to a google doc. It is set to accept edits, so you can type comments directly on the spreadsheet if you like. My questions are listed below the results on this post. Here is the link to my full lab report: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0AhpZKlPqqhpFdHBtTWVnZXp1Y2Q5OFA5dWJBOWVBRXc#gid=0.

\-B12 - holy cow, I have never tested my B levels before, but I guess that's one thing that's responding to the supplements...Weird. I also was taking them every day leading up to the test. Maybe I should stop taking them and ask for a restest on Wednesday.

\*Questions

\-I know it's hard to determine much without cortisol and sex hormone levels, but I mainly just want to know about the cholesterol and inflammation relationship. Could this be an indication of something else going on?

\-Does anyone know what would cause chronically low TSH and vitamin D with glaring hypothyroid symptoms?

\-Could low D be related to low TSH? What else can I do to get my Vitamin D up? I've been supplementing for at least a year and the level hasn't really budged. Also, not much sun in Chicago, but I feel like there's something else causing this. When I came back from 3 months in Phoenix,AZ where I spend a lot of time outdoors, my vitamin D was still in the low 40s

\-Finally, I thought I might have absorption issues but the high vitamin B and improved Iron are making me second guess that. So, I'm left wondering what could be causing low D...

Reminder: I'm 30, 5'4", 160 lbs (still no weight loss, 6 weeks into leptin rx)

Thank you for any and all help!

### quelsen — 2012-04-09T18:23:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-results-im-lost-without-you.747/post-13017>

lets tell a story here

Your body isn't asking your brain to make thyroxine.\[TSH\]

and you dont have good levels of active thyroxine in your blood\[Free (T3)\]

and you dont have good levels of storage thyroxine in your blood to convert into active thyroxine \[T4,Free\]

but you dont seem to have any conversion blockers \[Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO), Antithyroglobulin Ab\]

so the issue is most likely an energy imbalance.

Your BMI is normal range \[HDL Cholesterol\] to possibly thinner than normal

but you have a lot of oxidative damage to be controlled \[LDL Chol. Calc\]

and you are a poor metabolizer of glucose \[Glucose, Serum\] but we already knew that from your thyroid status.

Your ferritin is low but your B is high so you must be suplementing.

and with your D low and your T3 low your cortisol is out of balance and i would suspect your sleep is poor.

that is the story your labs tell me given what i know so far

ETA i missed your height and weight. that tells me more information

your estorgens are high ( pcos? ) as your hdl is strong but your weight is up. also causes you to make less t3 as adipose tissue exerts a depressive metabolic effect to thyroxine

### mambo — 2012-04-12T06:26:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-results-im-lost-without-you.747/post-14095>

Those are good numbers. Your thyroid #s are very similar to mine. TSH is really in the sweet spot. Your Free T3 isn't really that low: just a tad off from 3.25. There aren't many conventional doctors who'll decide to treat you based on those #s and your negative antibodies. You may be considered "subclinical" but most will claim that it's "psychosomatic."

Excellent pH, excellent Ferritin, which you claim is too low. Really, on paper, you look good. FBG isn't really reliable based on just one number. LDL could be from ApoE status or something else; even if it is, you're mostly Type A. Vit D at 40 is low if you're supplementing 5,000. I'm clueless why it would be low.

### herringsalad — 2012-04-16T17:46:27+0300

> **optimus prime;13064 wrote:** \*
>
> What else can I do to get my Vitamin D up? I've been supplementing for at least a year and the level hasn't really budged. !

What kind of Vitamin D supplements are you taking? You have to make sure that you take Vitamin D3 not Vitamin D2. And it's a good idea to take one that is in an olive oil capsule so that it's bioavailable. I take Healthy Origins - almost doubled my levels in less than six months.

### herringsalad — 2012-04-16T17:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-results-im-lost-without-you.747/post-15588>

Also if you want to build up your levels 5,000 IU is probably not high enough. According to the Vitamin D Council website, many adults use that much a day. So if you want to build up your levels shoot for 10,000 IU for three months, then test again. Once it's at the level you want then you can drop back down to 5,000 IU.

### chocolate — 2012-04-11T19:16:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-13800>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/w63j14p154872837/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/w63j14p154872837/) this talks about environmental clean up with the bacteria

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/3372815ut8252573/Mercury](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/3372815ut8252573/Mercury) Microbiology: Resistance Systems, Environmental Aspects, Methylation, and Human Health

Mercury has no beneficial biological role, and is highly toxic to all forms of life. Bacteria are involved in the global environmental cycling of mercury, both by reducing Hg2+ to metallic Hg0, which is less soluble in aqueous systems and therefore less bioavailable, and by oxidizing and methylating Hg species, and in the process making Hg more bioavailable and more highly toxic. The most thoroughly studied bacterial biotransformation of mercury is reduction by the widely distributed mer resistance operons found on plasmids and transposons in Gram-negative and -positive bacteria. **The products of these resistance operons transport ionic Hg2+ from outside the cell to the cellular cytoplasm, where mercuric reductase reduces divalent Hg2+ to Hg0, which is less toxic than Hg2+. Metallic mercury vapor, Hg0, is volatile under aerobic conditions, leaves the cell by passive diffusion, and is volatilized from the growth environment.**

I guess if its volatile under aerobic conditions, we need it gone!

### chocolate — 2012-04-13T00:30:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-14438>

[http://www.pnas.org/content/106/39/16799.full](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/106/39/16799.full)

Gliadin, the environmental trigger of CD, reportedly reproduces the effects of EGF on the actin cytoskeleton (16). These effects are very similar to the effects we reported for zonulin (7). Gliadin binds to the CXCR3 chemokine receptor (29), and this interaction is coupled to zonulin release from both intestinal cells (9) and whole intestinal tissues (10). Hence, it is likely that the gliadin-related EGF effects are mediated through zonulin release. We also have identified intestinal bacterial colonization as a stimulus for zonulin release (8). Gliadin and microorganisms both cause polarized luminal secretion of zonulin (8). Therefore, we focused our studies on early zonulin action (i.e., its activity at intestinal luminal side). This approach may appear counterintuitive, given the observation that both EGFR and PAR2 are expressed basolaterally (3, 30). However, evidence exists that they also are apically expressed (31). The fact that we have demonstrated that zonulin exerts a permeating effect, both in ex vivo and in vivo, when applied to the luminal aspect of the intestinal mucosa does not dispute the possibility that the protein acts basolaterally as well. When environmental triggers (i.e., bacteria, gluten) are present in the intestinal lumen, zonulin is released from enterocytes, a process that is mediated, at least for gliadin, by CXCR3 (29).

I think it has a job. It opens the gates for metals, sugars, and gliadin (rotavirus mimicker). We know what attracts the metals and they are supposed to be ok anaerobically (insulin receptors and dopamine receptors). That would make them winter. IL -15 when the sugar opens it and the metals. It goes after lyme, chagas, and other biofilms. The sugars act like antifreeze and are converted to lactic acid for a preservative and anaerobic fuel. That would make them winter or fasting. The rotavirus can't be outsmarting the body.... it's just ridiculous. But that is what activates the il 6 in the spring the same time you want the metal gone. If you pooped it out and the magnesium was consumed at the same time from the biofilms, it could be repatriated to the insulin receptors and thyroid and wherever else a gal needs her magnesium. I guess the right stuff would be there somewhere for the flora and fauna. That 's my notion of things. Just a theory.... I'd love to hear other ideas.

My family had that rotavirus once. The girl just ran and heaved. She didn't let it slow her down. The boy would only eat meat or starve and didn't get sick. You couldn't starve him into eating anything else but the odd broccoli head. My husband and I felt like dogs for a full day. Down and dirty, but felt fresh and renewed the next day. What seems to make it worse, is when people try to eat, from what I read. Rotavirus can happen in foals and they call it swamp fever. I guess it isn't always dirty handed kitchen help. Maybe it happens in swamps. Horses foal in the spring also. They also have probiotic issues and there is a terrible habit of giving them a shot of penicillin when they hit the ground. Stupid.
