### chocolate — 2012-04-21T00:46:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-17238>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120415151332.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120415151332.htm)

Blood Type A May Predispose to Some Rotavirus Infections

An antibody to the histo-blood group antigen A blocked infection by the virus into human intestinal cells in culture.

**"No one expected this," said Prasad. "Is there an emerging theme here with these intestinal pathogens? Do other viruses use these blood group antigens as a door to enter the cell?"**

Further studies identified a second rotavirus strain P\[9\] that uses the histo-blood group antigen as a receptor, he said.

Novel mechanism of transmission

"The question now is do different strains use other histo-blood group antigens in this way?" he said.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T03:58:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-17307>

\[h=1\]Metal Chelation and Inhibition of Bacterial Growth in Tissue Abscesses\[/h\]http://www.sciencemag.org/content/319/5865/962.abstract

Bacterial infection often results in the formation of tissue abscesses, which represent the primary site of interaction between invading bacteria and the innate immune system. We identify the host protein calprotectin as a neutrophil-dependent factor expressed inside Staphylococcus aureus abscesses. Neutrophil-derived calprotectin inhibited S. aureus growth through **chelation of nutrient Mn2+ and Zn2+**: an activity that results in reprogramming of the bacterial transcriptome. The abscesses of mice lacking calprotectin were enriched in metal, and staphylococcal proliferation was enhanced in these metal-rich abscesses. These results demonstrate that calprotectin is a critical factor in the innate immune response to infection and define metal chelation as a strategy for inhibiting microbial growth inside abscessed tissue.

### chocolate — 2012-05-07T19:54:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-21969>

Gut Microbiota Transplantation May Prevent Development of Diabetes and Fatty Liver Disease

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120419091026.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120419091026.htm)

ScienceDaily (Apr. 19, 2012) — Exciting new data presented April 18 at the International Liver CongressTM 2012 shows the gut microbiota's causal role in the development of diabetes and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), independent of obesity1. Though an early stage animal model, the French study highlights the possibility of preventing diabetes and NAFLD with gut microbiota transplantation -- the engrafting of new microbiota, usually through administering fecal material from a healthy donor into the colon of a diseased recipient.2

In the 16 week study, two groups of germ free mice received gut microbiota transplants; one set from donor mice displaying symptoms of insulin resistance and liver steatosis (responders), the other from normal mice (non responders). The donor mice were selected due to their response to being fed a high fat diet.

The germ free group that received microbiota from symptomatic mice (responder receivers -- RR) showed higher levels of fat concentration in the liver as well as being insulin resistant. The germ free group that received microbiota from healthy mice (non-responder-receivers -- NRR) maintained normal glucose levels and sensitivity to insulin.

EASL Scientific Committee Member Dr Frank Lammert said: "The factors leading to Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD) are poorly understood, but it is known that NAFLD and Type 2 diabetes are characterized, respectively, by liver inflammation and metabolic disorders like insulin resistance."

"This study shows that different microbiota cause different metabolic responses in animals. By implanting microbiota from healthy mice, the study authors prevented the development of liver inflammation and insulin resistance, both indications of liver disease and diabetes. Thus, gut microbiota transplants could have a therapeutic role in the development of these diseases."

**The RR mice also showed lower levels of microorganisms than usually found in the healthy gut. Lachnospiraceae was identified as the species most important in developing fatty liver and insulin resistance.**

### chocolate — 2012-05-07T19:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-21970>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22228654](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22228654)

Lachnoanaerobaculum a new genus in Lachnospiraceae; characterization of Lachnoanaerobaculum umeaense gen. nov., sp. nov., isolated from human small intestine, Lachnoanaerobaculum orale gen. nov., sp. nov., isolated from saliva and reclassification of Eubacterium saburreum (Prevot) Holdeman and Moore 1970 as Lachnoanaerobaculum saburreum comb. nov.

Abstract

Two new obligately anaerobic Gram-positive, saccharolytic and non-proteolytic spore-forming bacilli (strain CD3:22 and N1) are described. Strain CD3:22 was isolated from a biopsy of the small intestine of a child with celiac disease and strain N1 from the saliva of a healthy young man. The cells of both strains were observed to be filamentous with lengths of approximately 5 to >20 Âµm, some of them curving and with swellings. **The novel organisms produced H2S, NH3, butyric acid and acetic acid as major metabolic end products.** Phylogenetic analyses, based on comparative 16S rRNA gene sequencing, revealed close relationships (98 % sequence similarity) between the two isolates, as well as the type strain of Eubacterium saburreum CCUG 28089T and four other Lachnospiraceae bacterium/E. saburreum-like organisms. This group of bacteria were clearly different from any of the 19 known genera in the family Lachnospiraceae. While Eubacterium spp. are reported to be non-spore-forming, reanalysis of E. saburreum CCUG 28089T confirmed that the bacterium, indeed, is able to form spores. Based on 16S rRNA gene sequencing, phenotypic and biochemical properties, CD3:22 (CCUG 58757T) and N1 (CCUG 60305T) represent new species of a new and distinct genus, named Lachnoanaerobaculum, in the family Lachnospiraceae \[within the order Clostridiales, class Clostridia, phylum Firmicutes\]. Strain CD3:22 is the type strain of the type species, Lachnoanaerobaculum umeaense gen. nov., sp. nov., of the proposed new genus. Strain N1 is the type strain of the species, Lachnoanaerobaculum orale gen. nov., sp. nov. Moreover, E. saburreum CCUG 28089T is reclassified as Lachnoanaerobaculum saburreum comb. nov.

### chocolate — 2012-07-02T05:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-34944>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22260250](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22260250)

Ammonia increases paracellular permeability of rat brain endothelial cells by a mechanism encompassing oxidative/nitrosative stress and activation of matrix metalloproteinases.

> Department of Neurotoxicology, Mossakowski Medical Research Centre, Polish Academy of Sciences, Warsaw, Poland.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Ammonia is responsible for cerebral edema associated with acute liver failure, but the role of the vasogenic mechanism has been a matter of dispute. Here, we tested the hypothesis that ammonia induces changes in blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability by a mechanism coupled to oxidative/nitrosative stress (ONS) evoked in the BBB-forming cerebral capillary endothelial cells. Treatment of a rat brain endothelial cell line with ammonia (5 mmol/L, 24 h) caused accumulation of ONS markers: reactive oxygen species, nitric oxide and peroxidation products of phospholipid-bound arachidonic acid, F2-isoprostanes. Concurrently, ammonia increased the activity of extracellular matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2/MMP-9), increased cell permeability to fluorescein isothiocyanate-dextran (40 kDa), and increased the expression of y+LAT2, a transporter that mediates the uptake to the cells of the nitric oxide precursor, arginine. The increase of cell permeability was ameliorated upon co-treatment with a MMP inhibitor, SB-3CT and with an antioxidant, glutathione diethyl ester, which also reduced F2-isoprostanes. Ammonia-induced ONS was attenuated by cytoprotective agents l-ornithine, phenylbutyrate, and their conjugate l-ornithine phenylbutyrate, an ammonia-trapping drug used to treat hyperammonemia. The results support the concept that ONS and ONS-related activation of MMPs in cerebral capillary endothelial cells contribute to the alterations in BBB permeability and to the vasogenic component of cerebral edema associated with acute liver failure.
>
>
>
> Â© 2012 The Authors. Journal of Neurochemistry Â© 2012 International Society for Neurochemistry.

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T04:09:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-35205>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17250680](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17250680)

Oxidative stress activates protein tyrosine kinase and matrix metalloproteinases leading to blood-brain barrier dysfunction.

Haorah J, Ramirez SH, Schall K, Smith D, Pandya R, Persidsky Y.

Source

Center for Neurovirology and Neurodegenerative Disorders, Department of Pharmacology and Experimental Neuroscience, University of Nebraska Medical Center, Omaha, Nebraska 68198-5215, USA. [Jhaorah@unmc.edu](mailto:Jhaorah@unmc.edu)

Abstract

The blood-brain barrier (BBB) formed by brain microvascular endothelial cells (BMVEC) regulates the passage of molecules and leukocytes in and out of the brain. Oxidative stress is a major underlying cause of neurodegenerative and neuroinflammatory disorders and BBB injury associated with them. Using human BMVEC grown on porous membranes covered with basement membrane (BM) matrix (BBB models), we demonstrated that reactive oxygen species (ROS) augmented permeability and monocyte migration across BBB. ROS activated matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, -2, and -9) and decreased tissue inhibitors of MMPs (TIMP-1 and -2) in a protein tyrosine kinase (PTK)-dependent manner. Increase in MMPs and PTK activities paralleled degradation of BM protein and enhanced tyrosine phosphorylation of tight junction (TJ) protein. These effects and enhanced permeability/monocyte migration were prevented by inhibitors of MMPs, PTKs, or antioxidant suggesting that oxidative stress caused BBB injury via degradation of BM protein by activated MMPs and by PTK-mediated TJ protein phosphorylation. These findings point to new therapeutic interventions ameliorating BBB dysfunction in neurological disorders such as stroke or neuroinflammation.

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T04:13:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-35206>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19241241](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19241241)

> Involvement of ROS in BBB dysfunction.
>
> Pun PB, Lu J, Moochhala S.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Defence Medical and Environmental Research Institute, DSO National Laboratories, Singapore.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The blood-brain barrier (BBB) forms a protective barrier around the brain, with the important function of maintaining brain homeostasis.
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19241241](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19241241)
>
>
>
>
>
> Pathways thought to initiate BBB dysfunction include the kinin system, excitotoxicity, neutrophil recruitment, mitochondrial alterations and macrophage/microglial activation, all of which converge on the same point--reactive oxygen species (ROS). **Interestingly, ROS also provide a common trigger for many downstream pathways that directly mediate BBB compromise such as oxidative damage, tight junction (TJ) modification and matrix metalloproteinases (MMP) activation. These observations suggest that ROS are key mediators of BBB breakdown and implicate antioxidants as potential neuroprotectants in conditions like stroke and traumatic brain injury (TBI). This review explores some of the pathways both upstream and downstream of ROS that have been implicated in increased BBB permeability and discusses the role of ROS and antioxidants in neuropathology.**

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T21:59:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-35336>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10901420](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10901420)

Dietary cod liver oil decreases arachidonic acid in rat gastric mucosa and increases stress-induced gastric erosions.

> Science Institute, University of Iceland, Reykjavik.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The purpose of this study was to examine the influence of long-term feeding of dietary fat rich in either n-3 or n-6 fatty acids on the availability of arachidonic acid (20:4n-6) in major phospholipids of gastric mucosa in rats. Three groups of male Wistar rats were fed either a standard diet, a cod liver oil-enriched diet (10% by weight), or a corn oil-enriched diet (10% by weight) for 8 mon. **Dietary cod liver oil significantly reduced the level of 20:4n-6 in phosphatidylcholine (PC) and in phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) of gastric mucosa. The loss of 20:4n-6 was compensated for by eicosapentaenoic acid (20:5n-3) in PC, whereas the decrease in 20:4n-6 in PE corresponded to the increase in three n-3 fatty acids: 20:5n-3, docosapentaenoic acid (22:5n-3), and docosahexaenoic acid (22:6n-3). The level of 20:5n-3 was higher than the level of 22:6n-3 both in PC and PE of mucosa in rats fed cod liver oil. Diets supplemented with corn oil increased the level of 18:2n-6 but decreased the monoene fatty acids 16:1 and 18:1n-7 in PC but not in PE of gastric mucosa. The 20:4n-6 levels of both PC and PE were markedly reduced by dietary cod liver oil, to about one-third of control levels. Similar changes were also observed in the stomach wall. Gastric erosions were observed in all rats exposed to restriction stress, but this form of stress induced twice the number of erosions in rats fed fish oil compared to control rats or rats fed corn oil. We conclude that a diet rich in fish oil altered the balance between n-6 and n-3 fatty acids in major gastric mucosal phospholipids, markedly reduced the availability of 20:4n-6, and increased the incidence of gastric erosions induced by restriction or emotional stress.**

### Michael — 2012-07-05T11:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zonulin-leaky-gut-and-blood-brain-barrier-articles.748/post-35599>

> **chocolate;13082 wrote:** [http://blog.cholesterol-and-health.com/2011/04/new-genetics-part-v-is-intestinal.html](http://http://blog.cholesterol-and-health.com/2011/04/new-genetics-part-v-is-intestinal.html)

> genetics determine intestinal flora
>
>

>
> Except Chris didn't say that. In fact he highlights in bold:
>
>
>
>
> > the mere fact that a trait is heritable not only does not show that it is "genetic" but it does not even show ...
>
>
>
> Don't be misled into thinking that the word "inherit" indicates "genetics". I may inherit my father's watch: that's not genetic.
>
>
>
> Sure, you inherit your mother's gut flora. Why? In the womb, the gut of a fetus is sterile. However, when the baby passes through the birth canal, it swallows some of the mother's vaginal fluids. Those will contain bacteria because they migrate there from the digestive tract. (And this may be one reason why caesarian section is probably not a good start in life - because you don't get that early innoulation.)
>
>
>
> Chris did lay that out:
>
>
>
> Babies appear to acquire their first and main inoculation with gut microbes from the vagina and feces of their mother at the time of birth.

Dr. Natasha Campbell-McBride, the doctor who specializes in gut health and its relation to psychological problems - see her book on the GAPS diet - believes from her clinical experience that a gut microbiome can get worse over generations. So the grandmother eats a little worse than her ancestors; she passes the microbes on to her daughter (as described above); the daughter maybe eats a bit worse, or is bottle-fed (which also affects the flora), or has a few antibiotic courses - or all three - so the already-damaged flora she initially inherited from _her_ mother gets damaged further. This twice-generationally-damaged flora is what the baby starts with - remember, through swallowing during birth. This, says, Dr. McBride could explain the increase in conditions such as autism, since there is a strong connection between gut-health and how the brain functions.

I decided to do injections and started...today is day 3, just did third injection and 11:30 am and still loading today but only healthy fats (CO and butter), dark chocolate, some peanut butter, usually proteins.

Do I need to keep injections at the exact same time every day? I used to do this with sublingual. My first injection was at 2pm on Friday because I got my supplies in the mail and then did it as soon as I could. Then, I tried to move the time earlier yesterday and did it at 11am. Today, I missed 11 am, forgot, and did 11:30. How important is it? I could try to move it earlier to a more convenient time. I work at home, morning rush isn't good because the kids are around so I could do it after dropping them at school, come home to work, do injection, around 9:30, but that doesn't work on Thursday because I go straight to yoga from drop off at school.

I am doing the same as you Colleen, no fats or oils. Although I do have a question about soap and shampoo/conditioner. What do you use for these, oil free versions? I have a bath and shower gel that I use to wash my hands and in the shower. It's an Alba Organics and I used on previous rounds. Now I am wondering since it does say very emollient. Perhaps I would do better using something else. I could use a good recommendation for shampoo/conditioner. My hair is dry and on the coarser side and long. Thanks so much.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-15T20:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mixing-for-sublingual-rx-help.751/post-15348>

It's fine to vary the time a bit. There usually isn't any problem with that. I have never changed my shampoo or soap. Only my face and skin products. What dose did you start with?

### Claudia — 2012-04-15T21:46:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mixing-for-sublingual-rx-help.751/post-15371>

I am doing 125 iu, so one injection per day.

I don't use face and skin products and I don't wear makeup, except for some eye liner and mascara and lipstick sometimes. So I guess I am ok then?

### colleencoble — 2012-04-15T23:14:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mixing-for-sublingual-rx-help.751/post-15389>

No hunger on that dose? It's a tad low.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-04-19T15:13:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mixing-for-sublingual-rx-help.751/post-16554>

> **colleencoble;15458 wrote:** No hunger on that dose? It's a tad low.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-19T15:21:51+0300

# Thread: Learning how to breathe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-04-09T20:23:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13058>

For me, all signs point to breathing as something we will be dealing with soon on our journey to optimal. I feel to dumb sounding like I don't know how to breathe....but I actually think I do it wrong. Any tips on how to optimally oxygenate? Are there exercises or ways to train the body to breathe correctly? I tend to be a shallow rapid breather.

### Pardus — 2012-04-09T22:00:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13112>

Hi ColdBren,

I did a little research on Buteyko breathing a while back. It's a controversial method though...

Lately what I have been trying:

\- breathing from the diaphragm (not the chest cavity) similar to how a baby breathes (before they grow up and become self conscious of their tummy sticking out)

\- breathing slower

\- breathing through your nose, to moisten the air as it comes in.

### ColdBren — 2012-04-09T23:17:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13136>

nothing more beautiful than a baby with their stomach out for all to see.....i wish that didn't stop being cute lol

### SimonM — 2012-04-10T00:36:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13147>

Yeah, what is it with Buteyko? All sounds logical, but never seems to have caught on. @ColdBren, there is a stack of material in Buteyko and elsewhere saying that "overbreathing" - which sounds like you may be doing - is bad news. Carbon dioxide seems to be as important as oxygen for us, and rapid breathing decreases Carbon dioxide levels which then leads to us, paradoxically, breathing harder and getting lots of oxygen into our blood, but less of it gets out of our blood and into our tissues...

This is typically what happens at the start of (running) races and during extreme climbing.

Agree with Pardus: breathing through the nose seems to be a key idea, and taking fewer breaths is also good. [Stephen Elliott's website](http://http://www.coherence.com/) has a stack of info on breathing plus a free timer you can download to practise getting into their recommended rhythm.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-12T19:19:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-23431>

Yes, I agree, breathing can really alter your inner world and help those with problems with thoughts driving adrenals (like me). I agree with the breathing with your belly mentioned up there. I also like what is called the yogic three part breathing. Basically you imagine that you are breathing into your belly first, then middle chest, then upper chest and then you exhale in reverse order. I also will do 5 breathing - breathe in to the count of 5, hold for count of 5 and exhale for count of 5. I just bought this little gizmo that trains you on breathing if you wanna check it out-

[http://www.bulletproofexec.com/emwave2-hrv-data-export/](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/emwave2-hrv-data-export/)

good luck

# Thread: Jack's busy Marketing Monkeys -- a format problem
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### skline@peak.org — 2012-04-09T21:05:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13085>

You guys seem to be working non-stop today.

Anyway, I'm 65, and I need a moderately generous font size.

I don't see how to change that when reading other people's posts.

I can enlarge the posts with my browser, just by typing ctrl-+, however, once the font size gets big enough the page is too wide for my monitor. To see the whole line in the post, I lose the avatar and name of poster, and have to scroll back and forth.

A way to make the page narrower? A way to increase font size when reading the forum?

### admin — 2012-04-09T21:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13109>

There is not another solution to this other than the ctrl+ or ctrl-

### skline@peak.org — 2012-04-11T01:05:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13512>

> **Jack's Marketing Monkeys;13178 wrote:** There is not another solution to this other than the ctrl+ or ctrl-

Excuse me, why not?

If I use ctrl+ till the print is large enough, the lines are too long to read without scrolling.

Other sites have a little icon to click for a larger or smaller font. Why not here?

### admin — 2012-04-11T01:20:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13519>

I don't believe I've seen those icons on other sites. It has become universal to change font sizes using the ctrl+ and ctrl- in all browsers.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-04-12T06:53:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-14108>

> **Jack's Marketing Monkeys;13588 wrote:** I don't believe I've seen those icons on other sites. It has become universal to change font sizes using the ctrl+ and ctrl- in all browsers.

However, when one changes the size of font in articles using ctrl+, the line lengths adjust to the larger print. On this site, the lines just get longer and longer, till one has to scroll.

I'll see if I can fish up some sites which still have the font size icon. Maybe they have become fewer and I haven't noticed.

### Guest — 2012-04-10T15:22:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-getting-nasty-heartburn-at-night.756/post-13312>

I am a blood type O and the only time I would have problems with heartburn at night is when I use to eat animal protein and starches. ### edance83@yahoo.com — 2012-04-10T18:01:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-getting-nasty-heartburn-at-night.756/post-13367>

Glam is right. Anything with vinegar will stop acid reflux/heartburn. You can use apple cidar vin, reg vin, or even mustard (it has ving). But, I'd def start testing to see what food does it to you. For me it's any kind of tomato sauce type of thing. I can eat tomatoes themselves fine and even crushed tomatoes or salsa (unless it's made w/ tom paste) just fine though. It's weird.

### Owl — 2012-04-10T18:07:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-getting-nasty-heartburn-at-night.756/post-13369>

You need to take more HCL, with every meal that has protein. One capsule is not enough is you're having heartburn.

"If the first dose of Betaine HCL produced no noticeable stomach discomfort, try taking two capsules at the start of your next meal, again monitor for burning and or heaviness during and after eating. If taking two capsules produces some discomfort, but one capsule does not, restrict yourself to one capsule at the start of each meal. If the two capsule dose produced no discomfort, try three capsules at the start of your next meal.

If all goes well when taking three capsules at the start of each meal, stay with that dosage, most individuals will not require increasing the dosage to four capsules. Regular use of supplemental hydrochloric acid will in most cases "re-train" the stomach to produce higher concentrations of acid on its own. This process may take anywhere from several weeks to several months, depending on the individual. The most significant indication that acid production is improving is that Betaine HCL supplementation can no longer be comfortably tolerated.

An Example:

After having used the Betaine HCL for eleven weeks, at a dosage of two capsules per meal, without any discomfort, Joan now notices that she feels a burning sensation when taking the two capsules. She therefore reduces her dosage to one capsule at mealtime. After several more weeks, even one capsule per meal produces burning and Joan discontinues altogether. This is a sign that the stomach has been re-trained to produce adequate concentrations of hydrochloric acid without the further need for supplementation.

When consuming meals that contain little or no protein, much less hydrochloric acid is needed by the stomach to process its contents."

### janagram — 2012-04-12T09:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-getting-nasty-heartburn-at-night.756/post-14141>

thanks, owl. will implement.

# Thread: Zakalyatsa: Russian cold adaptation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zakalyatsa-russian-cold-adaptation.759/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-10T01:16:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zakalyatsa-russian-cold-adaptation.759/post-13164>

I was born in northern Russia. I immigrated to the US when I was 9.

It dawned on me a few days ago that there is a word in Russian, ZAKALYATSA. It basically means cold adapted.

I went to my parents house for Passover dinner on Saturday. there were a bunch of their friends, all Russian, ranging in ages form 55-75. I asked them about the meaning of the word and the the process.

the general consensus was this: zakatyatsa is done to improve the immune system and people who practice this never get sick in winter. basically it is people who tolerate the cold or are able to withstand the cold. they spoke about taking cold showers, splashing themselves with cold water in the morning, swimming in cold water. they said the epitome of this practice are people who swim in ice cold water in the winter. they are called "seals". In the US, we call this "the polar bear club".

Thuis has been practiced for hundreds if not thousands of years in Russia. it is ingrained in the language and culture. People who are "zakalyeniye" are very respected for their abilities.

Thought this was pretty interesting, and in line with what Dr K believes!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-10T01:31:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zakalyatsa-russian-cold-adaptation.759/post-13169>

Thanks; awesome! There's a great video of people doing this on YouTube; unfortunately it's all in Polish, so I LOVE your English explanation!

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-10T05:54:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zakalyatsa-russian-cold-adaptation.759/post-13249>

Awesome!! Thanks so much for sharing this story

### iliarski@yahoo.com — 2012-04-10T06:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zakalyatsa-russian-cold-adaptation.759/post-13259>

I'm also from Russia (Ukraine, to be exact...). ZAKALYAISYA means something like "expose yourself to cold to get stronger", similar to how you convert iron to steel - by putting hot iron into a cold water. Ever since reading about CT and every time I get in the cold shower/tub, I cannot help but start singing a Russian song from the Soviet times, I think, 1940s... Rough translation of this song from Russian to English sounds something like

"If you want to be healthy, ZAKALYAISYA, try to forget about the doctors, get under cold water. Sun, air, and water are the best remedies from all illnesses, zakalyaisya and get as strong as steel..." This song has a very uplifting melody - you can find it here

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JXk3gPD3s34&feature=related\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;JXk3gPD3s34\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JXk3gPD3s34&feature=related\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;JXk3gPD3s34)

[http://www.idpublications.com/journals/PDFs/JACI/JACI\_MostCited\_1.pdf](http://http://www.idpublications.com/journals/PDFs/JACI/JACI_MostCited_1.pdf)

I was trying to compare my raised skin from bad allergies to an earlier ct reaction. I was thinking the brown stuff was plumping up. The white stuff harbors IL-6. Talked about the need for a controlled burn.

"Other cytokines and chemokines

In addition to IL-6 and TNF-a, WAT expresses and

releases a variety of other cytokines and chemokines.

WAT-derived IL-1 receptor antagonist is markedly increased

in the serum of obese subjects, as is IL-18,

although its ultimate source has not been identified.57,58

WAT also produces chemokines, including IL-8, MCP-1,

and macrophage inflammatory protein 1.

CORRELATION BETWEEN ADIPOSITY

AND IMMUNITY-INFLAMMATION

Obesity, particularly visceral adiposity, is associated

with chronic low-grade inflammation, as indicated by

increased levels of the inflammatory markers CRP and

IL-6 in the circulation of obese subjects. Although not

completely demonstrated, the current working hypothesis

is that adipokines, cytokines, and other factors produced

and released by WAT are responsible for the chronic

inflammatory state of visceral obesity. The concomitance

of inflammation, hypertension, and dyslipidemia increases

the likelihood of development of type II diabetes and

CVD.59 However, the ultimate reason for increased production

of proinflammatory factors byWATin obesity has

not been identified. Furthermore, visceral adiposity, rather

than simply a high body mass index (BMI; in kilograms"

I wonder if cold is any good for inflammation?

# Thread: High CRP + Labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AmyJ — 2012-04-10T03:00:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13194>

Hi everyone,

I could really use some help with the inflammation!! 51yo female, total hysterectomy 2008, no hormones then. Hashimotos. Gluten intolerant/not Celiac. 5'3.5" 173lb. Started Leptin Rx mid December and lost 15lbs which is phenomenal for me, I've never been able to lose more than 3-4lbs and would always gain it back + more. Cholesterol was 300 a year ago, too, 50mcg of Synthroid knocked it down to 230 in 5 months but I started going hyper. Dropped to 25mcg and up the cholesterol went again. At the time of the tests below I started on 60mg Armour and .5MG/.25MG E2/E3 and 50MG Progesterone, both sublingual. The original prescription was double but it made me very depressed/moody. I eat mostly keto, GF beef, shellfish, pastured pork, sometimes chicken. Not fond of veggies, no fruit, only dairy is GF ghee and occasional hard cheese. Once in a while a nightshade will slip in, as will a grain. I was traveling when that happened, just before the tests. Haven't had them in a while again. I've been spot CTing because it feels so good. I've been waking up between 2-3am with what feels like a surge of adrenalin, otherwise sleep ok. A 24 hr cortisol test will be coming up.

What is the most effective and quickest way to reduce the high CRP? How long will it take to get it down and are there other tests I should have? I'm not sure what's causing it but it's probably stress. I live in an area that was devastated by a wildfire last Labor Day weekend. I'm working on the mental aspects of the situation but I'm not quite where I should be.

VAP

Total LDL-D Direct 212 (=40 md/dL)

Total VLDL-C Direct 21 (

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-10T03:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13200>

Your ratio of rT3 is too low (should be >20). This means some of your T4 that should be converting to T3 is actually getting converted to rT3. The FT3 should be in the top part of the range and yours is in the bottom part. The FT4 should be middle to highish in the range and it's in the bottom. All this tells you that

1) you have some underlying issue that is causing your rT3 and leaving you leptin resistant in the brain (might want to check your cortisol ...wake up times for cortisol surges are usually between 2-4am and get a full iron panel as your ferritin is super low.

2) you are probably not on enough thyroid meds. 60 mg is a very low dose in the scheme of Hashis. You need CT for sure!

The HA1c of 5.8 puts you at the pre-diabetic stage. CT will help this tremendously according to Dr. K. This number is the average of 90 days of your insulin so one time is not going to affect this. Something that you are eating is causing an insulin response. It may be the lactose in the cheese???? Maybe re-examine all the foods you eat from this perspective.

I'm not sure but I think both the Hashis and pre-diabetes can trigger a high CRP. Someone correct me if this is not right. Sorry can't help with hormones or cholesterol.

### shilohman — 2012-04-10T03:39:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13202>

AmyJ, you also need your omega 6/3 ratio tested. With your LDL at 212, this indicates as you suspected a lot of inflammation. Until you get your omega 6/3 ratio tested, try supplementing with krill oil. But you are on the right track. Be careful of meat that is labeled grass fed, but is fattened up before going to market with grains. I know this mostly is what Whole Foods sells, so you might be getting a lot of

omega 6/ low omega 3s with out knowing it. Just something to verify.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T03:58:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13205>

Shilohman, I hadn't heard this about Whole Food's GF beef. Disappointing.

Well, I didn't like the fact that they only sell lean GF beef..

### AmyJ — 2012-04-10T04:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13208>

Patty Cakes: I’m due to have my next thyroid labs done. These will reflect the Armour. I didn’t realize the ferritin is low. I switched to all cast iron and started eating lots of beef after that one, does that help with that? That test was in early December.

I’m confused by the A1c. I was checking my blood sugar when I first started the LR but then quit after a few weeks because it was always so low. I’m not sure I trust that test. At any rate, I’ve quite eating cheese again.

Shiloman: the 6/3 ratio is on my wish list. I got the bill from Quest today. $209. The insurance paid 14.00. Whoopee!! I’m hoping part of that was deductible. Gosh, is it really 262.00 for an RT3? Crazy.

I get my beef from Slankers or a local cattle company. Grass finished. Thanks for the reminder. I'll look at everything more closely.

Thank you both!

From what I understand the hsCRP is a tool that can identify potential cardiac disease - due to the levels of CRP protein in your blood. It isn't a true measure of inflammation, its just 1 piece of the puzzle. my CIMT showed inflammation but no plaque. inflammation can be a sign of rapid aging of the body - ie shortening telemores. Inflammation can be the result of what your eating, it can be influenced by environmental, and what epigenetic switches have been turned on.

Amy I've had great success just doing the LR from June to January - by then I had seen a reversal of leptin resistance - toward Leptin Sensitivity. In Feb I began CTing...and In March I added BHRT - btwn the 13 of March and this past monday I 've seen a drop in at least 7lbs & I'm mostly migraine free (not counting my stupidity from last night- causing one) I'd give the LR and CT a chance. if your doc's push statins and such - push back. Ask them to show you the journal articles where Statins are BENEFICIAL to WOMEN. (They can't show you any b/c there aren't any) Continue to push on diet/exercise and follow the LR - and discuss closer monitoring, Tell them You know that diet/exercise/weightloss will turn things around but its not something that's going to happen over night... tell them you're shooting for small slow weightloss - that in the end will result in HUGE POSITIVE GAINS in health. Figure how every long you been at weight X - it will take at least that long to get to a normal weight. Remember your doctor works for you, the recommend a treatment - but they CAN'T make you follow said treatment. IF you don't do what they recommend - what's the worst that can happen - they "Fire"

you as a patient - if so good - find a better doc, willing to work w/you. The best outcome - they work w/you , and you change their perspective on how they interact w/patients, and how they treat patients... and you become the starfish....

### mambo — 2012-04-29T21:22:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-19900>

The source of your IR is post-prandial, as is typical among women. Your BG stays elevated too long. My experience among ketogenic or VLC dieters who experience declining BG control is that their diet is way too acidic. If you're just eating meats and very little vegetables, your body's become way too acidic and this affects the functionality of your pancreatic beta cells. Combine this with physiological insulin resistance and you get prediabetes.

Add some veggies to your diet. You don't have to add safe starches but add broccoli, cauliflower, kale, and collard greens. Steam them if you can. Drink the juice after steaming them.

Your issue with inflammation is that you haven't optimized your thyroid function yet and there could be other sources causing inflammation: i.e., autoimmunity. Omega 6-3 imbalance is a red herring: it turns around slowly but should not affect your CRP much if you're already low-carbing. You have to look elsewhere and it could be your Hashimoto's, which overlaps with myriad other autoimmune conditions. That would be the avenue to look, not focusing on grass-fed/grass-finished beef. Ask for Anti Nuclear Antibodies.

> **Patty Cakes;13288 wrote:** My HA1c number just came back as 5.8 even though I haven been VLC for over 6 months.

### Jim — 2012-04-10T03:55:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photographic-food-journal.763/post-13204>

I haven't tried this but Robb Wolf did a podcast recently about a photographic food journal that allows you, your friends, or your physician to literally see what youâ€™ve been eating. It's called PhotoCalorie. It looks like a great service. It will even estimate the nutrition in your food based on the picture.

Is anyone using it? If so what do you think?

Here is the link to Robb's podcast.

[http://robbwolf.com/2012/02/21/larry-istrail-ancestral-weightloss-registry-photo-calorie-paleo-solution-episode-120/](http://http://robbwolf.com/2012/02/21/larry-istrail-ancestral-weightloss-registry-photo-calorie-paleo-solution-episode-120/)

Here is the link to PhotoCalorie.

[http://www.photocalorie.com/](http://http://www.photocalorie.com/)

### Glamorama — 2012-04-10T04:02:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photographic-food-journal.763/post-13207>

Its a great idea, but for me, it would trigger obsessive behaviour (I had an eating disorder many years ago). I take great joy in doing photos of my yummy meals... but to do so in order to keep track of my calories etc would just kill the fun for me. It would be a wonderful tool for people who want and need to track their meals though!

### Jim — 2012-04-10T04:18:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photographic-food-journal.763/post-13217>

I know what you mean. I used to do FitDay and record everything but have pretty much stopped. This is pretty neat for folks interested in an easy way to keep track.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-10T04:23:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photographic-food-journal.763/post-13220>

> **Jim;13286 wrote:** I know what you mean. I used to do FitDay and record everything but have pretty much stopped. This is pretty neat for folks interested in an easy way to keep track.

I used FitDay too but it really sparked my ED and I felt like I was trapped, once that happend I stopped. But I learnt quickly from it, I can now roughly estimate the ins and outs of all my meals- when I bother.

# Thread: Canadians: where to get these labs done
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadians-where-to-get-these-labs-done.768/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Endless — 2012-04-10T09:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadians-where-to-get-these-labs-done.768/post-13279>

I can get basic bloodwork done at my doctor's office and it's covered under medical insurance provided I have symptoms or eligible risk factors....but nothing as extensive as I'm seeing here!

Where do I go to get this done, and how the heck do people afford to keep getting it done so often??

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-04-10T15:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadians-where-to-get-these-labs-done.768/post-13315>

> **Endless;13348 wrote:** I can get basic bloodwork done at my doctor's office and it's covered under medical insurance provided I have symptoms or eligible risk factors....but nothing as extensive as I'm seeing here!
>
> Where do I go to get this done, and how the heck do people afford to keep getting it done so often??

you find a doctor willing to order labs with the right marker so you don\`t have to pay (ie for thyroid s/he has to write fatigue as a reason) or you go to a naturopath and pay out of pocket and hope your extended covers the cost

however you should know that some of the labs just aren\`t drawn up here, RT3 for example is no longer available as a serum test the only way I\`ve seen to get it done is to go through a naturopath and have it ordered as a larger blood draw that is about $300

# Thread: Krill vs Fish oil

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-04-10T11:09:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13286>

I'm almost out of omega-3 and thought I'd look into getting a new supply.

However; it seems that krill oil have a much lower amount of DHA/EPA than fish oil for the same amount of O3. The additional benefits from krill seems to make it much better than fish oil overall, but do I need to increase my total daily O3 intake to correct for less DHA/EPA with krill?

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-11T21:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13870>

good question. Krill oil is also much more expensive.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-11T23:12:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13890>

Krill has PC and PS and Astaxanthin in it which are all critical for myelin, nerve, and brain function. Those eating a paleo diet do not need a ton of Fish oil......Krill is the better choice and the one I use for CT in the clinic. I take it myself and I use it more than any other supplement in neurosurgery.

### freshveggies — 2012-04-11T23:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13898>

read this article on the over-consumption of fish oil [http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBio/docs/BodyBioBulletin-TheInsideStoryofOmega3FattyAcids.pdf](http://http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBio/docs/BodyBioBulletin-TheInsideStoryofOmega3FattyAcids.pdf)

### ealachan — 2012-04-11T23:26:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13902>

> **freshveggies;13967 wrote:** read this article on the over-consumption of fish oil [http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBio/docs/BodyBioBulletin-TheInsideStoryofOmega3FattyAcids.pdf](http://http://www.bodybio.com/BodyBio/docs/BodyBioBulletin-TheInsideStoryofOmega3FattyAcids.pdf)

Not to nit-pick, but that's less an article and more a sales pitch for the supplement listed at the end of it.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-11T23:27:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13903>

I like the fact that I don't have to take as much krill oil, as I would of the fish oil. Also, krill oil doesn't make me burp, so I think I am absorbing it better. If I ever get a headache again, however, I'd like to try taking fish oil, to see if it zaps headaches like krill oil does.

### freshveggies — 2012-04-11T23:28:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/krill-vs-fish-oil.770/post-13906>

Through the last 2 pages out. There is good info in that aritcle.

# Thread: Free the Animal does CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 141
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### agatha — 2012-04-10T13:14:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13292>

Richard Nikoley at Free the Animal has just set up a tub for CT - take a look!

### mistigi — 2012-04-10T16:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13328>

[http://freetheanimal.com/2012/04/cold-water-therapy-and-experimentation-recommences.html](http://http://freetheanimal.com/2012/04/cold-water-therapy-and-experimentation-recommences.html)

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-10T17:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13339>

I set the same thing up on my patio last week. Post a pic in the pictures thread. I have a 100 gallon tank but live in florida so keeping the water cold is becoming a problem. I have a tent over top of it to keep direct sun off of it but before I add ice the the water temp 75 degrees after 20 pounds of ice it still only drops to around 73 degrees. I thinking of a way to insulate it so it can hold its cold temp but still allow me to move and clean it. The fit is just right, I can sit in it with the water up to my neck just by bending my knees and can slide under the water by rolling on to my back.

I got my tank for 60 dollars at a local feed store

[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/13408=114-cold tub.jpg>)

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-10T17:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13342>

My husband and I were thinking of getting something similar when our pool heats up and we can no longer use it. It would certainly be easier to cool off than our huge pool. Do you keep the water in the tub and just add ice? If so, how do you keep the water clean? Could you just throw some chlorine in?

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-10T17:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13349>

> **Trisha;13411 wrote:** My husband and I were thinking of getting something similar when our pool heats up and we can no longer use it. It would certainly be easier to cool off than our huge pool. Do you keep the water in the tub and just add ice? If so, how do you keep the water clean? Could you just throw some chlorine in?

So far I have no changed the water (its only been 5 days) I keep a tarp tightly over top of it when not in use to keep bugs and dirt out. so far so good. I do plan on changing the water every 1 -2 weeks depending on how dirty it gets from my natural body oils. Since I plan on changing it out I passed on the chlorine.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-10T17:39:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13352>

I set one a 75 gallon stock tank too, got it at the local feed store for $65. I guess I'm lucky? temps here are still below 60 and the water out of the hose is 48 or less. If you have a big freezer, try freezing 5 gallon buckets of water?

@Trisha, I empty it every Saturday morning, scrub it out, and fill it back up. I also add a splash of chlorine and a dash of epsom salt, not sure why... doing that on instinct. If you aren't scrubbing & refilling it regularly, I would think you would need to do something otherwise it will get icky and slimey, even more so if you live in a warm place.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-10T17:42:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13354>

quick, someone register freezetheanimal.com before this catches on.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-10T17:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13356>

Good to hear what you all are doing. Our pool has been hanging between 48-52 degrees the past 4 weeks we've had it open, so we don't need to have a new set up quite yet!

### Adriana — 2012-04-10T17:53:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13362>

> **Continuum Fitness;13408 wrote:** I set the same thing up on my patio last week. .. I thinking of a way to insulate it so it can hold its cold temp but still allow me to move and clean it. The fit is just right, I can sit in it with the water up to my neck just by bending my knees and can slide under the water by rolling on to my back.
>
> I got my tank for 60 dollars at a local feed store
>
>
>
>
> [](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/13408=114-cold tub.jpg>)

The best option is to sink the tank in the ground. I had a hydroponic greenhouse operation in Florida and had to insulate my above ground tanks. You can buy reflective thermal bubble wrap insulation at Home Depot. It is used for hot water heaters. Wrap the sides and make a cover of Corning Pink or Dow Blue foam board, also from Home Depot.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-10T18:15:12+0300

I've looked at the fish tank chillers - but they are a bit pricey, and I'm not sure they will hold up outdoors. My goal is to not put too many obstacles between me and CT. . I need to be able to walk out and hop in!

### Cron23 — 2012-04-10T18:52:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13384>

I have a full bath with a tub in the basement of my house. I drain it once or twice a week and go in clean. The water generally stays around 55 and I throw in a bucket of ice paks which brings it lower. It's my ice room w/all the indoor luxuries. So if you have an extra bathroom in your house you can dedicate it to ice bath!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-10T18:58:23+0300

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-11T16:59:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13730>

My biggest problem with KH, Melissa, Evelyn CarbInsane, et al is not that they (vehemently) disagree with Jack Kruse, or that they don't care for or understand his writing style. It's that they don't care for the real people behind these screens. They called us stupid, acolytes, and many other insulting things... but they don't offer any help. Oh sure, if you are already fit and Paleo, they think you are great... but if you are trying to make your way back to health, then you suck and you're a fat idiot...at least that has been my impression of their sentiments. They probably don't really feel that way, but that is what they are projecting, and even more so when their brain goes into Kruse overload...at that point they're all venom and spittle, failing to address the actual points: does it work? if so, why?

totally agree and why I like Richard. He is not everyone's cup of tea but neither am I.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-11T18:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13778>

Come to think of it, Richard Nikoley actually introduced me to a modified form of CT last year. From his post about not using soap, I applied the concept to use while camping. It is so much easier to take sponge baths when you don't have to try to rinse off the soap. I came to really like taking the cool sponge baths, and even the skin on my upper back got used to the cool washcloth. I began to use the spongebaths at home too, several times a week, though I still continued with the shampoo and some use of soap. The sponge baths are so quick! They also helped cool me off in Summer.

I'm guessing my getting used to the cold washcloths helped me a bit when I started CT this year.

[http://freetheanimal.com/2009/12/paleo-i-dont-care-i-like-no-soap-no-shampoo.html](http://http://freetheanimal.com/2009/12/paleo-i-dont-care-i-like-no-soap-no-shampoo.html)

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-11T18:34:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13786>

Ha ha. After reading his post I decided to try it too. I haven't used soap on my body or anything on my hair for a year and a half. A nice rinse eliminates unpleasantness from the days' activities and my wife cannot tell any difference. So I have now nixed the mismatch of our cultural obsession that destroys our natural skin flora.

Noticed many improvements all around with whole fam after dropping beauty products. I still wear a little makeup, but it's a work necessity.

### Guest — 2012-04-11T23:03:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13883>

> **Jack Kruse;13945 wrote:** A psychiatrist talks to people and does not lay hands on patients.......so what does she really know but what she reads. Moreover, while she is paleo she does not apply paleo to her practice. I asked her about this on her blog and several others asked her this at Paleo fx and she confirmed it to them. I was shocked at hearing this in Austin.
>
>
>
> Let us look at doctors who treat patients, and what they say: [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2011/11/14/deep-chilling-trauma-patients\_n\_1093546.html?ref=healthy-living-health-news](http://http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2011/11/14/deep-chilling-trauma-patients_n_1093546.html?ref=healthy-living-health-news)

So because psychiatrists do not "lay hands" on their patients, what they have to say is irrelevant? I'm not sure I'm following. They, like all other MDs, have been to medical school and completed residency. They, like MDs in other specialties, conduct clinical interviews, order blood work, and prescribe medication. I've heard Dr. Deans speak and, per her report, she applies paleo to her practice to the degree that the data support it. So that may mean taking a vitamin D level on a patient and encouraging supplementation, or educating a patient on the importance of good sleep. But the fact of the matter is, there is not much good data beyond anecdotal reports to indicate that a paleo lifestyle results in better mental health outcomes. And that HuffPost article is on the use of hypothermia in patients who are critically injured, so I'm not sure how directly relevant it is to patients on this forum, unless, of course, they're currently suffering from gunshot or stab wounds.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-11T23:04:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-13885>

I make my own pure high end vanilla. Sooooo awesome. We go through several liters a year. Seriously. It's vodka and vanilla beans steeped for at least 6 months. Awesome gift item.

For deodorant

Equal parts coconut oil and baking soda. Add enough macadamia nut oil to give it a spreadable consistency. Add vanilla to your liking. Shake a lot as it cools to keep it mixed.

I don't think the vanilla is more than just an aromatic, but perhaps the vodka has some antibacterial property?

Just made the first batch after hearing about it here I think - and in a month has become a friends fav for both men and women.

### Birdy — 2012-04-12T05:08:14+0300

I find it odd that someone that is so concerned about her reputation that she would unblogroll someone who merely associates with Dr. Mercola, would then continue to make fun of pro-Kruse participants in other places with her posts and tweets. And then you have Kamal shuttling back and forth to post parodies on PH so they can all twitter about it together? Melissa has abused her priveledge on PH. The attacks have never been even on PH, because Melissa and other so-called moderators remove posts that disagree with her, and they all pile on the Kruse posts, and pile on everyone there who posts positively about Kruse. Dick's post brought out all the ugliness, as expected. All the usuals were there. ### Guest — 2012-04-12T05:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14077>

> **Jack Kruse;13957 wrote:** not at all.....it means her experience is very limited to non hands on patient care. That is the point. What I am doing is completely hands on in surgery and in my clinic. And the Huff post can work on those critically injured and dying what might that mean for others that are less injured or ill.....???? Might it be beneficial? Only a clinician who lays hands on people would know. There in lies the real point you are not getting.

There seems to be an interesting pattern emerging in the paleosphere over the last few weeks. It goes something like this . . .

1) Select a scientific paper or news article that contains the key words "cold" or "ice".

2) Insert a huge inferential leap.

3) Conclude that said paper/article supports the theory that cold thermogenesis results in weight loss and longevity, treats myriad conditions (diabetes, Hashimoto's, multiple sclerosis, anxiety, depression, autism, etc.), reverses paralysis, and revives dead babies.

A related trend . . .

1) Notice a study that seems to contradict one's previously held belief that CT is a cure-all.

2) Dismiss the results of said study by claiming that it was conducted on non-cold-adapted organisms (despite the fact that there is no widely accepted measure of cold-adaptedness).

I'm not saying that CT is without merit. But does it do all the things it's purported to on this forum? I highly doubt it. I was particularly intrigued/galled by the suggestion that CT might be beneficial for Asperger's. Based on what? Unfortunately, advocates of CT risk losing all credibility when they start making claims that are not based on relevant data and/or theory.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-12T05:25:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14084>

> Kurt Harris, Melissa, Evelyn, et al. think people who are trying to regain their health suck and are fat idiots? Yes, that is definitely your impression of their sentiments, given that that is not even close to what they've said. Melissa and Evelyn, in particular, have written extensively about their own health struggles. Venomous statements have passed back and forth between both sides of this debate. I think everyone wants to know what interventions work and want to help. They all just have very different styles, methods, and standards of proof.

Yes, it is _my_ impression... is that not what I said? I'm not the only one getting that impression, and if that's the case, then maybe their message is getting scrambled in the noise? The noise being the cheerleading/attack squad (mostly passive aggressive and sometimes very funny!) that seems to show up the minute someone references some positive result from following a Kruse recommendation, or if \*heaven forbid\* one has a question about following a Kruse recommendation.

I think it's great too that they are rigorous in their scientific pursuit, and desire very high standards of proof. Proof, in the scientific sense, implies significant results have been through enough peer reviewed studies that it becomes CW.... which sometimes will prove to be flawed after more discoveries are made. We are still evolving, and discovering... as for me, I'm not one to wait on fixing my health until a committee approves my plan or my reasoning behind it.

### Guest — 2012-04-12T05:28:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14085>

> **Birdy;14149 wrote:** Apow, you make interesting points. There are plenty of sweeping statements in the paleosphere. Not much is backed up with randomized clinical trials. But, why is Jack's stuff, and his supporters, singled out for the most vicious ridicule?

Birdy,

The lack of RCTs isn't all that bothersome to me. There are lots of reasonable objections to using RCTs as the "gold standard" of proof. What bothers me about the the emergence of CT as the paleosphere interest du jour is that Dr. Kruse and others seem to be making inferences that go soooooo far beyond the available evidence, and that are sometimes only tangentially related to the evidence that is cited. I haven't been paying attention to the paleosphere for all that long, but I've certainly never seen anything like it.

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-12T05:56:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14088>

> I was particularly intrigued/galled by the suggestion that CT might be beneficial for Asperger's.

I'm not sure where you read that, and I readily admit I do not read every thread or post on this forum. I did join in on a thread where Aspbergers came up, and in that case the conversation was how a ketogenic diet could be beneficial.

Every time a new "concept" is thrown out, it does not lead to blind acceptance. It leads to further research and sharing of knowledge. Likely leads to self experiments, tracking, trial & error, comparing progress... taking matters into our own hands. CT is a new concept for many, but there is evidence it is beneficial for weight loss and pain control. There are dangers involved for some people, and for some who jump into it too quickly, too cold for too long, or freezing the skin - all of which Dr. Kruse warned against and laid out specifics for safety. As time goes by, and more people try it and share effect, the results should speak for themselves. These may not be rigorous scientific studies, but if the population is big enough, anything significant will become obvious... and we don't have to wait for the grant funds before we begin!

### Glamazon — 2012-04-12T08:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14131>

> **Jack Kruse;13945 wrote:** A psychiatrist/radiologist talks to people/reads films on a computer and does not lay hands on patients.......so what does she really know but what she reads. Moreover, while she is paleo she does not apply paleo to her practice. I asked her about this on her blog and several others asked her this at Paleo fx and she confirmed it to them. I was shocked at hearing this in Austin.
>
>
>
> Let us look at doctors who treat patients, and what they say: [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2011/11/14/deep-chilling-trauma-patients\_n\_1093546.html?ref=healthy-living-health-news](http://http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2011/11/14/deep-chilling-trauma-patients_n_1093546.html?ref=healthy-living-health-news)

One of the things I do is behavioral health case management and utilization review as a nurse. The model of psychiatric patient management is different than it was years ago. In my state, psychiatrists do medication management, titration, no real talking therapy. They adjust and prescribe meds. It is the psychologists and other therapists who have more hands on patient care. It is frustrating to consumers to have one provider to write the meds and another provider does the therapy.

There are regional and state differences in how things are done in health care. There are practice models to follow but docs can modify how they practice.

When I first started reading Dr. K's blog I had to chuckle when he talked about contemporary medicine's slavishness to evidence based practice. He pointed out that if your evidence is skewed or in error so is your practice no matter how excellent the study protocols. His pointing out that ALL medical evidence is based on warm adapted humans shatters a sacred paradigm. That is just as important as when it was finally understood we can't use data gathered from trials on men to treat women. But, for a very long time that was standard practice. There was a time not too long ago because I remember when the studies starting coming out when thinking differently that men and women should be treated differently was considered a radical and stupid idea.

Health care practice is as dogmatic as religion. Heck, it is a religion. People can push their shiny evidence based data out there but it is inherently flawed because it was done on warm adapted humans. ALL medical management/health care is based on warm adapted human trials. It's FLAWED.

That is what makes Dr. K's theories so important. Does he have them wrapped up in a pretty box with a satin ribbon? No. There really is no such thing as a finished project here. So much more research needs to be done on cold adapted humans. This really is a beginning and to shoot the horse before he gets more than a few steps out of the gate is tragic and a waste.

I still haven't seen any evidence that Dr. Deans denigrates people who follow Dr. Kruse . . .

### Guest — 2012-04-12T14:30:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14177>

> **LisaAPB;14157 wrote:** I'm not sure where you read that, and I readily admit I do not read every thread or post on this forum. I did join in on a thread where Aspbergers came up, and in that case the conversation was how a ketogenic diet could be beneficial.
>
>
>
> Every time a new "concept" is thrown out, it does not lead to blind acceptance. It leads to further research and sharing of knowledge. Likely leads to self experiments, tracking, trial & error, comparing progress... taking matters into our own hands. CT is a new concept for many, but there is evidence it is beneficial for weight loss and pain control. There are dangers involved for some people, and for some who jump into it too quickly, too cold for too long, or freezing the skin - all of which Dr. Kruse warned against and laid out specifics for safety. As time goes by, and more people try it and share effect, the results should speak for themselves. These may not be rigorous scientific studies, but if the population is big enough, anything significant will become obvious... and we don't have to wait for the grant funds before we begin!

Asperger's is mentioned in a thread entitled "CT and Aspberger's (one of the autism spectrums)". A mother asks if keto + CT would be beneficial to her adult son with Asperger's and Dr. Kruse replies, "Yes it would be." There is some _limited_ evidence that a keto diet can be beneficial for autism, so sure, try it. But CT? Why? Based on what theory? I was appalled by the confidence of Dr. Kruse's response to this woman. So many hucksters have infiltrated the field of autism over the years and and promised snake oil cures to desperate parents. The last thing the paleo world needs is its leaders becoming wildly overstating the benefits of certain practices.

If all the users of CT were conducting well-controlled, carefully documented N=1 experiments on themselves (which, frankly, is really hard to do), I would be more confident that any clinically relevant results might trickle up to the medical establishment. I'm not convinced this is what is happening though and the the physicians I know are not, for better or for worse, going to find a bunch of anecdotes on an online forum compelling evidence to recommend CT.

That is just as important as when it was finally understood we can't use data gathered from trials on men to treat women. But, for a very long time that was standard practice. There was a time not too long ago because I remember when the studies starting coming out when thinking differently that men and women should be treated differently was considered a radical and stupid idea.

>
> Health care practice is as dogmatic as religion. Heck, it is a religion. People can push their shiny evidence based data out there but it is inherently flawed because it was done on warm adapted humans. ALL medical management/health care is based on warm adapted human trials. It's FLAWED.
>
>
>
> That is what makes Dr. K's theories so important. Does he have them wrapped up in a pretty box with a satin ribbon? No. There really is no such thing as a finished project here. ### Glamazon — 2012-04-12T17:06:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14225>

When I first got out of nursing school in the late 80s, I used to work with a combat surgeon who served in Vietnam. Combat surgeons are very interesting people, fearless. They work in the most awful of situations with sometimes less than optimal surroundings. They will try almost anything to save a life convention be damned.

I had a patient code and we worked on him for quite awhile. In retrospect I think the patient threw a clot which is why he arrested. It is difficult to know for sure at the time.

The combat surgeon recommended we try a drug. It wasn't in the Advanced Cardiac Life Support Protocols so there were questioning looks all around the bed. We had exhausted all the protocols so far. He looked at us and said, "Can't hurt, might help, always a crowd pleaser."

It didn't work but we did our best to save this relatively young man. He died.

I recently had a young man die from a broken leg while waiting to have it fixed in the OR. He may have thrown a fat emboli but nothing was working to save him. Desperate situations call for desperate measures. The surgeons cut his chest open to massage his heart. He still died.

It makes me wonder how CT might have affected these tragic outcomes. It would be an interesting thought experiment if a hypothermia blanket with cold protocols was attached to every crash cart.

There are a lot of desperate stories like this. Every health care provider has them.

I think health care is in a desperate place. More people are dying and getting chronically ill at a younger and younger age. It is a time for desperate or extreme measures. The ironic thing is CT is only extreme to neolithic thinking. What we believed to have worked before is only making things worse yet CM is attached to these processes even when the outcomes are horrible. We even see patients being blamed because the outcome does not match the evidence based results.

CT is relatively safe when done with guidelines. The risk to benefit ratio is so very low. The financial cost is so low compared with the long term costs of treating people with chronic co morbidities. We could discuss for hours on emotional and social costs of warm adapted medicine on patients and how that would change when cold adapted medicine becomes available and common. It would be interesting to do cost analysis on Dr. K's patients. Just the money alone saved is significant. He's right in that he no longer has to do a walletectomy on people who walk through his door. Insurance companies should be banging on his door and funneling money into his research. This process is a dream come true for a Utilization and Resource Management nurse like me. Yes, those are the people who make sure that health care dollars are not spent on wasteful practices and are of the most benefit to the patient. In my day job I can deny re-imbursement to hospitals with wasteful and unsafe practices. I work for Kaiser Permanente. This stuff is all so real to me.

My mind is just blown apart and expanding with the implications. This, folks, is a game changer. I know this is not something that is going to be relegated as an "outlier" for people.

I wish more people would take the 30,000 ft view on this rather than the 3 inches view. It would certainly create a more harmonious environment to practice and do research together.

### Guest — 2012-04-12T17:10:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-the-animal-does-ct.771/post-14229>

> **Glamazon;14200 wrote:** One of the things I do is behavioral health case management and utilization review as a nurse. The model of psychiatric patient management is different than it was years ago. In my state, psychiatrists do medication management, titration, no real talking therapy. They adjust and prescribe meds. It is the psychologists and other therapists who have more hands on patient care. It is frustrating to consumers to have one provider to write the meds and another provider does the therapy.

>

He pointed out that if your evidence is skewed or in error so is your practice no matter how excellent the study protocols. His pointing out that ALL medical evidence is based on warm adapted humans shatters a sacred paradigm. That is just as important as when it was finally understood we can't use data gathered from trials on men to treat women. But, for a very long time that was standard practice. There was a time not too long ago because I remember when the studies starting coming out when thinking differently that men and women should be treated differently was considered a radical and stupid idea.
>
>
>
> Health care practice is as dogmatic as religion. Heck, it is a religion. People can push their shiny evidence based data out there but it is inherently flawed because it was done on warm adapted humans. ALL medical management/health care is based on warm adapted human trials. It's FLAWED.
>
>
>
> That is what makes Dr. K's theories so important. Does he have them wrapped up in a pretty box with a satin ribbon? No. There really is no such thing as a finished project here. So much more research needs to be done on cold adapted humans. This really is a beginning and to shoot the horse before he gets more than a few steps out of the gate is tragic and a waste.

Hi Glamazon,

I also work in behavioral health and it sounds like our states have a very similar division of professional labor (psychiatrists prescribe, psychologists/social workers/other clinicians do the therapy).

I'm perfectly willing to admit that there are TONS of problems with much of the behavioral health research literature. Where do I even start? Big pharma funds some of the drug studies and withholds information about the terrible side effects associated with certain medications (Zyprexa comes to mind). Many trials have such small Ns that they are underpowered to detect hypothesized between-group differences (and are woefully underpowered to allow researchers to closely look at characteristics differentiating responders from nonresponders). There's the whole "statistical vs. clinical significance" issue that is also relevant to other fields. All of this is to say that I'm not blind to flaws of our current evidence base.

But do I think we have to discard all the research we've done so far because it's been done on "warm-adapted humans"? Absolutely not. First of all, we have no way of distinguishing warm-adapted from cold-adapted humans (i.e., there's no standard way to measure these constructs). Second, as far as I can tell, we have no reason to believe that cold-adaptedness would influence a patient's response to existing treatments (pharmacological or behavioral). And we have even less evidence to believe that cold exposure (CT) would ameliorate mental health outcomes. Although CT does open up some new research ideas, it's premature to conclude that it invalidates "ALL medical management/health care" research.

They get visited by people selling very expensive drugs that destroy your body. Oxytocin is deficient in autism. I can't think of any metabolic damage that would come from swimming in cold water. Exercise and new experiences help. I can source this if you doubt it. And as far as eating, there is oxytocin with that. Talk about bad relationships. If cold water gives a new oxytocin source and good health, is that bad? (It's free) A clean liver could really screw things up.
