# Thread: Home remedies for warts,skin tags, and pigment problems
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-remedies-for-warts-skin-tags-and-pigment-problems.816/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 34
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-12T00:31:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-remedies-for-warts-skin-tags-and-pigment-problems.816/post-13946>

I'm trying to get a handle on this pigmentation, now that I understand how important it is for our skin to generate anti-inflammatories.

Example, my kid won some eyelash grow serum and the active ingredient in all of them with the good reviews seems to be castor oil. Anyway, it really works. And I've read that thyroid folks can rub it on their eyebrows and they grow back. Supposedly, castor oil evens out skin tones. The ct is helping, but more is more. I know walnut will take warts off, and I think the pigment is the poison. I used powdered buttermilk for tan fading to make my winter makeup work in the summer, the lactic acid is supposed to bleach it and stop the melanin. It lightened the skin right up.

The dark spots around a bug bite or old zits is supposed to be where the melanin presented itself as an anti-inflammatory. I'm guessing its just like scar tissue, you only need it for a bit, but it lingers.

[http://www.annualreviews.org/doi/full/10.1146/annurev.phyto.37.1.447](http://http://www.annualreviews.org/doi/full/10.1146/annurev.phyto.37.1.447) I am wondering if that pigment gene that turns off eating is poison. That might be why some people are allergic to their spouses.

Fructose Malabsorption Symptoms, Diet, Food To Avoid

Patient suffering from this disorder is subjected to rapid bacterial fermentation, high osmotic load, poor skin, nail and hair. When not considered at earlier stages, it may even lead to osteoporosis and anemia. Disease alters bacteria profile, promotes mucosalbiofilm, **decrease folic acid amount, tryptophan and zinc concentration in blood. Occurrence of severe stomach ache followed by constipation or diarrhea is another problem suffered by fructose malabsorption patients. Disease creates many ill health like vomiting tendency and bloating in patients. Patients even show signs of mental depression.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-28T01:23:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-remedies-for-warts-skin-tags-and-pigment-problems.816/post-19467>

In fact, the gut produces about 95% of the body's serotonin.15 So consider recommending probiotics for a depressed patient before a drug such as fluoxetine, since probiotics can increase serotonin and bridge gut-brain communication.

[http://www.clinchem.org/content/47/4/745.full.pdf](http://http://www.clinchem.org/content/47/4/745.full.pdf)

Overproduction of cytokines may be associated with

decreased blood zinc concentrations (17 ) because of increased

renal loss of zinc (18 ). We previously have shown

that fructose malabsorption is a promoting factor for

small intestinal bacterial overgrowth syndrome and is

associated with higher serum neopterin concentrations

(19 ), an indicator of immune activation. The different

bacterial composition of the gut could thus be a promoting

factor for zinc deficiency in fructose malabsorbers by

chronic immune stimulation. It has been shown that

dietary carbohydrates, such as sucrose and fructose, reduce

the bioavailability of copper by 30% in humans, but

not in rats (20 ). Because zinc is handled very much like

copper and high intestinal fructose concentrations are

seen in fructose malabsorption, the same phenomenon

could play a role in low zinc status in fructose malabsorption.

In summary, fructose malabsorption is associated with

lower serum zinc concentrations; in this study, 10 of 147

subjects (6.8%) had zinc deficiency, and all 10 suffered

from fructose malabsorption. Although this is probably

not attributable to a shortened transit time of a defective

DMT-1-transporter, the exact mechanism still remains to

be elucidated. Fructose malabsorption may thus reflect

only part of a more complex malabsorption syndrome.

Because fructose malabsorption can be seen in approximately

one-third of the Western European population,

fructose malabsorption could be a major etiology of low

zinc status.

### chocolate — 2012-04-30T05:59:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-remedies-for-warts-skin-tags-and-pigment-problems.816/post-20004>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3060432/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3060432/)

Protective Effect of Inositol Hexaphosphate Against UVB Damage in HaCaT Cells and Skin Carcinogenesis in SKH1 Hairless Mice

UVB radiation damages keratinocytes, potentially inducing chronic skin damage, cutaneous malignancy, and suppression of the immune system. Naturally occurring agents have been considered for prevention and treatment of various kinds of cancer, including skin cancer. Inositol hexaphosphate (IP6), an antioxidant, is a naturally occurring polyphosphorylated carbohydrate that has shown a strong anticancer activity in several experimental models. We assessed the protective effects of IP6 against UVB irradiation-induced injury and photocarcinogenesis by using HaCaT cells (human immortalized keratinocytes) and SKH1 hairless mice. We found that IP6 counteracts the harmful effects of UVB irradiation and increases the viability and survival of UVB-exposed cells. Treatment with IP6 after UVB irradiation (30 mJ/cm2) arrested cells in the G1 and G2M phases while decreasing the S phase of the cell cycle. Treatment with IP6 also decreased UVB-induced apoptosis and caspase 3 activation. **Topical application of IP6 followed by exposure to UVB irradiation in SKH1 hairless mice decreased tumor incidence and multiplicity as compared with control mice. Our results suggest that IP6 protects HaCaT cells from UVB-induced apoptosis and mice from UVB-induced tumors.**

I'm sure it has to do with water chemistry and dysfunction of the autonomic nervous system and the gut. Because it causes such temperature dysregulation, is Cold Thermogenesis and sunlight something still to consider to regenerate the DC current and restore the flow of electrons? Thanks Jack!

ANSWER: Nature had the foresight to make decisions in hindsight using sunlight. Why? Because light has more non linear properties than anything in the cosmos. The government found out how to make LIGHT A BIOWEAPON in the 1950s. It should be both fascinating and humbling to scientists that the massless, timeless, and phenomenal manifestation of light are the irrefutable requirement for all physical structure on Earth and in the cosmos. It turns out these abilities are why ME is mis-understood. The only constant in a universe of relentless flux because of how light works in us.

The problem with these "discoveries" is that they become systematized and programmed by paradigms. The public needs to realize that "systems and programs" are for computers.... and they are not designed for living things like us. We are designed to be wirelessly connected to the sun and fully connected to Earth to work properly in nature to remain well.

We simply need to connect with nature and feel the zeitgebers..........and Mother Nature will take care of us.

My answer is complicated and illuminating: If you read the last few blogs in my OSF series of blogs at [www.jackkruse.com](https://forum.jackkruse.com/www.jackkruse.com) you will see the reason a system goes south is because there is not enough electrons present within the cell membranes or the mitochondria of that system. Fibromyalgia is a loss of electrons in the muscles and collagen fascia of the entire body that dehydrates us (mitochondria) and lowers our piezoelectric current. This lowers the dielectric constant in cell water and it is become magnified when things like blue light and nnEMF dehydrate our cells. It usually starts in one cell type but it can spread fast if your base line redox is low. It also will alter proton recycling in the mitochondrial matrix. So if you are "young healthy 20 something" who works out in a blue lit gym, or a blue lit Harvard lab, you by definition, have a lowered redox potential because you DESTROYED POMC signaling via alpha-MSH. You also live at a high latitude and in a city with high population density when you were struck down. You likely have no idea how those two things dehydrate cells to set the stage for ME/FM/CFS. Dehydration of cells due to mitochondrial damage is the key initial starting point for ME. It can lead to a local loss in physiologic control over certain muscles too, just by a lack of red light from the skin above the muscles. The goal is not to avoid foods, molds, or toxins, with this issue, but to fix the structure of the tissues and gut by replacing the electrons lost to build the capacitor in cell water back. This turns your energy engine, the mitochondria, back on to protect your cells once again. Here is how I figured it all out.

# Thread: Second chance.

# Thread: Hypothyroid Worsening??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-12T02:56:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-14013>

Has anyone seen their hypothyroidism worsen with CT? I know it's supposed to do wonders for it, but mine seems to be going the other way. Just a few weeks ago I was doing great with my CT. I was increasing to 30 minute baths in 53 degree water. My constantly cold feet were improving, dry skin was getting better, sleep was more restful and energy levels were increasing. In the last week it seems like everything has taken a nosedive.

My feet are freezing again and my hands are so dry they're cracking. I'm having trouble falling asleep and am waking at least once throughout the night unable to fall back to sleep for hours. I never feel rested or refreshed in the mornings any longer. I also just feel "down" in both attitude and energy level. I feel like I have no motivation to do anything, especially CT. My constipation is back and I'm nearly a month late on my cycle. Could all this just be related to detox and hormone balancing?!?!

I'm on 1 grain (65 mg) Naturethroid and am wondering if I need to increase it. I have a lab slip to get re-tested, but I was holding off until after a few months of CT so I could see how it was affecting things, but now I think I need to get it checked sooner. I'll probably head in this weekend.

### selma@decna.no — 2012-04-14T23:12:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-15131>

It's difficult to know how CT is affecting thyrois. I've held back because I'm afraid of this. At the same time - CT seems to be doing wonders for clearing out rT3...

At present I've fallen down on trying CT, but not full submersion, just on my bottom-back. I hope to stimulate back-BAT this way, without too much stress... Just feeling my way.

### shilohman — 2012-04-15T01:44:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-15165>

> **BJK77;14082 wrote:** Has anyone seen their hypothyroidism worsen with CT? I know it's supposed to do wonders for it, but mine seems to be going the other way. Just a few weeks ago I was doing great with my CT. I was increasing to 30 minute baths in 53 degree water. My constantly cold feet were improving, dry skin was getting better, sleep was more restful and energy levels were increasing. In the last week it seems like everything has taken a nosedive.
>
>
>
> My feet are freezing again and my hands are so dry they're cracking. I'm having trouble falling asleep and am waking at least once throughout the night unable to fall back to sleep for hours. I never feel rested or refreshed in the mornings any longer. I also just feel "down" in both attitude and energy level. I feel like I have no motivation to do anything, especially CT. My constipation is back and I'm nearly a month late on my cycle. Could all this just be related to detox and hormone balancing?!?!
>
>
>
> I'm on 1 grain (65 mg) Naturethroid and am wondering if I need to increase it. I have a lab slip to get re-tested, but I was holding off until after a few months of CT so I could see how it was affecting things, but now I think I need to get it checked sooner. I'll probably head in this weekend.

Check your diet maybe some excess omega 6 has slipped. Dry cracked skin is a sign of omega 6 excess.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-15T02:51:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-15179>

I am doing the same as you. Up and down and all over the place with everything. My skin was doing great for awhile too and now it is worse and it has been 7 weeks or so of CT'ing. I have had nausea with headaches, hormone issues, bad pms, bad TOM, moodiness, etc. Dr. K says this isn't linear. I am not concerned and will keep on a doing. I know over the course of time things will right itself. Some of us have a lot of issues to fix and I think that is why this is so difficult. The poor body can only do so much at a time. I have read some of these threads about bad symptoms and we are not alone in this. Anything bad outside the norm and different has to be good or it wouldn't be happening. Hang in there.

### Bjkerley — 2012-04-15T04:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-15208>

Awesome initials BJK, I'm BJK83

### chocolate — 2012-04-15T05:03:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-15217>

I wouldn't have called myself hypothyroid, but I think maybe I was until I started swimming. Anyway, I think our thyroids are weather sensitive, just weather event to event.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hypothyroid-worsening.819/post-15230>

>

# Thread: BAB w/in 30 minutes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-12T03:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14033>

Are you all really able to eat your entire BAB within 30 minutes of waking? This is the only part of the protocol I seem to be struggling with. I eat a super clean diet and am really trying to stick to the leptin rx as closely as possible, but the timing is a bit tricky for me.

It's usually around an hour after waking until I am finished eating. For one thing, my BAB is so stinkin' huge it takes me a good 20-25 minutes just to eat it let alone the cooking time. I do prepare everything the night before and have it in a bowl so all I have to do is throw it in the pan to warm it up, but I take ox bile before my meals to help with fat digestion and that has to be taken 15 minutes before eating. I also try to do oil pulling every morning for 20 minutes and read that should be done prior to consuming breakfast.

I'm trying to figure out how I can fit in all of the pieces to this optimal puzzle!

### ealachan — 2012-04-12T03:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14036>

Yep, I have no problem with this part, but I've always been a hungry-first-thing-in-the-morning type, so I'm really used to getting up, making a quick pit-stop in the bathroom, then getting the kettle on and breakfast going.

### Croak — 2012-04-12T03:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14041>

I've always went by the assumption that you needed to _start_ on the BAB within 30 minutes, no need to wolf it down. Worked for me.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-12T04:23:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14057>

> **Croak;14110 wrote:** I've always went by the assumption that you needed to _start_ on the BAB within 30 minutes, no need to wolf it down. Worked for me.

Yes this is correct. I normally eat within 20 min of rising, and eating takes me about 25 min.

### cantweight — 2012-04-12T07:58:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14121>

I'll be honest, most days it more like an hour/hour+. I am getting 3 kids off to school and I havent been able to make it work yet...I know simple solution...get up early and feed myself before them. Easier said than done for me. I have a had a few of those well rested early rising days that I ate first, perhaps that will become the new norm for me. I am still having really great results. Week 5 LRX, week 3 CT

I take ox bile also...did not realize it should be taken 15 min prior to eating...thanks!

### Croak — 2012-04-12T08:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14135>

Thing is, once you get going on the LR, you'll find yourself getting up earlier, naturally. Of course, that also means bed by 11.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T10:50:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14148>

+1 on the starting BAB within 30 minutes of rising, but taking your time eating it. If I'm going to eat, I want to enjoy it (not get indigestion). Once you work out a routine, it's pretty easy.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-12T18:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14244>

Thanks for all the feedback. When I initially started the reset I thought too I only had to start eating within 30 minutes. Somewhere down the line I thought for sure I read a comment by Dr K on the blog saying we should be finished within that timeframe . . . I must have misunderstood.

It would be much easier to get it in faster too if I could get my rear end moving in the mornings. When I first started the Rx I was waking much more refreshed and ready to start my day. The last 6 weeks or so (since starting CT actually) I just feel so dang tired and have a tough time crawling out of bed to get going. Hopefully this passes soon!

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-16T17:26:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-15577>

I find it very difficult indeed to eat within 30 minutes of rising - I can manage within 40, but 30 continues to elude me unless I shower and dress after breakfast, which is not always easy to achieve.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-16T17:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-15589>

One thing that I do is have all my meat/veggies/CO measured, cut, prepped the night before, so once I'm in the kitchen, all I have to do is throw the meat on the grill, in the pan or in the oven and then go about my day. This saves a lot of time, vs. not having a plan and having to defrost something quick when you have nothing ready in the am.

### shilohman — 2012-04-16T17:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-15593>

> **PaleoCowgirl;15659 wrote:** One thing that I do is have all my meat/veggies/CO measured, cut, prepped the night before, so once I'm in the kitchen, all I have to do is throw the meat on the grill, in the pan or in the oven and then go about my day. This saves a lot of time, vs. not having a plan and having to defrost something quick when you have nothing ready in the am.

Yep that is what I do too. Saves a lot of time.

# Thread: Protein grams in meat??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-grams-in-meat.821/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nzgal — 2012-04-12T06:43:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-grams-in-meat.821/post-14101>

Was just wondering if there was any place online I could go where I could plug in how many grams of meat I had for breakfast and the exact correct protein grams would come up.

Just really wanting to make sure I hit at least 50 grams with breakfast.

Thanks guys!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T08:43:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-grams-in-meat.821/post-14133>

[www.fitday.com](http://http://www.fitday.com) (you may need to convert grams to ounces first)

### janagram — 2012-04-15T00:03:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-grams-in-meat.821/post-15144>

Wah, I don't wanna convert grams to ounces....hasn't anyone ever done this before? Like how many grams of protein are there actually in our BABs? How many in eggs? hmm, isn't it 7 grams per egg?

And how many in an 8 oz steak?

### janagram — 2012-04-15T00:07:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-grams-in-meat.821/post-15145>

ok, I looked it up...(not on fitday)

steak and most meat is about 7 grams an oz. Eggs are about 7 grams per egg.

# Thread: snacking: is coconut oil snacking?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-04-12T08:53:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14138>

how bout coconut bark?

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-12T09:55:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14146>

Yes, and yes. But some people who were really struggling in the beginning of their Leptin Rx, had a small between meal snack of CO just because it was the lesser evil. But we're not supposed to snack at all, and CO is a snack.

### freesia — 2012-04-12T11:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14151>

I needed CO between lunch and dinner for the first 4 or 5 days. Then I made sure I got some of extra oil at lunch, plus the LeptinRX worked it's charm, and I haven't needed it since.

### Petra — 2012-04-12T12:34:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14161>

Given the structure of CO, I thought it would help with the fat burning part of the diet.

I only use it when my stomach tells my brain I need lunch. That rumble makes people around me scared.

So it is a snack, yet one of the better ones in my opinion. About coco bark I don't know any thing.

Petra

### Glamorama — 2012-04-12T12:45:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14164>

Doc used to recommend it in-between meals specifically for PCOS sufferers.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-12T19:09:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14282>

It's probably on the MDA thread from a while ago.... I'd say probably pre-December. Or maybe his blogs.

Snacking is not supposed to be done because it stresses the pancreas. Every time you put calories in the body the pancreas has to work. Now I have used CO as a meal when not hungry enough for a full meal but still needed something. I'd eat a couple of Tbsp for lunch and be fine until dinner.

### janagram — 2012-04-12T19:14:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14284>

Ohhhh, thanks guys! Been having a T. or two in evening or aft. but don't really haaaave to, so won't.

August, support thread would be great!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-12T20:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snacking-is-coconut-oil-snacking.823/post-14313>

Its a snack sadly......I use it a lot especially now in spring and summer.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

My mind is playing with me, and gave me this insight for the future:

Because being cold adapted is a game changer in the medical world. And medical care can be handled differently on cold adapted patients: I suggest we should were a dog tag saying that, so that when we are in need medical staff can edit their care.

How long will it take before we can get those?

Just feeling WOW-ed,

Petra

# Thread: Wiggle room, or All-or-Nothing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ginastarke — 2012-04-12T15:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14194>

Hi all,

I'm trying to figure out how strict I need to be for the long haul, since I'm past the first 6 weeks. I have 70-something pounds to lose, and haven't had a cholesterol test in the last few months, so no nightshades for now. I try to get to bed as soon as it's completely dark, but you know how it is sometimes- life doesn't allow that.

Since I'm going to be under 25 grams carbs for the rest of the year, I was wondering if it's possible to work in some fruit as long as I stay under 25 grams, or if it has to be "permanent winter" for me until I see my goal weight.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T23:40:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14411>

I think there are two issues to consider. One is seasonality - you would never eat any fruit that wasn't in season. Two is your boy's ability to be a fat burner (vs a sugar burner). If you are a sugar burner and/or LR, fruit will simply fuel that process, mess with your blood sugar, weight loss, etc etc. Personally, if I'm craving fruit it typically means that I'm craving sugar and cravings are a symtom that something is still amiss. Just my experience.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-13T02:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14493>

I would consider opting for lower glycemic fruits. I.e. avoid:

banana

apple

pear

You can look up the glycemic load online easily. It seems that most berries have a really low glycemic load. On the other hand we don't need fruit in the short term and I haven't had more than a handful of grapes in the last 3 months. I plan on loading up over summer though! As far as I understand it it's the fructose in the fruit that gets the insulin resistance going as it has to be processed by the liver. I know there are plenty of beneficial food stuffs in fruit, I'm just laying off of them while doing CT etc...

jesse

### ginastarke — 2012-04-13T02:28:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14498>

Summer's what I'm thinking about, too. I could live without most fruit, but going all summer without any Oregon strawberries or wild blackberries is a little unbearable. In my area in August, you can smell ripe blackberries on the wind, and it's more tempting than coffee, bakery, heck even a barbecue smell, (I bike home hungry for dinner, LOL ) I've been low carb so long I'm definitely a fat burner. I get a kind of sugar high off prime rib - only it lasts a lot longer without a crash!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Cheers from Spain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheers-from-spain.828/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AndrÃ©s — 2012-04-12T17:06:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheers-from-spain.828/post-14224>

Hi there!

I have been on paleo-like way of eating since my waking-up from the conventional wisdom faith one and a half years ago. Paleo-like because I just love hard cheeses, those forbidden to me for years due to a "high cholesterol", and I am at the moment also having some potatoes and rice, PHD-like. I am not trying to cure any ailment at all on myself and I will try (slowly) to go for optimal. My father is another case with its COPD, that I am trying to keep in check with vitamins D and C, and NAC in two weeks. I do not foresee convincing him to try CT in the immediate future.

I suppose I am 1/10th cold adapted, since it has been more than 20 years showering always in cold water. I am 43. I have been trying to expose myself to cold air in the last month (driving with AC on and walking at night outside in shorts), and I had to stop twice due to two colds that I have treated [titrating with ascorbic acid](http://http://www.doctoryourself.com/titration.html). So it seems that I will have to be more strict with the diet (have I mentioned cheese? ) on my third attempt, this time with cold baths, probably next autumn.

Actually, I am an ascorbic-nut for the same reason that I am focusing on being a cold-nut. They just make sense:

-   Ascorbic acid: animals without the [GULO mutation](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L-gulonolactone_oxidase) [increases their ascorbic acid production upon stress](http://http://perfecthealthdiet.com/?p=636).
    CT: Successful polar explorers ate only meat with lots of fat and they did not become hypothyroid (or with euthyroid sick syndrome), AFAIK.

So the next logical step is self-experimentation.

Today the doctor has said us that my father (77) has to undergone a prostatectomy in the near future. I think I will have to accelerate my cold adaptation if I want to have a slight possibility of making up his mind. All of you will be my inspiration.

Cheers!

# Thread: Thermometers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Santiago — 2012-04-12T18:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-14254>

Hi everyone,

I was looking for a good thermometer to read the skin temperature, and the infrared ones looked perfected, however I've realized that the accuracy is +- 2ÂºC, which is not very precise if one wants to make sure the skin is at 10/12ÂºC.

Mercury thermometers don't seem ideal for this application, cause probably the skin get warmed during the measure.

Do you any recommendation for precise thermometers?.

Best.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-14347>

Even the best IR thermometers are about 1.5 deg C accurate. I used my infrared thermometer to measure a couple of mercury thermometers and determine how far off it was at different temps.

For instance, I have a mercury thermometer that is accurate to +/- .1 deg C. I hung that in my garage for a couple hours. It read 62 degrees F. I took my IR thermometer and aimed it at the base of the mercury thermometer and it read 61.5 deg F.

I did this at a couple different temperatures, and was able to determine that my IR thermometer was about 1/2 a degree low over the range of 30 - 75 deg F. That sort of put my mind at rest that it was fairly accurate to measure skin temp in the 55-75 deg range.

Hope that helps.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-14T03:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-14982>

That helps me. I use an IR therm and I don't have another therm to compare it against, so as long as it's reading in the 50-52f range then I'm assuming my skin is

### jsrhoades — 2012-04-15T03:41:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-15194>

A couple of very accurate reference points: Well-stirred water with ice will be at 32 deg F. Boiling water will be at 212 deg F. at sea level. Reduce by one deg F. per each 560 feet above sea level.

I bought a digital meat thermometer (Oxo brand) from Target for a $5-6 bucks, and it seems quite accurate. It read 31.6 in ice water and 212.0 in boiling water (altitude 250 feet). I think it is accurate for skin temp by laying the metal tip sidewise against the skin, but I haven't calibrated that.

### Santiago — 2012-04-15T21:08:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-15361>

I think I'll invest on a expensive (about $100) precise thermometer. For peace of mind, I think it will be the cheapest decision on the long run.

I'll buy something like this ( +- 1ÂºC) when I do a trip to US (customs and currency exchanges make it crazy buy it from Europe)

[http://www.amazon.com/Fluke-62-Mini-Infrared-Thermometer/dp/B000MX5Y9C/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1334513222&sr=8-1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Fluke-62-Mini-Infrared-Thermometer/dp/B000MX5Y9C/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1334513222&sr=8-1)

### hellojtm — 2012-04-15T22:27:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-15376>

I dropped my IR thermometer in the tub. Thankfully I dehydrated it in the oven over nite (just pilot light) and it's functional again! But I don't trust it, I'll just use jsrhoades good advice to double check it when it becomes convenient.

Santiago, hola amigo, Usted vive en EspaÃ±a?

jesse (from Art's forum)

I just got back from a week in Mexico and discovered it was actaully difficult for me to get color early on when I was using sunscreen. ### Rhoswen — 2012-04-13T08:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-14624>

For me using coconut oil on my face keeps it looking super nice. I still have some acne, so I like to mix a little tea tree oil and a little coconut oil together in my hands at bed time after I wash my face. I wake up in the morning and my skin just looks so nice!

I'm a very pale, like a ghost, and a red head. I found that when I eat clean, I rarely burn in the sun ever even with no sunscreen. In fact, last summer when I stuck to the Paleo lifestyle, I actually got a tan lines on my feet and arms, even though most people still thought I was ghostly pale, haha! My mom thought the tan lines on my feet were hysterical cause it really did make my toes look super white, like porcelain.

I like to use an all natural cleanser on my face that I got from an Etsy seller right [here](http://http://www.etsy.com/listing/89832398/balance-organic-foaming-face-wash-all) and I use [this](http://http://www.etsy.com/listing/62300993/botanical-hydrating-toner-organic-facial) toner. The cleanser smells DIVINE. When I use those with my coconut/tea tree oil combo, my skin looks great! Could probably skip the tea tree oil if you don't have any acne problems.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-14T09:49:04+0300

I greatly enjoy the all natural products I've found. I actually look forward to washing my face at night.

I have noticed my skin is much more taught since I started CTing and others have said so. Do you think spot CTing on the face has made wrinkles less noticable, Shilohman?

### thyme — 2012-04-15T13:07:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-15271>

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-16T14:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-15540>

Granted I am 47, but I just had my second Moh's surgery in January to remove Basal Cell Carcinoma from my head. This is the most common form of skin cancer and given my age, I wanted to know if I could do BHRT or not with this type of cancer history. It turns out that estrogen is protective against BCC - although NOT true for other skin cancers. That would explain as my hormonal cascade was plummeting that voila I now have BCC. FWIW, I did NOT go BHRT as of yet. Instead I got serious about my Paleo/Primal and started the Leptin Rx with chronic low vit D as my major motivator.

On the vanity end of things... well I have a mini Mohawk growing where the 15 staples and skin graft put my head back together. I do use La Roche Posay's Mela -D for my face and also Le Roc's sensitive versions of Retinol about 50% of the time. I started those last Aug and that has aided some, but the biggest change came with the Leptin Rx - skin everywhere is better - except I think it is die off that is causing some erruptive eczema on my knuckles.

BCC doesn't usually kill, but it does disfigure. And watch the legs. For women the more serious skin cancers start there. So before shedding the sunscreens entirely, think how close you are to optimal and make sure you have your estrogen rocking its protective self.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T14:41:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-15543>

> **vera;15152 wrote:** One of the leading experts on vitamin d dr. michael holick says we don't absorb vitamin d all that well from facial skin. while it's too cold for short sleeves and shorts, i let the sun shine on my face with no sun block. once the sun gets stronger, i will wear a hat and expose my arms and legs. i don't mind all that much if my swag goes to sag. it's normal. if i start freaking out about it as it gets worse, i'll see a plastic surgeon and get injections or something.

I read, a long time ago, that UofM did a study on what part of the skin absorbed Vitamin D the best and it was the back of the knees.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T01:40:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-17256>

[http://www.vogue.com.au/beauty/news/why+glycobiology+matters,17559](http://http://www.vogue.com.au/beauty/news/why+glycobiology+matters,17559)

### chocolate — 2012-05-16T22:32:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-24678>

[http://www.naturalnews.com/025302\_skin\_disease\_piperine.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/025302_skin_disease_piperine.html)

Skin Pigment Disease Reversed with Piperine Nutrient From Black Pepper

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/025302\_skin\_disease\_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xE9I00](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/025302_skin_disease_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xE9I00)

An extract derived from black pepper may be able to help reverse the effects of the skin pigmentation disease known as vitiligo, according to research conducted by scientists from Kings College London and published in the Ridge Journal of Dermatology.

Vitiligo is a disease in which sections of the skin lose their pigmentation and turn white, often leading to a blotchy, mottled look. Because melanin, the chemical that gives skin its pigmentation, also protects against the ultraviolet radiation causes skin cancer, people with vitiligo may be at increased risk for that disease.

The current study focused on piperine, the chemical that gives black pepper its spiciness. Researchers applied piperine and its synthetic derivatives to the skin of mice, and found that doing so caused the animals' skin to turn an even light brown after only six weeks.

When combined with ultraviolet radiation treatment, piperine caused the mice's skin to become darker faster. The effect was also more long-lasting

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/025302\_skin\_disease\_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xUaGHw](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/025302_skin_disease_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xUaGHw)

### chocolate — 2012-05-20T19:12:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-25740>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430)

Photodamage of the skin: protection and reversal with topical antioxidants.

Burke KE.

Source

[heather.nolan@gs.com](mailto:heather.nolan@gs.com)

Abstract

Controversy exists as to whether topical antioxidants can be effective in protecting against and reversing photodamage to the skin. Topical vitamins C and E, as well as topical selenium, protect skin against sunburn, suntan and skin cancer and **also reverse the mottled pigmentation **and wrinkles of photoageing**. However, only certain forms of these labile antioxidants are stable and active after percutaneous absorption. For effective topical application, vitamin C must be non-esterified, acidic and optimally at 20% concentration; vitamin E must be the non-esterified isomer d-alpha-tocopherol at 2-5% concentration. Selenium is only percutaneously absorbed and active when applied topically as l-selenomethionine,** optimally at 0.02-0.05%. There are two great advantages in applying an active formulation of topical antioxidants to the skin. First, the skin attains far higher levels of each antioxidant than can be achieved by only taking these vitamins orally. The level of vitamin C attained in the skin by topical application is 20-40 times that achievable with oral vitamin C. With topical application, the concentration of vitamin E in the skin increases by a factor of 10.6 and selenium by a factor of 1.7. Second, topical application arms the skin with a reservoir of antioxidants that cannot be washed or rubbed off, a protection which stays in the skin for several days after application.

You can buy knock off knox gelatin and play around with making your own peels. It just paints on and showers off. It bondos your wrinkles also. Saran wrap is you friend. Whether its circadian or not.

### chocolate — 2012-08-14T03:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-44807>

UC Davis researchers identify cellular basis for how anti-aging costmetics work

> UC Davis researchers identify cellular basis for how anti-aging costmetics work
>
> Published: Monday, August 13, 2012 - 16:05 in Biology & Nature
>
>
>
> A team of investigators from UC Davis and Peking University have discovered a mechanism that may explain how alpha hydroxyl acids (AHAs) ― the key ingredient in cosmetic chemical peels and wrinkle-reducing creams ― work to enhance skin appearance. An understanding of the underlying process may lead to better cosmetic formulations as well as have medical applications. The findings were published in the Journal of Biological Chemistry in an article entitled "Intracellular proton-mediated activation of TRPV3 channels accounts for exfoliation effect of alpha hydroxyl acids on keratinocytes."
>
>
>
> AHAs are a group of weak acids typically derived from natural sources such as sugar cane, sour milk, apples and citrus that are well known in the cosmetics industry for their ability to enhance the appearance and texture of skin. Before this research, little was known about how AHAs actually caused skin to flake off and expose fresh, underlying skin.
>
>
>
> The cellular pathway the research team studied focuses on an ion channel ― known as transient receptor potential vanilloid 3 (TRPV3) ― located in the cell membrane of keratinocytes, the predominant cell type in the outer layer of skin. The channel is known from other studies to play an important role in normal skin physiology and temperature sensitivity.
>
> In a series of experiments that involved recording electrical currents across cultured cells exposed to AHAs, the investigators developed a model that describes how glycolic acid (the smallest and most biologically available AHA) enters into keratinocytes and generates free protons, creating acidic conditions within the cell. The low pH strongly activates the TRPV3 ion channel, opening it and allowing calcium ions to flow into the cell. Because more protons also enter through the open TRPV3 channel, the process feeds on itself. The resulting calcium ion overload in the cell leads to its death and skin exfoliation.
>
>
>
> "Our experiments are the first to show that the TRPV3 ion channel is likely to be the target of the most effective skin enhancer in the cosmetics industry," said Jie Zheng, professor of physiology and membrane biology at UC Davis and one of the principal investigators of the study. "Although AHAs have been used for years, no one until now understood their likely mechanism of action."
>
>
>
> Besides being found in skin cells, TRPV3 also is found in cells in many areas of the nervous system and is sensitive to temperature as well as acidity. The authors speculate that the channel may have a variety of important physiological functions, including pain control.
>
>
>
> Lead author Xu Cao, who conducted the study with UC Davis scientists as a visiting student from Peking University Health Science Center, focuses on TRPV3 channel research. With a team of researchers in China, he recently contributed to the discovery that a mutation in TRPV3 leads to Olmsted syndrome, a rare congenital disorder characterized by severe itching and horny skin development over the palms of the hands and soles of the feet. While in the UC Davis Department of Physiology and Membrane Biology, Cao discovered that AHAs also utilize the TRPV3 channel.
>
>
>
> "**Calcium channels are becoming increasingly recognized as having vital functions in skin physiology," said Cao. "TRPV3 has the potential to become an important target not only for the cosmetics industry but for analgesia and treating skin disease**."

I use this stuff every now and again. Makes you really understand the consequences of eating fruit.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-14T16:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-44897>

I've used every anti-aging cream on the market, including AHA's. I even met with a derm who wanted to do aggressive laser treatments on me. I read about the potential hazards and cancelled my appointment.

Funny thing is, I started Paleo and then epi-Paleo eating and people keep telling me how much better my skin looks.

In the past few weeks alone, 3 people have thought I was in my mid-30's (I am 50). I went to an ER for what I thought was a fish bone in my throat (gotta love epi-Paleo!). The ER doc called an ENT to get me a scope; I was in the other room, but I heard the ER doc describe me to the ENT doc as a "Female in her mid-30's".

I can't help wondering if all the anti-aging creams are worth it, and if they can actually harm.

### chocolate — 2012-08-30T05:27:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-48296>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/29/bacterial.cause.found.skin.condition.rosacea?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/29/bacterial.cause.found.skin.condition.rosacea?)

Bacterial cause found for skin condition rosacea

Published: Wednesday, August 29, 2012 - 19:31 in Health & Medicine

> Scientists are closer to establishing a definitive bacterial cause for the skin condition rosacea. This will allow more targeted, effective treatments to be developed for sufferers, according to a review published in the Journal of Medical Microbiology. Rosacea is a common dermatological condition that causes reddening and inflammation of the skin mostly around the cheeks, nose and chin. In severe cases skin lesions may form and lead to disfigurement. Rosacea affects around 3% of the population -- usually fair-skinned females aged 30-50 and particularly those with weak immune systems. The condition is treated with a variety of antibiotics, even though there has never been a well-established bacterial cause.
>
>
>
> A new review carried out by the National University of Ireland concludes that rosacea may be triggered by bacteria that live within tiny mites that reside in the skin.
>
>
>
> The mite species Demodex folliculorum is worm-like in shape and usually lives harmlessly inside the pilosebaceous unit which surrounds hair follicles of the face. They are normal inhabitants of the face and increase in number with age and skin damage -- for example, following exposure to sunlight. The numbers of Demodex mites living in the skin of rosacea patients is higher than in normal individuals, which has previously suggested a possible role for the mites in initiating the condition.
>
>
>
> More recently, the bacterium Bacillus oleronius was isolated from inside a Demodex mite and was found to produce molecules provoking an immune reaction in rosacea patients. Other studies have shown patients with varying types of rosacea react to the molecules produced by this bacterium -- exposing it as a likely trigger for the condition. What's more, this bacterium is sensitive to the antibiotics used to treat rosacea.
>
>
>
> Dr Kevin Kavanagh who conducted the review explained, "The bacteria live in the digestive tracts of Demodex mites found on the face, in a mutually beneficial relationship. When the mites die, the bacteria are released and leak into surrounding skin tissues -- triggering tissue degradation and inflammation."

### chocolate — 2012-10-02T03:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-55745>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/10/01/marine.animals.could.hold.key.looking.young?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/10/01/marine.animals.could.hold.key.looking.young?)

> Marine animals could hold the key to looking young
>
> Published: Monday, October 1, 2012 - 15:05 in Biology & Nature
>
>
>
> Sea cucumbers and sea urchins are able to change the elasticity of collagen within their bodies, and could hold the key to maintaining a youthful appearance, according to scientists at Queen Mary, University of London. The researchers investigated the genes of marine creatures such as sea urchins and sea cucumbers, known as echinoderms. They found the genes for "messenger molecules" known as peptides, which are released by cells and tell other cells in their bodies what to do.
>
>
>
> The study was published online in the journals PLOS ONE and General and Comparative Endocrinology.

### Jamie S — 2020-01-10T22:45:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284397>

> [chocolate said:](/goto/post?id=25740)
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430)
>
> Photodamage of the skin: protection and reversal with topical antioxidants.
>
> Burke KE.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> [heather.nolan@gs.com](mailto:heather.nolan@gs.com)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Controversy exists as to whether topical antioxidants can be effective in protecting against and reversing photodamage to the skin. Topical vitamins C and E, as well as topical selenium, protect skin against sunburn, suntan and skin cancer and **also reverse the mottled pigmentation **and wrinkles of photoageing**. However, only certain forms of these labile antioxidants are stable and active after percutaneous absorption. For effective topical application, vitamin C must be non-esterified, acidic and optimally at 20% concentration; vitamin E must be the non-esterified isomer d-alpha-tocopherol at 2-5% concentration. Selenium is only percutaneously absorbed and active when applied topically as l-selenomethionine,** optimally at 0.02-0.05%. There are two great advantages in applying an active formulation of topical antioxidants to the skin. First, the skin attains far higher levels of each antioxidant than can be achieved by only taking these vitamins orally. The level of vitamin C attained in the skin by topical application is 20-40 times that achievable with oral vitamin C. With topical application, the concentration of vitamin E in the skin increases by a factor of 10.6 and selenium by a factor of 1.7. Second, topical application arms the skin with a reservoir of antioxidants that cannot be washed or rubbed off, a protection which stays in the skin for several days after application.
>
>
>

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-11T20:51:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284447>

> [PrimalPam said:](/goto/post?id=44897)
>
> I've used every anti-aging cream on the market, including AHA's. I even met with a derm who wanted to do aggressive laser treatments on me. I read about the potential hazards and cancelled my appointment.
>
>
>
> Funny thing is, I started Paleo and then epi-Paleo eating and people keep telling me how much better my skin looks.
>
>
>
> In the past few weeks alone, 3 people have thought I was in my mid-30's (I am 50). I went to an ER for what I thought was a fish bone in my throat (gotta love epi-Paleo!). The ER doc called an ENT to get me a scope; I was in the other room, but I heard the ER doc describe me to the ENT doc as a "Female in her mid-30's".
>
>
>
> I can't help wondering if all the anti-aging creams are worth it, and if they can actually harm.

Persevere on your skin beauty quest-- you will find answers that work for you if you leave no stone unturned.

As a man I'm not interested in cosmetic coverups, but I surely don't want to look prematurely old with wrinkles and hyperpigmented skin lesions, etc.

I believe skin health comes most authentically from the inside out. So, besides a relatively clean diet and supplements, I ingest a cocktail consisting of MSM, BioSil, and marine collagen almost daily. It helps keep skin, ligaments, and other connective tissue plump, healthy, and youthful.

Now there is an exciting Ayurvedic natural substance growing in popularity for the anti-aging crowd.

It is **Bakuchiol** oil from plant _Psoralea Corylifolia._ Touted as a safer alternative to retinol it is highly reputed with abilities to reduce hyperpigmentation, inflammation, wrinkles (fine lines), and photoaging, and also boosts elastin and collagen production.

May you look more beautiful and youthful as you grow older. We health-aware guys are counting on you gals to keep up with us!

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-13T18:01:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284511>

> [Jamie S said:](/goto/post?id=284479)
>
> Thanks for the heads up... trying to keep up  What marine collagen do you use? I think I read about Bakuchiol in Dave Aspreys book. Which one do you use there? Looking forward to plumping!

Yes, Mr Asprey had a paragraph about Bakuchiol in Chapter 13 of his book [Superhuman.](https://www.amazon.com/Super-Human-Bulletproof-Backward-Forever/dp/0062882821/ref=sr_1_1?keywords=superhuman&qid=1578930391&s=books&sr=1-1)

In that chapter he discusses quite a few therapies for aging skin and graying hair. Some of his ideas sound practical to me, others not so practical (e.g. **cryotherapy**, where one strands in air temperature of **\-270 degrees F** for up to 3 minutes).

I currently use CollagenUP brand, which is sourced from enzymatically hydrolized fish collagen peptides, with added Vit C and hyaluronic acid. Its taste is relatively benign and I prefer it to beef collagen which tastes quite yucky 'gamey' to me.

You might want to try a beauty hack I affectionately called **Inger's Protocol**, which is alternating cold water face baths with red light exposures.

Good luck.

### Inger — 2020-01-15T11:33:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284616>

I watched a older lady as waiting to pay my groceries.. she was maybe around 70 yo, had almost zero wrinkles, but was also pale. Like Paul Newman above  She was not healthy at all, had trouble walking, overweight etc.
I had to think about this conversation... and how I have seen so many older people with almost no wrinkles, but all very unhealthy.
Then I know very wrinkly people, who are very healthy.

I am going to stop thinking wrinkles are bad.

I am still going to do my beauty routine though, I am happy if it helps. But to avoid the sun to avoid wrinkles is insanity to me.
Also, when you have lots of scars and wrinkles... man you are a WARRIOR. Yeah. Life was hard with you, but you survived and came out better of it. Hell yes, maybe we should be proud of our wrinkles instead
I know, it is hard. All media is so anti-wrinkle!

Christine, I have some gray hairs too...lol Not going to color my hair that is for sure.

### Christine_L — 2020-01-15T19:00:27+0200

She used to sun tan in a tanning booth everyday but now she does not. My other coworker is in her 40s with no wrinkles at all. She eats fat and is overweight. They are both very pale. I think the first girl has more wrinkles due to not eating fat and because she avoids the sun now. But what do I know! lol

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-15T19:23:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284650>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=284616)

> Also, when you have lots of scars and wrinkles... man you are a WARRIOR. Yeah. Life was hard with you, but you survived and came out better of it. Hell yes, maybe we should be proud of our wrinkles instead
> I know, it is hard. All media is so anti-wrinkle!
>
> Christine, I have some gray hairs too...lol Not going to color my hair that is for sure.

Inger, I'm glad you are comfortable in your own skin, and have made peace with signs of aging. That is a healthy attitude.

Of course we all have our own experiences to draw on with regard to perceptions of people's aging (and I'm guilty like many of [confirmation bias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confirmation_bias) in this).

However, generally I conclude that most people look their age or are even worse for wear in this toxic and stressful modern age. That is, in contrast to your older lady's appearance, they have pale and weathered skin with noticeable wrinkles. To complete the average senior's appearance (> 70 years for exam.), they often look a bit frail and stiff and walk with some discomfort.

Of course like everything in nature there just have to be big exceptions to the rule (keeps things interesting!). Who hasn't read or heard about very old people who are still active and healthy and yet who claim for example that they have smoked a pack of cigarettes and drunk a bottle of scotch every day of their life? **Good genes are priceless**.

Now, it is known that face aging characteristics are somewhat associated with ethnicity. For example, _European_ women can tend to develop lots of fine lines as they age, while _slavic_ types are known more for heavy facial folds and creases rather than fine lines. Interestingly, I have heard that **Catholic nuns** as a group tend to resist skin aging better and have longer longevity than other women. I doubt they are sun worshippers who strip daily to soak in sunlight outside in convent gardens, but I do think it has a lot to do with their healthy and active lifestyle and their calm spiritual attitudes. On the other hand, who hasn't seen pictures of modern cowboys with deeply carved and leathered skin from a lifetime in the saddle on the range?

What is indisputable is that sun UV exposure is associated with up to 80% of skin aging (_sunsafe rx_). I know a lot of Krusians will howl with disapproval at this, and that is okay. It should be noted that Kruse tribe members are doing a lot of other things to help mitigate and minimize resulting skin damage. As a side note, since UV rays are more intense at high altitudes, you get a generous UV dose from rays coming through aircraft windows (flight attendants have an increased risk of melanoma from this, according an analysis of studies in May-June 2006 issue of the _Journal of Travel Medicine).
_
So, all things being equal there seem to be two main variables (besides genes, stress, and diet) involved in accelerated skin aging-- _smoking and sun exposure_. And according to one study, your number of wrinkles is significantly related to your total hours of lifetime sun exposure (_Sunsafe RX_)

Nonetheless, the tradeoff (for some Krusians with greater sun exposure which may mean quickened photo aging) in exchange for better overall health seems to be a good one. Go for it.

### drezy — 2020-01-15T20:18:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284654>

> [ElectricUniverse said:](/goto/post?id=284650)
>
> So, all things being equal there seem to be two main variables (besides genes, stress, and diet) involved in accelerated skin aging-- _smoking and sun exposure_. And according to one study, your number of wrinkles is significantly related to your total hours of lifetime sun exposure (_Sunsafe RX_)
>
> Nonetheless, the tradeoff (for some Krusians with greater sun exposure which may mean quickened photo aging) in exchange for better overall health seems to be a good one. Go for it.

I think you have to look deeper into studies like those and sort out so many factors. Also understand what has to happen in skin cells prior to being damaged. I don't think the "quickened" will be as fast as some might think. There are many factors involved.

From: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/)
"Alpha melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). Upon exposure to sunshine, melanocytes and keratinocytes in the skin release α-MSH, which has been implicated in immunologic tolerance and suppression of contact hypersensitivity. α-MSH also helps limit oxidative DNA damage resulting from UVR and increases gene repair, thus reducing melanoma risk, as reported 15 May 2005 in _Cancer Research_."

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-17T18:10:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284732>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=284654)
>
> I think you have to look deeper into studies like those and sort out so many factors. Also understand what has to happen in skin cells prior to being damaged. I don't think the "quickened" will be as fast as some might think. There are many factors involved.
>
>
> From: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/)
> "Alpha melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). Upon exposure to sunshine, melanocytes and keratinocytes in the skin release α-MSH, which has been implicated in immunologic tolerance and suppression of contact hypersensitivity. α-MSH also helps limit oxidative DNA damage resulting from UVR and increases gene repair, thus reducing melanoma risk, as reported 15 May 2005 in _Cancer Research_."

With what little we definitely know now about aging, it is not certain about all factors that are in play with photo aging, given an individual's lifestyle and health practices. That is why I say only time will tell about outcomes from Black Swan's propensity to high levels of UV/sunlight exposure.

If we are doing things right with Dr Kruse's optimal health strategies most us would have potential to live a long time-- far longer than modern average lifespan. Whether we would relish living to a healthy 100 years but wrinkled as a prune is up to the individual.

The game changer is if a complete skin renewal/rejuvenating method were invented, possibly with stem cells. Not sure I would want to be the first guinea pig going there, however.

What if that process went terribly awry, and I ended up looking like this below picture. Would women still hit on me?

### crix — 2020-01-23T15:35:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284953>

This is likely old news to everyone here, but many
conventional dermatologists and plastic surgery clinics now use lasers (such as fractional CO2 devices) to remove signs of aging in the skin. This video is from the famous Mayo
Clinic <

\>.

Here is more information from "Lasers and IPL Techniques in Dermotology and Aesthetics", edited by Christian Raulin and Syrus Karsai:

"Fractional skin resurfacing generates improvements in surface texture, wrinkling, and global skin appearance. The treatment is ideally suited to patients with mild to moderate textural changes or rhytids who desire more dramatic results than traditional nonablative lasers may provide but less downtime than traditional ablative therapy. Fractional resurfacing delivers ablative or nonablative laser energy in a pixilated pattern, creating microscopic columns of thermal injury in the epidermis and dermis. These coagulated areas are surrounded by areas of untouched skin, which allow for rapid healing and re-epithelialization. As a result, fractional resurfacing more effectively treats rhytids and surface texture compared with nonablative or skin-tightening techniques and offers decreased down- time and a reduced risk of adverse events relative to ablative laser skin treatment."

Full laser resurfacing of the face can cost $1000, but I have seen used CO2 fractional lasers for sale for as low as $5000-$10000. A group of likeminded people in the same area could get together, buy one, and train themselves how to use it using free material put out by the manufacturer.

My statement obviously wasn't meant to be a generalized blanket condemnation of all pretty girls, and I think I worded it right then and I would say it again.

First of all, pretty faces are a dime a dozen, and I don't find a close correlation between looks and high character.

I know many very attractive, mature women (younger women under say, 35, are generally not mature in many character aspects-- often they are just looking for a good time and material pursuits until the biological clock starts ticking loudly) that are top shelf in almost every refined attribute that would be desirable to discerning men. However, I wouldn't say they are representative of the average female, especially in this [Kali Yuga](https://www.yogapedia.com/definition/5302/kali-yuga) age. That is what makes them so special, and yes indeed, uncommon. Same goes for men in general.

# Thread: Cool vs. Cold, Ray Cronise' Perspective
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-12T19:25:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14288>

This was on [www.freetheanimal.com](http://http://www.freetheanimal.com), and got lost in the convoluted threading of comments, so I will post it all. Very interesting.

"Cool vs Cold?

Here is where I think the big gap exists. I think chronic cool trumps acute cold in terms of metabolic function. It sounds like the cliche turtle and hare argument, but metabolically it is much different. Let me share some data from last week (while we were all waiting for you comments to load and crashing iPhones).

For 7 days last week we had unseasonably warm temps in north Alabama â€“ highs in mid 80sF and lows in upper 60s. I measured my waking RMR every morning, before eating, just out of bed. It averaged 1582 kcal. Now, over the weekend the temperature dropped down to the 70s day/40-50s night. I let my house ride with outside temperature up to max of 70F(21C). That mean when I woke, the house temp was between 55-60F.

The last three days, my waking RMR was 1900, 1930, and 1920 kcal. Nearly a 4 mile run every day for no additional effort. As well, I know that my sleep time is much better during these cold evenings.

Some more interesting data. I did some cold soak baths (50F/10C) (10 minutes) using CO2/O2 monitoring. What I found was pretty amazing. As you would expect, I saw a boost in CO2 production on entering the bath. Anyone that has done this knows there is a slight pain period if the water is cold followed by a relaxation about two minutes after. During that time, when the pain vanished, my CO2 production dropped as well. It did not go all the way back to baseline.

But it gets better. I allowed it to come to rest (now 5 minutes in) and since I had my hands out of the water (someone here mentioned their feet â€“ so this is for you), once again CO2 went back up. What it tells me is the body VERY quickly (less than a minute) assesses and adjusts the blood flow accordingly. But it goes further. After a minute more I am back to a new settled point and when I stand up to get out â€“ it goes up AGAIN â€“ this time responding to air/evaporative loss driven by my warm body, room humidity and thin water film (no discernible movement in the room). Finally 10 minutes after I started it was still up. If youâ€™ve ever dehydrated running â€“ youâ€™ll know about that cold flash that happens as that last film of perspiration flashes off.

Now I post this to let you know that this is all extremely complex â€“ the body is absolutely AMAZING engineering. keeping a 180 lb hunk of titanium +/-.5 degrees is hard enough (ok yes, engineers, -thermal conductivity â€“ its an example), but our body does it every day.

On ice immersions Wimâ€™s core temperature RISES by a degree. skin settles in just above freezing.

If I was going to give EVERYONE a recommendation it would actually be quite simple: swim. I know itâ€™s not â€œgrokâ€￾ or â€œdripping testosterone,â€￾ just do it. If the water is about 80F/27C or lower you are in GREAT territory. Swim. My former company manufactured pools and yet I didnâ€™t learn to swim until AFTER I lost my weight. I was an avid scuba diver and wasnâ€™t afraid in any way of the water, but didnâ€™t know how to swim â€œlaps.â€￾

Make sure you control caloric intake in that window (2-4 hours) after and if you can do this in the morning while fasting, all the better.

80 doesnâ€™t sound like much, but there is nearly 20F delta in temperature and that drives heat loss (energy not, not temperature). Because you are physically active, blood stays flowing to the skin to dump and radiate. What several studies have noted is that hunger rises 2-4 hours after you swim and that is homeostasis kicking in trying to return us to â€œnormal.â€￾ I debunk the swimming makes you fat here: [http://bit.ly/Ictcwo](http://http://bit.ly/Ictcwo)

Added to this take a look at this post: [http://bit.ly/HC9lFR](http://http://bit.ly/HC9lFR) from Scott Parazynski. We have a lot of data concerning his weight loss on Everest climbs. Itâ€™s grueling, but it provides a lot of insight to how the body processes cold. (incidentally Wim made it to about 24K feet in shorts, no oxygen and no shirt. He had to turn back because of the foot injury filming the -40F run you may have seen on discovery. 3 months and it hadnâ€™t healed. Wim is just a bad-ass I donâ€™t care what you say).

There are literally hundreds of papers Iâ€™ve poured over since 2008. There is so much data itâ€™s overwhelming. I am very happy to contribute to pushing it all in this direction, but if you want to REALLY help, then I will say, Ice is a great â€œmental game.â€￾ It letâ€™s you know you can â€œdo it.â€￾ It can be great for adrenaline junkies. It helps sports injuries. Itâ€™s extreme.

â€¦but you probably can do much more metabolic good with periodic chronic cool. Swimming, cool walks, a few less layers, etcâ€¦ This means you should be able to thermal load all the way through the summer.

Scott, Wim, and I are continuing to work through all of this. There are dozens of other scientists we have consulted with, from Harvard to Holland, and everyone is very positive and supportive. We have a few things (including the breathing mentioned above) that will get everyone going and are trying to distill it down to some basic steps.

Please note, this is NOT an endurance sport. Thereâ€™s nothing to prove. Itâ€™s more like chasing parked cars â€“ eventually youâ€™re going to catch one and it isnâ€™t pretty. I hope everyone gets that lesson loud and clear from me. If you want to go extreme, then please have medical supervision â€“ or at least a 911 speed dial (youâ€™re doing what?). Remember the BIGGEST risk is not cooling down or surviving cold â€“ itâ€™s warming up. That is where you will get in trouble and people can and will die. I canâ€™t over emphasize this. Tim and I were careful to stay within reasonable exposure worlds. Donâ€™t be foolish.

I am headed for TEDMED2012, which moved to the Kennedy Center this year. I apologize that this is my only comment for now (perhaps you are relieved

Thanks everyone and letâ€™s keep this out of ad hominem attacks. It doesnâ€™t move us forward. Thereâ€™s plenty to learn and plenty credit to go around. Like Newton said, â€￾ If I have seen further it is by standing on ye sholders of Giants.â€￾

Ray

Letâ€™s bring this together."

### hellojtm — 2012-04-12T19:36:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14294>

Hi AKMan,

I like Ray but after all his recent dietary posts I've kind of lost interest. Sorry for this off the cuff editorial but I think he blackboxes the metabolism too much. So much of what he writes about cold is just how much energy you burn. An example of why I don't buy into this is intermittent fasting. IF has metabolic benefits that aren't because of limited total weekly caloric intake. There is a big difference in metabolic adaptation that can occur from deep stimulus. Here is a better example, he's basically arguing that jogging is superior to sprinting with his 80F is superior to 50-55F argument. But the stimulus is totally different. If you want to gain muscle then you'd sprint. If you want to gain muscle you lift to failure (or close to it) and then have a long recovery time. I wantonly believe that it's the same for cold exposure, you want the "deep inroad" (to steal a term from Body By Science) and then a long recovery.

best,

jesse

### chocolate — 2012-04-12T19:48:45+0300

I'm kinda with Jesse. I'm just swimming in a health club pool right now, but the unheated pool and cold showers were doing me much better. Plus when you read the neonate gut flora, it is for the 98 degree range and the 50 degree connoisseur range. Plus I get lasting results without getting sore in the cold. I just wake up harder and it makes me want it more cold. Plus the chlorine isn't so crappy when its cold.

### janagram — 2012-04-12T19:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14303>

this is all science (awesome)...but a bit over my head, however, I too have wondered, why don't these folks just get a swimming pool and not heat it? and do something natural, like swimming?

Also, from my reading, the pre-communist Tibetans were very cold adapted. A guy who was in prison there said the cold was so painful at night, but added, everyone in Tibet was cold at night....there was no heating!!! They let toddlers go naked, covered in yak butter, etc...

I live in California, but was a kid in No. Dakota...and have wondered just why people settled there when they could've migrated south...but maybe they knew something we didn't! (Of course I loved the snow when I was a kid!!)

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T20:02:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14305>

Ray's motives are clearly fat-loss and not a cure-all for disease and longevity, but many around here are also just after fat-loss. As far as I know, Dr. K and Ray C are working together on some projects, so I definitely don't view him as the enemy!

One comment that keeps popping up is, 'what if I can't take 50 degrees?' I just wanted to post this to show that cool, not cold is accepted for weightloss/metabolism boost.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-12T20:47:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14329>

I'm wondering how one measures RMR in the way Cronise mentions also....

### ealachan — 2012-04-12T20:48:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14330>

> **AKMan;14374 wrote:** Ray's motives are clearly fat-loss and not a cure-all for disease and longevity, but many around here are also just after fat-loss. As far as I know, Dr. K and Ray C are working together on some projects, so I definitely don't view him as the enemy!
>
>
>
> One comment that keeps popping up is, 'what if I can't take 50 degrees?' I just wanted to post this to show that cool, not cold is accepted for weightloss/metabolism boost.

Thanks very much for the post, AKMan - very interesting stuff. I'm currently in the fat-loss camp myself, too. Yes, I want to be healthy and live a long time, but I know that until I get this overload of fat off me that REALLY isn't going to happen, so fat loss takes precedence for me right now. I'll worry about "optimal" more when I'm not 150lbs overweight, know what I mean?

### Croak — 2012-04-12T21:04:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14336>

### ealachan — 2012-04-12T21:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14338>

> **Croak;14405 wrote:** Me, I'm a fat fucker who realizes that fat loss is a _symptom_ of getting healthy. Not the other way around. I'm in it for the long game, not swimsuit season.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:09:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14340>

> **PaleoMom;14398 wrote:** I'm wondering how one measures RMR in the way Cronise mentions also....

You'd need O2 and CO2 analyzers, ventilation measurement by a pnuemotach or turbine ventilometer (as used for VO2 measurement). There are also specific devices to measure metabolic rate such as the BodyGem.

Probably not for the average person to do...

### agatha — 2012-04-12T21:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14341>

It would be interesting to look at the indigenous traditions about this. Looking at the Russian stuff on YouTube they seem to do brief exposures in severely cold water. I like all of it- cool, cold, whatever..

### hellojtm — 2012-04-12T21:14:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14343>

Thanks for the clarification AKMan. It's a good post and I missed it in the free the animal thread because... it's out of control  I _think_ the advice to get chronic low levels of exposure to cold make sense, but the deeper inroads are going to have a bigger impact on gene expression and ultimately the results. I normally fall back on the idea that if we don't want the calories, i.e. we are doing cold exposure to waste calories, then we shouldn't eat them. But I understand that some folks have already eaten them and now want to waste them off as fast and as comfortably as possible. The mild exposure should be a good tool in that regard.

best,

jesse

I've been wanting to take Ray to task on the dietary blog posts for a while but haven't mustered the energy... I definitely do not consider him the enemy. I like his writing and what he has done and his intellect, but I just want him to get the evolutionary biology angle. I think that relying on modern science is somewhat fragile, i.e. pointing out that vegetables have proteins doesn't mean they are a good source of protein for a human. If all protein in all substances were good sources then we wouldn't have celiacs and undigested proteins making it into our intestines. He also flirts with what I consider dangerously low cholesterol levels because he's following conventional wisdom on that topic. I respect him though, he is specialized and hasn't had time to dig into these other topics as extensively as the cold exposure stuff. If he did then I would hope he could school me on it

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:29:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14355>
