# Thread: nothing succeeds like success
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-04-13T00:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-14457>

I went to the doc today for a follow-up on my foot. I have been CT'ing my foot, and the inflammation is way down. There is a touch of redness, but now I have defined borders, and can walk comfortably. I asked him about CT, and he agreed with my other (Ukrainian) doctor, CT full speed ahead. But, he was curious, why would I be reluctant to do CT? I told him others thought it was controversial. He said, "well, what's controversial? Look how much better your foot is." This morning I did my first completely freezing shower, with freezing morning water. It was a shock for a few seconds, then easy, and I didn't shiver at all. My hands and feet were still very cold at the Dr's office. Next time I go back to the doc, he wants me after a day on my feet, because after the shower, it looked pretty good.

### Birdy — 2012-04-13T00:57:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-14458>

I think what is going on with these docs, is that they are being graded on their success, so when they see one, they are very interested in hearing about it and applying it to other patients.

### MJ* — 2012-04-13T20:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-14805>

> **Birdy;14527 wrote:** I think what is going on with these docs, is that they are being graded on their success, so when they see one, they are very interested in hearing about it and applying it to other patients.

Birdy that's awesome!

### nonchalant — 2012-04-16T00:44:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15403>

> **Birdy;14527 wrote:** I think what is going on with these docs, is that they are being graded on their success, so when they see one, they are very interested in hearing about it and applying it to other patients.

Graded on their success?!??! Where the heck are you?

That's not what happens around here... Docs here are rewarded for prescribing medicines and selling hospital procedures.

Um, present company (Dr Kruse) excepted, of course.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-16T00:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15405>

Docs are getting ready to be paid for success. This is one reason i am looking for better answers for certain diseases that we have now

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-16T02:41:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15434>

> **Jack Kruse;15474 wrote:** Docs are getting ready to be paid for success. This is one reason i am looking for better answers for certain diseases that we have now

I keep shopping for a doc that might be open to stuff. This last week I got everyone in the fam an appointment with a family doc that I knew socially. We had done athletic/bootcampy stuff together and worked out at the same time/place for a long time. He too wasn't any thinner than the previous two years he'd been busting ass following his own advice. My thought was that since he too was stuck on the weight roller coaster, he might be open to at least listening to what we have been doing.

Boy was I wrong. It was the most depressing thing. Hubby went in for his private appt (probe anyone?) and he wouldn't order any of the tests I knew were needed to evaluate. Wouldn't order expanded cholesterol and when my husband told him what his previous labs were on that front - he got a big statin and "healthy whole grains" speech.

I am trying to figure out a way to assess them a little better before wasting everyone's time and money. Anybody got any ideas on how to filter a little?

Fwiw, I'm looking for someone in our insurance group - so the pay-only docs are going to be a last resort. If I'm paying

out of pocket, I'd rather pay Jack!

I've looked at the paleo/low carb referral sites - nothing useful. There was one in Ft. Collins north of here - but he died last year!

We pay a very large amount of $ for good insurance, I am not settling for mediocre - I'm going to wrestle this damn system to work with me somehow

What kind of questions, what kind of appointments are you making? Should I go in with a litany of ailments so I can get relevant tests?

### chocolate — 2012-04-16T03:02:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15444>

Cavemam, in CA, you can go to the pot clinics and get labs written up for you and get the results over the internet. Or the cash and carry clinics are pretty good. Insurance just seems to put a monkey wrench in everything. We learned in Canada to go to the heroin clinics for triage. It seemed to be the fastest and simplest, until we could get below the border. Somehow, it cut the red tape and it was so much faster then the emergency room. The doctors were styled like the patients, and very thrifty about razors. Very chill. lol. The patients were very chill also and you could play checkers and share smokes. Maybe a heroin clinic is in your network. Sort of a hybrid deal.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T14:14:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15538>

> **Jack Kruse;15474 wrote:** Docs are getting ready to be paid for success. This is one reason i am looking for better answers for certain diseases that we have now

What has changed to bring this about?

I believe you will have many successes with people who follow your advice. In just a short time I have no swelling and no pain in my ankles with following your methods. It's a miracle to me because I have had constant swelling and pain in my ankles for over 20 years.

I haven't, as of yet, followed 100 % of the LR, but I am starting to understand the importance of eating 30 min upon waking. The rest of the protocol is quite easy for me. I think giving up coffee will help me eat at the right time of the morn.

I am all for doctors striving for excellence in patient care --with no drugs that is.

### caroline — 2012-04-17T13:36:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15842>

I asked about bioidentical hormones and she said no one in the practice \[12docs\] knew much about that. She is a lovely woman and I think she would work with me - but I will have to come up with all the info and a plan to implement. She asked me how I knew this stuff! We really do have to be in charge of our own Ferrari don't we!

> **Cavemam;15503 wrote:** I keep shopping for a doc that might be open to stuff. This last week I got everyone in the fam an appointment with a family doc that I knew socially. We had done athletic/bootcampy stuff together and worked out at the same time/place for a long time. >
>
>
> Boy was I wrong. It was the most depressing thing. Hubby went in for his private appt (probe anyone?) and he wouldn't order any of the tests I knew were needed to evaluate. Wouldn't order expanded cholesterol and when my husband told him what his previous labs were on that front - he got a big statin and "healthy whole grains" speech.
>
>
>
> I am trying to figure out a way to assess them a little better before wasting everyone's time and money. Anybody got any ideas on how to filter a little?
>
>
>

>
> out of pocket, I'd rather pay Jack!
>
>
>
> I've looked at the paleo/low carb referral sites - nothing useful. There was one in Ft. Collins north of here - but he died last year!
>
>

>
>
>
> What kind of questions, what kind of appointments are you making? Should I go in with a litany of ailments so I can get relevant tests?

Cavemam that's so typical...I think you threatened his knowledge/authority and also it's such a litigious society we live in, most doctors are not going to easily go against the grain (no pun intended  ) I wish I could find the link for the Paleo docs it's helped me find one not too far from me...I am going to make my appointment soon, but I even worry that she may not be open to all of Dr. Kruse's ideas...it's going to be interesting, but I plan to bring some education with me, and I'm right now trying to make sense of all the tests that need taking with short explanations. Last night I went through the supplements post and I'm trying to boil that down today, then by the end of the week I hope to understand the labs a little better so I can organize that information so it's easier to convey. The thing is some of what Dr. Kruse recommends depends on particular factors, so it's not like you can just say...prescribe these tests or these supplements in XYZ dose like for example the prescription grade fish oils, depend on testing...it gets complicated...

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs-please.848/post-14465>

Did a few tests, so I'll include it all.

Vit B12: 990 pmol/L (150-700). 5 weeks ago it was 416 so I've increased it by taking sublingual B12.

Estradiol: 770 pmol/L, Day 13 (235-1300). I think this shows I'm better from last time, when it was 589 on day 28, way too much!

Free T4: 16.0 pmol/L (10-19)

TSH: 3.02 mIU/L (0.5-4.0)

Free T3: 4.4 pmol/L (3.5-6.5)

Even though I requested rT3, they managed to ignore it... Gah!

So how is my thyroid looking?

# Thread: Going Naked
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-04-13T01:09:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14466>

So, how many of you have changed your wardrobe? I am eying my sweater collection carefully, and frankly, the sweaters are getting nervous. OTOH, I have a new-found love for wool socks. I find I have been padding about with little clothing on, with the house at about 60-65 degrees, and I feel very comfortable all-over with wool socks. What changes have you made?

### AKMan — 2012-04-13T01:51:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14484>

We have a wood-burning stove. The room it's in has to be pretty hot for it to heat the rest of the house. So, as a compromise, I generally just wear a pair of shorts and no shirt or socks. My feet get pretty cold as the floor temp is usually around 50-60 degrees. I also walk around outside in the snow and cold barefoot every evening for a while.

### taryn.ourso@gmail.com — 2012-04-13T01:57:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14488>

I live in Texas, so definitely no more long sleeves for a very long time!! At work I have to wear pants, but as soon as I get home I put on a sleeveless T shirt and that is IT! I just parade around the house in my underwear. This also works wonders for my relationship! I sleep with no covers, windows open, and no A/C since its been so nice out. I don't know WHAT I'm gonna do when the temps get over 100 lol...

### ealachan — 2012-04-13T04:32:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14544>

I don't wear a coat outside anymore (but I'll wear a hat and gloves if it's really cold out), I dub around the house in a tank top or tshirt most of the time even though I keep the thermostat down around 62, and much to my husband's chagrin I've started opening the window at night when I go to bed...even when it's like 30 degrees out.

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T04:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14552>

At work I have to wear pants, but as soon as I get home I put on a sleeveless T shirt and that is IT! I just parade around the house in my underwear. This also works wonders for my relationship! I sleep with no covers, windows open, and no A/C since its been so nice out. I don't know WHAT I'm gonna do when the temps get over 100 lol...

I am from Texas also, guess a lot of cold baths with ice in them. Oh well.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-13T04:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14553>

been naked for 6.5 yrs.....welcome to the naked club

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-13T05:03:35+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-13T05:05:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14560>

The wine is gone and I am right now in the man room......naked listening to pandora heading out to the pool. I take no prisoners and give no quarter. Just finishing an optimal bulletproof coffee though. Have two big bio hacks to do in AM.

### agatha — 2012-04-13T22:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14855>

I had to have an emergency dash to the shops a couple of weeks ago when I realised all my work clothes are too, too hot.

### Birdy — 2012-04-13T23:25:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-14866>

I am finding that many things are too heavy, but I really like the wool socks. I think it is weird for me to be developing a dislike for cotton. It seems to clammy in the cold, and almost drippy when I warm myself up. I prefer thin polyester/nylon or even rayon in the winter.

### jsrhoades — 2012-04-15T03:14:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-naked.849/post-15187>

I get some strange looks from the morning walkers when doing circuits of the beautiful lake in my neighborhood. I'm wearing gym shorts, running shoes, and an iPod. It's 45-50 deg F. I'm wishing it were a bit cooler, but others are wearing sweaters, coats, and sometimes hats and gloves. The dogs are smarter. I took a pic of a dog swimming in the lake, chasing the Canadian geese and ducks. He was having a ball. He was in the water for at least of my two circuits, about 30-40 minutes.

# Thread: Sleeping on the floor/hard surface - Better for recovery?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FF_BC — 2012-04-13T14:08:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-14651>

This is a thing I've been thinking about for quite a long time...

When sleeping in a bed post exercise I wake up with much more muscle soreness than sleeping on the floor, with only a really thin madress between me and the floor.

I discovered this while having back problems. When sleeping in a bed my back problems got worse, then I decided to sleep at the floor and the back problems dissappeared. When sleeping in my bed again for a couple of weeks the backache came back. Despite having backache I worked out and that's how I discovered that when sleeping on the floor I had less muscle soreness.

**_My Hypothesis is:

When sleeping in a "soft" bed (the softness is relative to your weight and body constitution of course), the soft bed squeezes at your body, and limits bloodflow. This means that your muscles are not getting all the bloodflow it would have if sleeping on a "non constrictive" surface. Also due to the increased pressure on to your body, your heart must work harder to pump the blood. The increased heartrate may be interfering with your sleep and recovery.

I have experienced that sleeping on a hard surface is causing another type of soreness_, a more pleasant type of soreness. **I feel that my muscles are less stiff in the morning, more ready to work.**

When sleeping on a hard surface you will change your position more often and this can cause disturbance in your sleep, however you will get "Hard surface adapted"**after a while I believe that the more frequent changes in position is a good thing, since you limit the time a certain part of the body are under pressure.

The same as sitting or standing in the same position for a long time can be bad, sleeping in the same position might be bad. A soft bed, a soft chair, shoes with thick soles are deafening our body to potential hazards.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-15T02:56:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-15181>

I think this article will answer your questions. I've tried this myself but gave up when I was finding so many spiders in my room before bed After going back to the mattress I felt unmotivated to try again. However, I am a side sleeper and seem to have bonier joints than most people. My shoulders and hips always hurt from it, perhaps things might be better with CT though.

[http://www.zafu.net/sleepergonomics.html](http://http://www.zafu.net/sleepergonomics.html)

### FF_BC — 2012-04-15T09:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-15260>

Thank you for the link!  In Sweden the spiders are quite few, the "problem" before was that you get a little cold sleeping on the floor. But since starting CT that's not a problem  Right now I am sleeping on the floor under an open window (30-40F outside), with only a sleeping sheet.

Awesome link!

### wyldeman — 2012-04-16T06:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-15510>

The author of that link and his business is right near me ! I actually have slept on one of their kapok filled thin futons for the past few years. I love it !

I didn't want to believe it, but when I payed attention I found I actually sleep better on a harder surface...then i found that article and the company (which just happened to be local).

# Thread: How big did you go?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-big-did-you-go.856/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-13T15:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-big-did-you-go.856/post-14660>

On your BAB I mean

I'm not really doing this for weight issues although I do gain if my carbs go > 30-40 gm/day. For the most part I'm at a comfortable weight so I'm surprised that I would need to eat so much, but then again I've always had a HUGE appetite and could easily out-eat my 6'3 DH (although he's a skinny thing!).

I was eating right around 50-55 gm protein with my BAB for weeks, but feeling like I could always eat more. The last few days I upped it to 60-65 gm and still feel like I could eat more. I've been doing the reset for 11 weeks now and I still need to eat all 3 meals too. I eat very good quality proteins too - my BAB is usually a combination of grass-fed beef (a fatty cut like chuck roast) and shrimp/scallops/wild salmon, etc.

Just curious how high some of you have gone with your protein?

### ginastarke — 2012-04-13T17:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-big-did-you-go.856/post-14721>

I'm kind of embarrassed about it. This morning's BAB was an entire Salmon fillet with green beans, But I also eat big steaks and lamb chops. I just realized that 50 grams of protein are a 6oz steak.Oops. I'm going to start cutting down on the portions.

# Thread: The Blood Type Diet.....Paleo Style!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-blood-type-diet-paleo-style.857/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Momma28 — 2012-04-13T16:03:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-blood-type-diet-paleo-style.857/post-14671>

Anyone doing the Blood Type Diet? I have been on it now for nearly 6 months and only backed my way into it by noticing how my face looked in the mornings. I am an A positive and we don't do well on red meat, dairy or grains. When I removed these....WOW! I looked ten years younger. Amazing! So I started eating only the foods for my blood type and have consistently gotten younger in appearance. I'll be fifty soon, so every year I can reverse makes me very happy considering I still have 6 children at home, the youngest two being 4 and 18 months. I don't want to look like their grandmother.

I'm now experimenting with chicken, fish and eggs to see how these affect me. Is anyone doing the Leptin reset vegan style? Blood type A is the only one that performs best on a vegetarian diet and I'm seeing it.

Christina

### janagram — 2012-04-13T19:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-blood-type-diet-paleo-style.857/post-14753>

I'm a longtime blood type dieter! D'Adamo is great! if he and Kruse got together....it would be so intersting!

Before the bloodtype diet, I was a vegetarian (not vegan!) for 25 yrs and nearly did myself in, ( I'm an O), before waking up to reality!!! My body was crying out for meat!! I am so much healthier on Paleo eating! which is great for bloodtype O! I believe type A is good with grains, but I can see ditching them, even as an A. So, go paleo, type A style, lots of fish and chicken. And eggs!

### chocolate — 2012-04-15T17:39:22+0300

I am a+ and everything worked for me. I just didn't have a swamp to spear carp from nor anywhere to brouse berries. The goat and lamb is a little tough at times to get your hands on as well. He was really concerned about how inconvenient it would be for me to get the nutrition I needed from red meat. He told me to wait a month and try a small portion of organic beef, I did and was fine. For years though, I just used that deck of card sized rule. The grain fed beef gave me the clue on the paleo. If I can't eat grain, I shouldn't eat something that eats grain. I eat small grain fed portions and large grass fed. feeling much better.

But, alas, my book is lent out the gal doesn't have the book with her. **I was wondering if any of you remember what it was about drinking alcohol that improved the a+ digestion? There is supposed to be something genetic and I was hoping to dig for it over the weekend.**

The tempeh was full of garbage and the grain allotment was so minimal it was tough to stock. Plus the tempeh is more expensive than really great meat. For it to be pallatable, it has to be full of junk. The soy makes your gazongas so huge, you can't haul them. (I guess some people do the soy for enhancement) I just know everything it told me to avoid, worked. My dad had food allergies and the stuff he was allergic to was excluded on my A+diet. ### Momma28 — 2012-04-18T18:03:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-blood-type-diet-paleo-style.857/post-16222>

> **Shijin13;14827 wrote:** 4yrs later I eliminated wheat/gluten - to begin my path toward health - but I didn't begin to feel like I didn't have anything wrong w/m until I went primal/paleo - and finally ditched all the grains, and sugar, and added fat and high quality meat back into my diet.
>
>
>
> If it works for you - great that's your N=1; my N=1 on this diet = BS

I have been gluten-free for nearly five years now and as an A+, this is a match. I came to the Blood Type Diet through the back door after keeping a journal of my responses to the foods I was eating. I have been organic for nearly 20 years now and have managed a local organics buying club for more than ten years. I have eaten grassfed beef for more than 10 years and pastured, locally grown chickens and eggs as well. We get local produce, raw honey, raw dairy, both goat and cow(no longer consuming)....you name it, I've done it.

The biggest jump in our health as a family, (Hubby is A+ as well which makes all 8 kids A+) was when we optimized our vitamin D levels and eliminated wheat and casein from our diet. We do not submit to being injected with foreign substances and drink purified water. The kids play outside daily, without shoes and we don't use antibacterial anything. The only illness we deal with is a cold or two per year. More than half of the kids have never been given, or needed, thank God, antibiotics. Hubby is 49 and consistently tests out as an 18 year old on his yearly physical. So, I have an enormous body of evidence that what we are doing works, I just needed to tweak what I was eating of late as I am nearly 50, too.

Age is catching up with my physical body, though I am told my outward looks 10 to 15 years younger. So I only looked at the Blood Type Diet when I began to notice how my face looked in the mirror the morning after consuming the grass-fed beef or dairy of any kind that I might have had out and about. I feel great on a meatless diet, but am wondering if that would just be a short term benefit. I am currently abstaining from red meat and dairy. Aside from the extra wrinkles and puffiness under eyes, I get bad body odor from it as well. It clearly doesn't agree with me chemically/metabolically. The whole family eats paleo style with an occasional rice pasta for an Italian dish. The kids will eat anything placed in front of them and they get two meals a day, with a fruit snack and all have optimal weight for the age and build.

My goal then is to marry the paleo with the blood type to optimize my health and also control blood sugar levels as my anti-aging strategy. I have slowly started adding in CT but very sloooowly. Oooooh, it's so cold.

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T22:48:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-blood-type-diet-paleo-style.857/post-17877>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Effects\_of\_high\_altitude\_on\_humans](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Effects_of_high_altitude_on_humans)

**The effects of high altitude on humans are considerable. The percentage saturation of hemoglobin with oxygen determines the content of oxygen in our blood. After the human body reaches around 2,100 m (7,000 feet) above sea level, the saturation of oxyhemoglobin begins to plummet.\[1\] However, the human body has both short-term and long-term adaptations to altitude that allow it to partially compensate for the lack of oxygen.** Athletes use these adaptations to help their performance. There is a limit to the level of adaptation: mountaineers refer to the altitudes above 8,000 metres (26,000 ft) as the "death zone", where no human body can acclimatize.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-appendix-for-anyway-my-granny-had-some-shot-in-hers.858/post-14673>

I heard years ago that the appendix was for storing seeds and nuts and they found some in a frozen guys appendix or something. That sounds like the seeds and nuts we aren't supposed to eat because of the phytates.

[http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/human-biology/appendix.htm](http://http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/human-biology/appendix.htm)

[http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/vestiges/appendix.html](http://http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/vestiges/appendix.html)

It is supposed to have its own little digestive enzymes and maybe that it what resveratrol gets its fasting fake out from. I have a friend that ferments grain and I didn't follow up. Now I am wondering about those in a calorie restricted state. I know the biggest disappoint with goats, along with them being gourmet grass eaters, they only eat weed seeds, in the fall when the grass is dry and sweet. They are kind enough to reseed with them. They must get something out of them.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1407343/pdf/annsurg00970-0063.pdf](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1407343/pdf/annsurg00970-0063.pdf) This is an old study that looks at objects found in the appendix and it seems to be just random heavy foreign objects. Not seeds. Shot and pins.

# Thread: CT affecting anyone's menstrual cycle?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Holly — 2012-04-13T16:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-14689>

I have been CTing about 3 months now. It has been great for me as far as my long-standing sleep issues. No detox symptoms that I am aware of other than my periods have become very irregular. Years ago, after having kids, my cycles shortened to about every 21 days. For a while, CT seemed to have lengthened my cycles to about every 30 days just as when I was younger. However, this month, I am still awaiting my cycle, its been about 5 weeks. I am 48. I wonder if CT is hastening the onset of menopause. Hopefully not. I would like to think that CT has elevated my levels of estrogen as its being liberated from my fat cells (though I really haven't noticed visible fat loss, maybe even more abdominal bloat) and my body therefore hasn't sensed the cyclical "drop" in estrogen that would trigger a period? Is similar happening to anyone else? Thoughts? I like my sleep. But I also like my hormones...

### jenn — 2012-04-14T03:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-14964>

Hi Holly,

About a month after starting CT, I got my period....then, eleven days later, I got it again! This time with more cramping and clotting. I am 45 and this is really the first time I've been irregular to any great degree. There are definitely some hormone changes going on! Also, like you, I've yet to see either weight or fat loss....coming up on 2 months of CT now. I'm hoping the chaos is temporary, but it could also be the beginning of the long road to menopause....

>
>

Same for me. Slow loser, but down a pants size kind of thing. TOM all over the place! Of course I am 43 and overweight so it's been suggested it's the pre-pause stuff. pooh. I feel too young for this!

### Glamorama — 2012-04-14T12:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15021>

My cycle went from 30 to 35 days with CT. I'm 31. I put it down to high levels of estrogen being released from fatty tissue, screwing up my cycles.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-14T17:06:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15055>

So far mine has gone from perfectly regular (has always been), coming with the shedding of the full moon (this started with my circadian work a few months ago), and 4-5 days long (used to be longer and heavier pre-primal) to the same, but only TWO days long, both med-heavy, no light days leading up and no super heavy on the end.

LOVING IT.

I think the experiences y'all are describing are likely from detox'ing estrogen.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-14T17:19:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15058>

I'm definitely seeing changes. After going from January - October of last year without a cycle, I finally started getting more regular only to have it all change again after beginning CT and the leptin rx. I'm now on day 50 with no sign of my TOM starting  I had hoped to do another hormone panel, so I'm anxiously awaiting its return so I can do it on the proper day of my cycle.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-14T20:27:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15098>

The first period I got after starting CT came on day 23 of my cycle. This was after months of 33/34 day cycles, so that was quite a shift. The following cycle was 25 days long with nearly no cramps during my period (a huge deal for me!!) and only 4 days of bleeding.

I just started my period (third since CT) this morning on day 27 (no cramps so far), so things seem to be recalibrating toward normal.

Another note: usually my period starts out with brown "old" looking blood - almost like it was left over from the previous one- and a sparse flow for the first few hours, but the last two have been red and free-flowing from the start, though not heavy or anything.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-04-14T22:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15126>

bouncing around as well, went from 35+ days to 23 this cycle, I have a cycle that heavyish at first for 3-4 days then seems to stop for a day and then starts again for a couple days (but light)

Dr Lam\`s site says the start-stop-start is a typical AF cycle for low cortisol but the lengthening to over 35 days last time was new for me, this 23 is more usual

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-15T17:48:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15306>

My cycle has been between 25 to 28 days. I started CT in March and have had 14 cold showers/baths so far. Last month TOM started on day 28. This one started today, actually surprised me on day 26 because I felt no cramps at all. Usually I would know the day before TOM arrives since I would start to have minor cramps and bad mood. This time, I only had some cravings for food yesterday, nothing else. I like this change.

### ealachan — 2012-04-15T18:08:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15311>

I was a few days late this month, but I attributed it to mis-counting. Will be interesting to see if it's different / late again this month. I've always been pretty much like clockwork - 28-30 days, with pretty marked PMS symptoms (moodiness, pronounced breast soreness) in the week leading up to my period. I'm 32 and very obese, FWIW. I've only NOT had a period twice - I once skipped a month when I was at my heaviest (400+ lbs) and once skipped a month during a time of extreme stress a long time ago.

I've also noticed in the last year or so that my PAC's (heart palpitations) seem to ramp up in frequency the week or so before I get my period. I mentioned it to my doctor, who said it was "interesting" but that she'd never heard of it before.

# Thread: Pardus' journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pardus — 2012-04-13T16:53:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-14712>

Hi all, welcome to my journal.

21 yo male living in Alberta, Canada.

5'8" 159 lbs.

Strict keto paleo: 20 months

CT: 2 months

Leaky gut Rx + Leptin Rx: 4 months

Problems:

\- leaky gut

\- jelly belly

\- dry skin

\- eczema

\- seborrheic dermatitis

\- brain fog

\- chronic bloating

\- allergies to mould, dust, pollen, etc.

\- tendonitis/tendonosis

\- cold hands (suspect hypothyroidism)

\- morning fatigue + difficulty falling asleep at night (suspect adrenal fatigue)

\- easily stressed out

History/past:

\- given anti-biotics multiple times throughout life due to pneumonia

\- SAD diet: instant noodles, canned food, TV microwave dinners, fast food, lots of soda

\- experimented with vegetarian for 4 months, vegan diet for another 4 months

\- chronic cardio maniac

\- chronic night owl

\- harsh chemical creams and steroids used for persistent eczema (since birth)

\- delivered by C-section

\- not breast-fed, only soy formula

Current supplements:

NAC

iodine

selenium

magnesium

zinc

MSM

coQ10

PQQ

K2 MK-4

D3

B complex

Quercetin

Curcumin

Resveratrol

Betaine HCL

Bromelain

Diet:

Fish

Grassfed beef + lamb

green vegetables

grassfed beef liver 1-2 times per week

No eggs, dairy, nuts, grains, legumes of any kind (no green beans or peas either), no fruits, no starches, no nightshades.

Low oxalate, low histamine foods.

2 meals per day. Breakfast at 6:00am, Dinner around 4:00-5:00 pm.

Lifestyle:

HIT 15 mins once per week, in the afternoon (Body By Science, Doug McGuff)

CT during day: cold walk/cold shower

CT at night, 2 hrs before bed: cold showers

Blueblocking glasses go on at sunset. In bed by 9:30pm, asleep by around 10:00pm, and up by 6:00am

### Pardus — 2012-04-13T17:33:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-14730>

Update:

Finally starting to see a little progress.

I have definitely learned to appreciate the importance of getting enough sleep. I always notice on days where I'm sleep deprived that my cold tolerance is terrible.

When I get more sleep, my skin seems to heal a bit better by next morning (as long as I don't scratch while I'm asleep).

My hands/feet are still mostly cold, only warming up for about 1 hr after eating. My cold shower has increased to 20 mins @ 40F (5C) (thankfully, it's still snowing up here in Alberta). Although I feel like I could go longer by the end of the 20 minutes.

In the beginning I absolutely dreaded the cold showers! The first 2-3 minutes are the worst!

However, over time I found that there are various tricks you can use to make the entry more bearable.

1\. I think by far the easiest way is to start the shower at just a tiny bit colder than skin temp, then drop the temp every minute (usually takes me about 3-5 increments), then start your stop watch and drop it down all the way cold.

2\. The second best way (if you don't have time to spare with gradual decrease in temp) is to hold your breath and stick your face right under the cold full blast. This activates the dive reflex. Your face will feel cold, but your body will feel less cold comparitively because there are more nerves on your face. Hold your face under the stream for as long as you can hold your breath. By the time you need to take a breath, you should be fine.

As for my leaky gut:

My skin looks a lot better since the past month or so. I would say there's about 70% remission in my eczema so far. The major factor in this was the fact that I addressed my chronic constipation.

I think there's a major problem with how I used to approach leaky gut. I would focus so much on healing the intestinal brush border with glutamine, coconut oil, bone broths, NAC, anti-fungals, candida-killers, bio-film breakers, nutrient rich foods, etc... The problem is, no matter how much of these "healing" agents I threw at it, I would never see results. Recently, I've realized that no matter what I do, NOTHING will heal if the whole thing is plugged up with crap (literally!).

I used high dose magnesium to ensure that I have a bowel movement between 1-2 times a day. I have gone regularly 1-2 times a day for the past month, and I feel fantastic! Just making sure to have a bowel movement every day has helped my leaky gut more than all the glutamine, bone broths, NAC, etc that I used for months and months.

Suggestions and comments are appreciated.

Thanks for reading.

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T21:20:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-14818>

My suggestion is take your time on the cold showers and don't push it. You eventually will get to where you like cold showers.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-14T00:48:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-14902>

I agree, Magnesium is great for bm's, plus muscle and nerve relaxation.

I guess you use orange lights at night? Or turn everything off after sundown? Amazing how that makes one sleepy so fast.

Hope you are mending your night-owl ways.

Oh, I see you are. Great!

### Pardus — 2012-04-23T22:48:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-18169>

Update:

I have been trying out topical vitamin C on the skin. I've read that it's used sometimes as a treatment for burns. I've done 3 or so treatments, spaced a few days apart. I can't tell if my skin has just been healing naturally or if it's an effect of the vitamin C, but it definitely looks and feels better. There are only a few active lesions remaining on my body, most of them have cleared up. In the past I have also had success with apple cider vinegar for eczema, but it always came back after I stopped using it. Apparently vitamin C stays in the skin for up to 72 hours after application, so I might be able to get away with less frequent treatments this way.

One thing about eczema is that the itch-scratch-itch cycle can sometimes leave me in a scratching frenzy. It's like I lose control and go into a trance while scratching. One thing I've found that really stops a scratching frenzy is cold. Cold is really great for calming my perpetual itching.

In my neolithic days I would sometimes ice areas of eczema on my body in a desperate attempt to get some relief so I could fall asleep. It really was more of a last ditch effort, I never got the idea to use it consistently. Nowadays, I run itchy skin under 40F water until it goes numb. If the itch returns after the skin warms up, I repeat the process, but usually cold makes it go away for a good hour or so.

I took some progress pics. I do not have any pics of when I was at my worst: I didn't have the heart to take them. It was really that bad. I couldn't even bear to look in a mirror. At the time, I thought I would never get better, so I didn't think to take pics because I wasn't planning on auditioning as an extra in the latest zombie movie anytime soon.

I will not post the progress pics yet. When I am optimal (however long that takes) I will post my final pics and progress pics here and you will be able to see the difference in how far I've come along.

It's finally starting to warm up, so I can't take cold walks anymore. I also have to settle for slightly warmer showers and longer times. My hands feel warmer a little longer after eating (maybe an hour and a half now instead of an hour), but it might be because the ambient temp is generally warmer now. Still, I'm hopeful that the thyroid might finally be starting to respond to CT.

Thanks for reading.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-24T01:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-18198>

+2 on the magnesium and the importance of bm's. I clog up easily and THAT has caused major issues with my liver which i thought I'd fixed by doing a liver cleanse as prescribed by a naturopath. I did feel a little better but taking lots of mg on a nightly basis has brought the most improvement.

Welcome, btw!

### Katiejem — 2012-04-26T06:58:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-18963>

How much mag do you take? I am taking 400 at night and it's not doing the trick...

Sent from my iPad using Tapatalk HD

### Pardus — 2012-04-30T00:23:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-19943>

Hi Katie,

Magnesium dosage depends on the individual. You may consider increasing the dosage 100 mg at a time until you become regular.

For more severe cases of irregularity, you may need to accept a period of higher dosage and uncomfortably loose stools in order to clear out the pipes initially, in order to start with a clean slate.

### TerrierMom — 2012-05-17T08:38:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pardus-journal.860/post-24833>

I took 800 mg mag maleate 2 hrs before bed..... And if that does not do the trick I add another 400 in the am with my BAB and other supplements. The only thing I take at night is the mag, and let's just say if I forget, well...... I am reminded in a hurry that I need it. So I am religious about remembering.

# Thread: Hibernating peasants
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### maggie — 2012-04-13T17:20:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/post-14725>

This morning I just remembered this article, which I read a few years ago, about how European peasants would spend the winter. I found it again today in the NY Times. This also made me think of Jane Smiley's wonderful and endless saga The Greenlanders--she also described how the Europeans would take to their beds during the long winter. Unfortunately for them, global cooling finished the settlement off because they were too narrow minded to learn from their Inuit neighbors, whom they believed were devils.

Anyway, back to The Big Sleep, from the NY Times. Hope you enjoy this, as I did:

[http://www.nytimes.com/2007/11/25/opinion/25robb.html?\_r=1&scp=1&sq=graham+robb&st=nyt](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2007/11/25/opinion/25robb.html?_r=1&scp=1&sq=graham+robb&st=nyt)

Economists and bureaucrats who ventured out into the countryside after the Revolution were horrified to find that the work force disappeared between fall and spring. The fields were deserted from Flanders to Provence. Villages and even small towns were silent, with barely a column of smoke to reveal a human presence. As soon as the weather turned cold, people all over France shut themselves away and practiced the forgotten art of doing nothing at all for months on end.In the mountains, the tradition of seasonal sloth was ancient and pervasive. â€œSeven months of winter, five months of hell,â€￾ they said in the Alps. When the â€œhellâ€￾ of unremitting toil was over, the human beings settled in with their cows and pigs. They lowered their metabolic rate to prevent hunger from exhausting supplies. If someone died during the seven months of winter, the corpse was stored on the roof under a blanket of snow until spring thawed the ground, allowing a grave to be dug and a priest to reach the village.

The same mass dormancy was practiced in other chilly parts. In 1900, The British Medical Journal reported that peasants of the Pskov region in northwestern Russia â€œadopt the economical expedientâ€￾ of spending one-half of the year in sleep: â€œAt the first fall of snow the whole family gathers round the stove, lies down, ceases to wrestle with the problems of human existence, and quietly goes to sleep. Once a day every one wakes up to eat a piece of hard bread. ... The members of the family take it in turn to watch and keep the fire alight. After six months of this reposeful existence the family wakes up, shakes itselfâ€￾ and â€œgoes out to see if the grass is growing.â€￾

It is unlikely this was hibernation in the zoological sense... \[Well OK, but maybe close enough for government work...\]

### Jane — 2012-04-13T20:42:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/post-14792>

Maggie,

Fascinating! Oh to be self-sufficient enough to hibernate through the winter without personal economic burdens. I bet the majority of babies were born in the Summer.  Thanks for sharing this. I also find it interesting that in the hotter climates people traditionally closed everything down and slept, taking a siesta during the heat of the blazing afternoons.

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T21:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/post-14810>

Man, those were the good ole days  Couldn't do that today, got work and bills to pay, rats.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-14T05:36:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/post-14999>

> â€œQ: Are there any reported side effects associated with supplemental Diindolylmethane DIM?â€￾
>
>
>
> â€œSome people taking Diindolylmethane DIM have noticed a darkening of their urine to a brownish color. This has been seen when Diindolylmethane DIM is taken by someone who drinks little water throughout the day. This is a normal, harmless occurrence and is not associated with any abnormalities in kidney function or health status.â€￾

# Thread: CT-Bikram Hack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mott — 2012-04-13T20:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14780>

OK...60 straight days of CT..I decided to re-enter the Bikram studio (mind you..down 12 lbs from ct + keto). I wanted to see if my CT adaptation affected my ability to tolerate heat. For those not aware of Bikram Yoga, it is done in a studio that is heated 10 110 degress...90 minutes..and it is very strenuous. It works on the principal of compression and release. A pose is held for a specific time, usually restricting blood flow to a certain body area and then the pose is release. Heat acts as both an enabler to drill deeper into the pose and also provides for a detox and cardio vascular work.

I found absolutely no adverse tolerance of heat since beginning my CT protocol. In fact, I felt stronger. I perspired per usual but it seemed less so than when I was not CT adapted. My breathing stayed calm and I was able to pick up my practice after a sixty day absence.

I had always wondered why people who practiced Bikram rarely changed their body composition. Most never lose the weight they seek to and many seem to gain. I believe that it is because the prolonged heat exposure depletes glucogen stores rapidly...Although, I am no scientist, I believe the keto CT protocol is the way for me. I will practice Bikram 2x per week because it does help my spine stay strong and it helps detox...but never again without frequent CT.

Does this qualify for a hack...geeks, please explain to me what is going on here. Thanks

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T20:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14785>

### AKMan — 2012-04-13T23:49:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14872>

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T00:28:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14887>

I've done Bikram before, but since learning all this not at all. My question is this: what is the advantage of Bikram over, say, the same yoga routine done in cold?

### Mott — 2012-04-14T04:45:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14992>

I would actually appreciate it if Dr. K would weigh in on benefits/negatives of exposure to heat for prolonged time. I know the benefits of heat from my practice: spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing, joint and muscle stimulation. The heat allows one to hold and maintain postures. I want to test a daily practice of CT + keto plus 2 sessions of Bikram per week. I don't think it's an all or nothing proposition and I find benefits with both.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T05:16:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14996>

I know the benefits of heat from my practice: spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing, joint and muscle stimulation. The heat allows one to hold and maintain postures. I want to test a daily practice of CT + keto plus 2 sessions of Bikram per week. I don't think it's an all or nothing proposition and I find benefits with both.

I'd be interested to know too (and keen to hear about your experiment).

I'm really just trying to question the thinking around what heat does and see if it holds up to scrutiny. Not sure.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-14T12:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15022>

> **Mott;15061 wrote:** I would actually appreciate it if Dr. K would weigh in on benefits/negatives of exposure to heat for prolonged time. I know the benefits of heat from my practice: spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing, joint and muscle stimulation. The heat allows one to hold and maintain postures. I want to test a daily practice of CT + keto plus 2 sessions of Bikram per week. I don't think it's an all or nothing proposition and I find benefits with both.

besides dehydration the negatives to any type of yoga is it is limiting. Once you adapt to being able to support your body weight, it becomes a balance issue. Once you get the balance down the positive affects of muscle stimulation diminish with each class. That is why you never see anyone really change after they adapt to the exercises. As your brain and muscles adapt to the stress put upon it from the exercises, you require less and less neural and muscular stimulation to perform each pose. It basically gets easier and because you are only using body weight for resistance, you limit the amount of stimulation once you adapt. That is why you NEVER see a muscular yoga practitioner unless they are strength training outside of yoga classes. It all becomes an endurance event and they develop thin, [type I muscle fibers](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skeletal_striated_muscle), great for holding poses, very oxidative and sucks for lifting heavy things.

### Mott — 2012-04-14T14:36:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15029>

@ContinumFitness I respectfully disagree...After practicing Bikram for 5 years, I can attest through actual experience that adaptation is not straightforward and performance rarely predictable. It has been one of the few modes of training where one cannot be certain of one's performance by going in. I say this after having lifted weights Olympic-style since the 70s and actually trained and competed in Olympic amateur competitions. For example, when in training, I could approach my best lifts in c&J and snatch in training. But in Bikram it's a totally different experience. Cross Fit is a derivation of Olympic style lifting techniques and methods. I apologize for turning this discussion sideways. The main point I am interested in is longevity, true fitness and ct as a means to help achieve it.

### shah78 — 2012-04-14T14:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15030>

I was surprised to find that Yoga/stretching was quite enjoyable right after the cold tub. I then extended the experiment to ice packs before my Neuromuscular therapy sessions. Very good results there, too. Add that to the pre-lifting ice down and the picture is complete. Ice before everything. And I include sex as well. Paridigm shift to the max. What a mind \*\*\*\*!Everything I thought was right before was wrong. Very entertaining six weeks.

### Mott — 2012-04-14T14:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15031>

Put another way: when I was young I was obsessed with weight on the bar..the more weight I put on the bar and lifted over my head I felt a rare feeling of total and complete "aliveness" Now that I am older (approaching 50) I recognize that I am the bar..and I need to take weight off so that I can live to fight another day...Now, as a side bar, our best training routines in Oly lifting came from the Russians..Isn't it strange and wonderful that the Ruskies know a thing or two about CT and Dr. K has referenced this...That's why they call it Physical Culture...Happy Training, as my coach from Vladivostock would say....

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-14T20:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15091>

> **Mott;15098 wrote:** @ContinumFitness I respectfully disagree...After practicing Bikram for 5 years, I can attest through actual experience that adaptation is not straightforward and performance rarely predictable. It has been one of the few modes of training where one cannot be certain of one's performance by going in. I say this after having lifted weights Olympic-style since the 70s and actually trained and competed in Olympic amateur competitions. For example, when in training, I could approach my best lifts in c&J and snatch in training. But in Bikram it's a totally different experience. Cross Fit is a derivation of Olympic style lifting techniques and methods. I apologize for turning this discussion sideways. The main point I am interested in is longevity, true fitness and ct as a means to help achieve it.

Mott, you may disagree but the fact remains that you are limited by your body weight. Once you achieve enough strength to perform a position and hold your BW in place it becomes an aerobic (endurance) exercise. You will not get any hypertrophy of the muscle beyond its ability to support its own weight. At this point you are developing slow twitch muscle fibers which are great at aerobic, endurance exercise. Yes you can tweak the movements through hand or foot placement but you still are only supporting or moving your body weight. You will not gain any more strength or size if you do not push your muscles beyond their ability to support just your body weight.

Back to what you originally stated: people rarely change never lose or seem to gain weight. What I am saying is once a person gains enough muscle strength to practice yoga at an above avg level. They will maintain/lose muscle hypertrophy(size) and gain muscle endurance.

Ex. sprinters vs distance runners. burst of power promotes growth vs slow and steady promotes efficiency of the muscle.

Caveat: this is all in warm adapted people....

### Mott — 2012-04-16T01:51:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15417>

@Contium..points taken and accepte. I just finished my 2nd Bikram class of the week and then proceed to a 30 CT session..Water consistent at 45 degrees..Oberservations:

1\. My aerobic capacity while under heat exposure has INCREASED substantially while cold adapting. I don't know if it's the CT or keto diet...but I was able to maintain a controlled practice under very hot conditions (wall to wall studio full of people..high humidity...and room temp of 110. So many regular practitioner struggled today and I was simply flowing from one posture to another, controlled breath, high perspiration, but nothing like some of my fellow classmates.

2\. My CT session was just as challenging but I was able to push through it and get the 30 minutes in. I felt the very cold toward the end of it..but I am receiving a jolt of endorphin release 20 minutes after..it's runners high without the miles.

Could anyone please explain why my aerobic capacity has increased with CT and Keto during heat exposure? Thanks

You are ketogenic and your body is more efficient because you are not burning carbs. Your need for oxygen is greatly reduced.

### Mott — 2012-04-23T02:03:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-17927>

My cold adaptation continues to make me more tolerant of long exposure to heat. It is fascinating to experience this. My perspiration levels remains high while exposed to 110 degrees for 90 minutes of rigorous exercise but my respiration rate stays steady. I no longer feel an increased heart rate while doing some of the most challenging exercises and I now only practice once a week and CT 6x per week. I lift 2x per week..all olympic style but short...

### chocolate — 2012-04-13T20:25:36+0300

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw)

4.9. Flavonoid-like Agents

Flavonoids are natural plant polyphenols found in leaves, bark and flowers. Some 4,000 members

have been identified to date, all benzo-γ-pyran derivates comprised of phenolic and pyran rings. These

polyphenolic compounds are known to have anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antioxidant and

anticarcinogenic properties \[20,50,63\]. The flavonoids may also have ROS scavenging properties and

the ability to chelate metals at the active site of metalloenzymes. Flavonoids may have

hypopigmenting capabilities by directly inhibiting tyrosinase activity at distal portions of the

melanogenic pathway \[20\]. Structure-function analysis of flavonoids suggests that flavonoids with an

α-keto group show potent tyrosinase inhibition due to the similarity between the dihydroxyphenyl

group of DOPA and the α-keto containing flavonoids \[63\]. Similar analysis completed by Kubo et al.

suggests that flavonoids have the capability to chelate copper in tyrosinaseâ€™s active site as long as the

3-hydroxygroup is free \[64,68\]. A comprehensive review on the properties of plant polyphenols has

been published by Kim et al. \[63\] Flavonoids that will be reviewed here include aloesin,

hydroxystilbene derivates and licorice extracts.

4.9.1. Aloesin

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&The flaesrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw](<http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&The flaesrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw>)

**

Mechanisms regulating skin pigmentation, the rise and fall of melanin****

4.9. Flavonoid-like Agents

Flavonoids are natural plant polyphenols found in leaves, bark and flowers. Some 4,000 members

have been identified to date, all benzo-γ-pyran derivates comprised of phenolic and pyran rings. These

polyphenolic compounds are known to have anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antioxidant and

anticarcinogenic properties \[20,50,63\]. The flavonoids may also have ROS scavenging properties and

the ability to chelate metals at the active site of metalloenzymes.\[**B\] Flavonoids may have

hypopigmenting capabilities by directly inhibiting tyrosinase activity at distal portions of the

melanogenic pathway \[20\]. Structure-function analysis of flavonoids suggests that flavonoi\[/B\]ds with an

α-keto group show potent tyrosinase inhibition due to the similarity between the dihydroxyphenyl

group of DOPA and the α-keto containing flavonoids \[63\]. Similar analysis completed by Kubo et al.

suggests that flavonoids have the capability to chelate copper in tyrosinase’s active site as long as the

3-hydroxygroup is free \[64,68\]. A comprehensive review on the properties of plant polyphenols has

been published by Kim et al. \[63\] Flavonoids that will be reviewed here include aloesin,

hydroxystilbene derivates and licorice extracts.

this article breaks things down quite a lot. Things that I was afraid of, like licorice, I may now know how to toy around with.

# Thread: Martin's Story
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 531
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2012-04-13T21:49:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-14826>

I'm a male, 59, 138 lbs. and a retired (emphasis on "tired") old house restoration contractor.

I've had a long history of low-grade health problems since I can remember. Fevers, muscle aches (my Mom called them "growing pains"), cold-natured, lethargy, mental fogginess, coming alive as the day wore on, and always wanting to stay up late and then sleep late the next morning. I saw others play harder, learn faster, and have more friends than me and got picked on a little by others. Because they looked down on me for being "weak" and "stupid", I eventually blamed myself for just not being "good enough".

I had my moments of clarity and courage and went on to get married, help raise my stepson, and have a great career restoring old Craftsman bungalows in Atlanta for a number or years. It was rough work and it wore at my 140 lb. frame, but it was by sheer force of will and a love for those old houses that got me through. Joint pain and slow healing wounds, along with a lack of energy, drove me to do research on the net. It led me to look at my past history in a new way.

I remember my Mom talking about her sub-normal temps, aches, weakness, low energy level, and she was always struggling mentally. She started me on a path of natural healing that I continue to practice to this day.

I eat organically, fresh air, spring water, saunas, and relaxation strategies, and now, Paleo eating and eventually, Leptin Reset and Cold Thermogenesis.

I am through blaming myself and allowing others to blame me for being "weak minded", "not good enough", "it's all in your attitude", and other bull\*\*\*\*. There is a part of this that is spiritual, of course, and I'm addressing it. But there is a physical part of feeling lousy and that's why I am here. It's called epigenetics! I'm fixing the bio-mechanical machine that is our body through what I have learned here and other sites such as the Weston Price Foundation, the Doctor Whithin, Hair Analysis and Mineral Balancing - Dr. Larry Wilson, and others.

I am coming at the Reset, CT, et al, from a biotoxin illness place. Recently dx'ed with two genetic defects: mold exposure and low aMSH. These explains so much about my life.

I found Dr. Kruse's work through a chance question on a WPF forum and dove right in. I have always searched for a well-researched method of diagnosing imbalances and Kruse is the man. I feel so fortunate to learn from him and look forward to a life restored, just like my old houses.

Martin

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T21:56:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-14830>

Way to go Martin, welcome aboard.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-13T23:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-14867>

Welcome, Martin! I look forward to hearing about your journey to Optimal!

### janagram — 2012-04-14T20:36:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-15104>

I'll be checking here for more....

### Martin — 2012-04-15T15:33:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-15279>

Mold doc visit went very well. The four-month wait for the appointment was finally over.

I had read up on the treatment in "Mold Warriors" by Dr Ritchie Shoemaker and knew what to expect and more importantly, what I wanted to take away from the first visit - a script for cholestyramine.

This ancient cholesterol drug is not absorbed into the body. It just absorbs fatty stuff and, along with it, the biotoxins that have been circulating in my body for a long time creating an unchecked feedback loop of cytokine release and the resulting misery.

I feel as if I've had the flu for three years. Painful joints, headaches, vicious leg cramps, unrelenting fatigue, and a gathering hopelessness. Like Dr. Kruse said, chronic pain will change your life.

Doc took a nasal swab to test for a nasty infection that is sometimes present in us Chronic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (CIRS) sufferers. The tiny little Q-tip looked so small and innocuous until it was several inches back o' my nostril. Then it felt like a cotton-covered baseball bat. A quick dab or two and it was all over. At least no bending over this trip.

I learned from the doc that the VIP testing (vaso-intestinal peptide, a very important marker for mold illness) is currently going haywire among the testing labs out there and the test results cannot be relied upon. $75 wasted? It's all good. The way out is the way through. There are plenty of other tests to provide a path.

I had introduced the good doc to Dr. Kruse's work a few months ago and have mentioned how CT raises aMSH, another important (extremely!) hormone in everyone, let alone mold folks. Mine is

### freesia — 2012-04-16T00:43:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-15402>

Hi Martin,

Do you mean your saw Ritchie Shoemaker? Or another Mold doc who works with Dr Shoemaker's protocols?

I know a few people using CSM, they seem to think it's effect is potentiated by other measures, extreme mold avoidence in some cases or other detoxification support. I also think CT could work very well with CSM. I'm really interested to see how you do!

Did you get significant mold exposure while renovating? I guess with that genotype any exposure was going to make you sick?

I googled Atlanta craftsman bungalows because I was curious, and I love old houses. Nice! Not _too_ different from bungalows built here, in NZ, in the 20's.

Best to you!

### Martin — 2012-04-18T21:34:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-16318>

Hi freesia, I found a mold doc who follows Shoemaker's work. I started CSM two days ago and no changes yet. I am curious about using other clearing supplements like chlorella. Do you know if those people you mentioned used it?

I know I was exposed like crazy during the remodeling, but it wasn't anything I haven't been exposed to for 12 years. The house is not water-damaged, and we have a very dry basement, but the mold grew on the backsides of the rough-sawn pine interior boards that were on the wall. Humidity over the years and poor construction would let air thru from the outside and deposited mycotoxins on the inside. Ugh.

I theorize many would get respiratory issues if exposed, but would quickly clear once they left the area. But if you have haplotypes, watch out! I'm still wondering how I did as well as I did all the years up here. I actually got very healthy 3 years ago thru watching diet, etc., but then something really kicked my a\*\* - bad!!! I have not recovered from that episode and the search for healing brought me here. I thought it was Lyme's and that brought Shoemaker's work up, but I forgot it until Dr. Kruse referenced him. Bingo, I'd found my path thru that I could trust.

Got more blood tests back.

TGF-beta 10,820 pg/ml range: 344-2,382

VIP 42 pg/ml range: 0-58

ACTH 14 pg/ml range: 7.2-63.3

TGF-beta is in line with CIRS (chronic inflammatory response syndrome) range.

VIP is probably worthless due to current lab difficulties going on presently according to my doc in Atlanta.

ACTH ? not sure what to relate this to yet.

New Zealand! Always wanted to be a Kiwi, albeit an import!

### Martin — 2012-04-23T14:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-18028>

Ninth day in CSM and not doing well. More aching and discomfort than before. Constipation is a side affect of CSM and they ain't kiddin'. Doing steps to improve "flow".

Contacting mold doc to see if other tests are back yet and whether there is some strange thing going on.

I seem to respond very little to ANY therapy I've ever done thru my search for relief. Pills, tablets, tinctures, decoctions, all go down and nothing resembling a big change happens. I read success stories and about new (and old) therapies and get really good inspiration, but don't seem to fall in line with the healing crowd.

More testing will show me the path through this. The answer is there in my blood.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-24T01:32:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-18202>

Martin, have you eliminated all grains from your diet? I just did a quick rescan of your posts and didn't see anything. If you haven't (or even if you have) and haven't yet read Dr. William Davis's "Wheat Belly," I'd sincerely like to recommend it to you.

Good luck. I'll keep checking back to see how you're doing so don't leave. You have found a place with lots of good support.

### Martin — 2012-04-24T02:27:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-18216>

Hi Darleen,

All grains? Only brown rice sometimes mixed into my veg/beef soup. And sometimes rice crackers with sesame seeds on them (makes them palatable!) just to have some sort of CHANGE from beef, broccoli, beets, and bacon.

I read "Dangerous Grains" and got tested, but results were not high. I went off wheat, rye, oats, and corn anyway after reading Dr. Kruse.

No gluten for six months. I could tighten it up and eliminate ALL grains, though.

Thank you for the book suggestion. That's what great about this forum. People do seem to care and want to spread the knowledge and experiences.

Speaking of that, you sang in a rock band! COOL! You are inspiring me with your life story.

I want to play in a band before I die. Country prolly, and sing harmony behind my quite talented wife. Too fatigued right now. Haven't even picked up my guitar in months. Time is slipping...

### Martin — 2012-04-25T19:06:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-18765>

I got some good news from my testing. No Marcons! It's a nasty nasal infection that constantly triggers cytokine releasing. You may not even have a runny nose!

And so far, I am not having to take Actos, an effective, but sometimes risky drug (when taken long-term) that does a lot of good when used off-label to treat moldy, lymie people.

Eating lots of prunes these days.

### Martin — 2012-04-28T17:29:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-19575>

I spent part of the day yesterday in the garden finally planting some raspberry and asparagus crowns I ordered. I almost decided not to get them, but if you don't act on it when spring comes, you waste an entire year. It's not like a tupperware bowl you can just go out and buy anytime you want one. Timing is everything.

Having said that, I left the poor things imprisoned in their little shipping box for over a week as I struggled to get more pressing things done with my limited energy. Rental house madness. Believe me, having to dig deep holes and putting in lots of plants is much easier than dealing with a socio-pathic tenant. People can suck.

While I was out digging my holes, I had a slight inkling of a lessened painful reaction to getting out and working. Just barely perceptible, but enough to get my attention.

When you feel crappy for so long, even the slightest improvement is noticed. In the past twenty years, I have called upon these relaxed, warm, comfortable, can't-feel-my-aching-ass-body, instances for solace and inspiration, and it spurs me on. Is this how normal people feel?! I can only barely remember what that's like and it has been getting fainter by the year.

I want to be feeling so good and free of pain that I am "outside" my body, ahead in my plans, and excited about my dreams - like I used to be as a child. All I think about these days is how I feel and what the hell I ought to be doing about it. I know I'm overstating it, but it's a large part of what occupies my mind.

This morning my arms and legs ache and I can feel the fatigue slipping in. Not to worry! I push through and do what I have to do, albeit at a minimum. Bills need paying? Write a check. Hair's on fire? Put it out. Then go back to resting and shoving my nose into research on what to do next to get well.

I got back my Comprehensive Metabolic Panel and all of the 20 or so items are in range. No major values high or low is a good thing, if the ranges are anything to trust. A few were on the low side, a couple on the high, but most are in the middle. I'm sorting and researching(!) to find out what they mean.

Most of my testing comes back "normal". My FBG is always good, no renal problems, gluten sensitivity

### Martin — 2012-04-29T17:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/martins-story.870/post-19786>

Bad crash yesterday afternoon. I suspect it's the parasite cleanse (mimosa pudica) and the constipating effects of the cholestyramine. In bed for hours, never did recover before the night was over. Flu-like feeling I always have was heightened big-time.

I did not do night dose of CSM, doc said back off if it gets too rough.

Hammered today, but got to go plant my garden. The seasons wait for no one.
