The only other thing is I sometimes eat more protein than the 200g for the day. I've probably gone over the 500 calls some days. I am not counting calories and just going by servings and sizes. I am still at 1-2 fruits per day and can't seem to give those up. I have 2 veggies and 2 protein portions but have been over on protein. Could that really be a reason? I do find my clothes are looser but I haven't taken measurements and don't think it's anything significant. Another thing I am noticing is a feeling of being overly full. I don't remember that in previous rounds. I just remember no hunger but not this overly full feeling after some meals. I ate a little extra protein at lunch today, bison and cabbage. Then, I decreased shrimp portion for dinner to half and that was 3 shrimps. I am beyond stuffed. I am also having broccoli this round as someone suggested it to me and it's a veggie I like and make for the kids a lot because they like it. So, Colleen, I think my losses are even worse than yours and I'm not adding fat.

ok I've been lurking but my round is essentially over so I'll start fessing up.

First round was in January 31 dose days. I did it protocol except: No grains, limited fruits, mixed veggies, no supplements.

Second round started late March- 37 dose days. Protocol except: no grains, no fruit, mixed veggies, non-protocol type of veggies, ate 550-650 calories, HIGH protein, no food after 7, LOTS of sleep, CT when I could (cold showers, ice bathes, face dunks), pycnogenol, curcumin, PQQ and african mango daily.

Here are the weight for both rounds (starting post load):

### AndreaC — 2012-05-02T21:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-20772>

To continue my previous post, some key differences between rounds:

Round 1-

Cheats- I had jack daniels a few times, it cost me the second day after, cocoa powder!

Energy- I was lethargic, had to talk myself up my single flight of stairs at night. I didn't work out at all.

Temperature- I froze the entire round

Mood- Besides the dose euphoria (1-3 hours later), I was pissy, I didn't want anyone to touch me, I was unpopular.

Round 2-

Cheats- Sugar free gum (pure death I know), ate over 500 cal (often, but all protein), ate up until 8 pm once, cocoa powder (a lot!)

Energy- Totally normal, actually did some weight lifting from time to time. Felt great.

Temperature- Fine, aside from stupid office fluctuations, I was warm and normal. Ice bathes always give me cold feet after but no change otherwise.

Mood- Happy, normal. No sex drive like before, but not pissy.

Other things I noted in R2 the lead to some goooood losses.

\- Sleep in a very dark room (with my blanket above my head), and lots of it. Bed around 9-10 most nights

\- Mg, took it both rounds but more in R2 and the days when I ate a lot of cocoa powder (a very good source of Mg) I lost #'s a day.

\- Cold showers/rinses at the end of my morning shower seemed to keep me warm enough for half the day.

\- Longer time between food and bed = greater weight loss.

\- Darkness after sunset, either kept the lights very low or not on at all.

\- Oxytocin!! Nights with the beau snuggling in the dark were great for loss. Happy = skinny for me.

Oh and I'm 29, female, 5'9", tried LR last year and lost 6lbs in 6 weeks (and gave up!).

I'm starting LR on saturday (omg fattttttttt!) to stabilize and very confident it will go well.

Ok there's (some of) my data to add to the pool.

I have lots more specifics but its a lot to type. Very detailed notes on both rounds.

Oh yeah, bottom line. I didn't try adding fat because I was scared.

But HCG + CT + Mg + Dark sleep = Greater losses.

### colleencoble — 2012-05-02T23:42:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-20830>

By one weekend not being worth it, Claudia, I mean you would quickly regain the lost territory. If you do an interruption, you have to go off 72 hours before the weekend, then you have the weekend and you will gain anyway because you won't be following P3. And then you go back on the protocol to lose it. So better to take your lumps on the cheat weekend and then lose it the next 3 days.

It's not just the fat with CT that I've heard people have run into the problems, by the way. Several reported more hunger no matter how they tweaked their dose so they stopped the CT. I don't know what the issue is.

### Claudia — 2012-05-03T03:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-20875>

Well, maybe I should explain myself better. Thanks ColleenC, but this is not a cheat weekend that I am planning, it's just a weekend off p2. I would not be eating any junk (I never do, haven't in years as I have been Paleo for 3 and before that I followed high fat, low carb). I haven't had any sugar in 1.5 years and no grains in 3 years. During the mother's day weekend, I would just not be limiting myself to p2 foods and I'd be following p3 foods, like I might have some healthy fats, like coconut oil, an omelet for mom's day with a slice of bacon. I'd probably have a steak for dinner at the restaurant but I could eat more than 100grams, maybe some veggies with a little butter. Different veggies. Things like that. If I know I have hcg in my system, I will not feel like I can eat anything but p2 foods and portions. It's a mindset as well. I want to have a weekend of not weighing portions, of maybe having 1-2 eggs. I will be in p3 so why would I gain necessarily? It's a break from the restrictions of p2, it's not cheating.

And, I agree, this is not just the fat with CT that is a problem. I haven't added any fat while on hcg.

### colleencoble — 2012-05-10T22:01:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-22980>

Pounds and Inches seems to indicate that if you have a job where you are eating out and must cheat, it's not fatal but if you have a lot of obligations like that, it's best to stop and try again when there aren't so many social occasions.

Persons in high official positions such as embassy personnel, politicians, senior executives, etc., who are obliged to attend social functions to which they cannot bring their meager meal must be told beforehand that an official

dinner will cost them the loss of about three days treatment, however careful they are and in spite of a friendly and would-be cooperative host. We generally advise them to avoid all around embarrassment, the almost inevitable turn of conversation to their weight problem and the outpouring of lay counsel from their table partners by not letting it be known that they are under treatment. They should take dainty servings of everything, bide what they can under the cutlery and book the gain which may take three days to get rid of as one of the sacrifices which their profession entails. Allowing three days for their correction, such incidents do not jeopardize the treatment, provided they do not occur all too frequently in which case treatment should be postponed to a socially more peaceful season.

### colleencoble — 2012-05-10T22:22:09+0300

### spinnsugar@hotmail.com — 2012-05-14T16:58:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-23894>

Hello everyone. I am having a slow round, and it is number 5.

Until Jan, I had regained most of my weight of previous rounds back, then I found LRx and later CT. I am on day 11, and have 24lbs left til goal, which is 20lbs higher than I weighed 10yrs ago.

I plan on staying on my round until then, and am hoping that it need not be too much longer than 50days. I am not a fan of breaking protocol!

Therefore I had a question to Colleen, or anyone else who would like to give opinions, if instead of an interruption, I do a day of super high fat(coconut oil, bacon, HWC, hamburger etc), like a reload, around the 25day mark.

I know by this time I will feel dried up, and should be hitting TOM. I understand it might set me back a few days, as would an interruption...72hrs plus the interruption time. I think a day would suffice for me.

Anyone do this, or hear good/bad results from this? I sure dont want to blow the round, and I have no problem being strict at all. Especially after for the last 4 weeks.

Thank you so much and Hope you all had a Happy Mothers Day!!!

### colleencoble — 2012-05-14T18:40:35+0300

I would not do that. I have always done long rounds and there's nothing special about the 25 day mark. I never feel any different that week than in any prior weeks. Most gals don't skip during TOM. You really aren't gaining anything by a mid round cheat like that. So that's my view.  Good luck!

### Claudia — 2012-05-18T03:27:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-25052>

I just read about Rob Wolff's autoimmune protocol: . If you suffer from an autoimmune disease we highly recommend you start a Paleo diet and let us know what your results are. To give your body its best chance to heal we recommend that you initially limit the following foods:

• Eggs

• Tomatoes & eggplants

• Peppers including bell peppers and hot peppers

• Spices such as curries, paprika, and chili powder.

Some of these otherwise Paleo-friendly foods have been shown to be problematic in individuals with autoimmune issues. We recommend you fully remove not only these foods but also all Neolithic foods (grains, breads, potatoes, beans and dairy) for at least a month to see if they pose a problem for you.”

I spent 30 days without eggs while doing my round and then, I had them on my interruption, and now this week back to no eggs. I did not notice any reactions to those eggs, 4 of them, that I had over the weekend. With eliminating, you take out these foods and then, add them back slowly one at time to see if you have any reactions. Since I didn't, I'm thinking it's ok for me to have eggs when I start p3 on Monday. What do you think? Glad things are going better for you Colleen.

### colleencoble — 2012-05-18T03:39:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-25062>

I think mine was 20 mg in the morning. I can't remember for sure as it's been several years ago. Weight loss is finally picking up. I had to stop CT to get it to go though because I had hunger.

Claudia, this is what Dr. Kruse says as well about autoimmune disease. And because they aren't something you're actually allergic to, the effects are very subtle and you can't really easily tell. It's more about how your body is reacting with your autoimmune disease and it may take days or weeks to really see a difference. If you have an autoimmune disease, I wouldn't eat much of them. I have them in mayo on occasion and that's about it.

### LinD — 2012-05-18T03:47:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-25068>

I have MS. After not knowing about the issues with these problematic foods for those with AI disease, I started eating 3-4 eggs per day after losing 40# (with hCG). About six weeks after eating all these eggs, I started having numbness in both feet. MS? Eggs? I don't know!

While I stopped eggs altogether middle of November 2011, I still have this numbness so it's been eight months now! I've never had an MS symptom last this long. What the heck?

I would love to have eggs again, but do not dare yet. At least until the numbness goes away...for quite awhile

Signed,

Sad Without Eggs

Sprint Samsung Epic

week 1: 2.5 mg in the morning

week 2: 2.5 mg in the morning, 2.5 at noon

week 3: 5 mg in the morning 2.5 at noon

week 4: 5 mg in the morning, 5 at noon

week 5: 5 mg in the morning, 5 at noon, 2.5 at noon...

### colleencoble — 2012-05-19T15:28:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-25464>

You need a different doctor. Bioidentical progesterone will protect you from cancer. You desperately need it!

Doc, it's been a bust, I'm sorry to say. Without the fat in my diet, I got very hungry on CT, no matter how I tweaked my dose. And I'm an expert at dosing so I knew it wasn't the dose. I stopped doing CT about a week ago now and the hunger has gone away. Losses are finally normal. I don't understand it though. I sure thought CT would be the bomb with hcg. At some point later in the summer, I think I will try CT+the hcg diet without the hcg and see if it works the same. I'll just try it a week or so because I really don't have much to lose but I could tell if I felt the same as on hcg--cold feet, very "up" mood. I just sort of think it might. What do you think?

Or was I doing the wrong kind of CT? I was immersing 3x a week and icing my thighs 2x a week. ChimpChick didn't start CTing until the middle of her round and was just face dunking and sitting on ice cushions, I believe she said. So maybe I'd been doing CT for too long before I tried the hcg. I'd been immersing for 2 1/2 months before I started hcg. I tried heavy whipping cream first then switched to coconut oil halfway through the round. The losses were lousy on cream and nearly non existent on coconut oil. I lost 4.6 pounds in 23 days with the fat +CT which is really lousy. I should have lost about 12 pounds. I didn't lose much in the way of inches either. I'd hoped that even though the number on the scale was low, the inches lost would have been good and the difference was muscle or something but that doesn't appear to be the case.

I'd sure love to figure it out! I love CTing though. I'm going right back to it in another week. I do think my cellulite is better too which is enough reason to do it!

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-05-28T20:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-28066>

i'll see how i'll do...i have coconut butter and planned on taking the artisana packets to work to serve as a sort of "lunch" or to keep on hand in emergencies. hopefully it won't adversely affect me...never noticed a problem with it before but wasnt really weighing myself regularly at the time.

i'm super excited. my husband has been doing my weigh ins (i can get a bit obsessive compulsive and never have been good with knowing my weight numbers so we agreed this is best...i've been doing this for years, i even turn around when the dr is weighing me). anyway...i avoided weigh-ins the first couple of weeks of HCG bc i didnt want to be discouraged if it was slow going and just trust that it was working....and lost about 7 pounds at the end when i started weighting....but my inflammation must be way down from 1.5 years ago. i tried on a dress i wore at my rehearsal dinner and one from my bachelorette party and they were both coming off of me! this was before going gluten free/paleo (bc i knew i was sick and didnt know with what) and i was exercising excessively trying to stave off the random weight gain that was occuring (at the time my stupid docs kept saying nothing was wrong with my thyroid). i ended up looking good for my wedding but had to do juice fasts (which is the only time i ever experienced relief....juicing is not the best idea but i suppose since it eliminated dairy and gluten its no wonder i felt better while doing them!) to avoid a bloated look on the big day. anyways...i was SHOCKED that this was the case. my body must be doing so much better! i'm excited to go into ketogenic paleo/leptin reset mode.

i can only imagine what my CRP was around then. i just have to give myself credit for making progress. my wedding would have been alot less stressful had i realized what was going on. i remember everyone saying "aren't you stressed out about the wedding?" and me not feelilng ANY stress about it, but instead being completely freaked out and crying daily over my deteriorating health. its been a long road but i'm finally feeling i'm on my way...this is so exciting!

### colleencoble — 2012-05-30T03:56:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-ct-fat-what-are-you-doing-and-what-are-your-results.908/post-28303>

I don't count calories. I usually have salmon for breakfast, cooked in coconut oil. For lunch, I might have a grilled chicken breast topped with a whole avocado, pepperjack cheese and a bit of Greek yogurt. A salad with homemade avocado dressing. For dinner it's often steak or seafood of some kind with a veggie. I have coconut oil in most everything and also heavy whipping cream in 2 big mugs of coffee a day.

### chocolate — 2012-04-16T01:30:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-15413>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11896744](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11896744)

Because the role of elemental sulfur in human nutrition has not been studied extensively, it is the purpose of this article to emphasize the importance of this element in humans and discuss the therapeutic applications of sulfur compounds in medicine. Sulfur is the sixth most abundant macromineral in breast milk and the third most abundant mineral based on percentage of total body weight. The sulfur-containing amino acids (SAAs) are methionine, cysteine, cystine, homocysteine, homocystine, and taurine. Dietary SAA analysis and protein supplementation may be indicated for vegan athletes, children, or patients with HIV, because of an increased risk for SAA deficiency in these groups. Methylsulfonylmethane (MSM), a volatile component in the sulfur cycle, is another source of sulfur found in the human diet. Increases in serum sulfate may explain some of the therapeutic effects of MSM, DMSO, and glucosamine sulfate. Organic sulfur, as SAAs, can be used to increase synthesis of S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe), glutathione (GSH), taurine, and N-acetylcysteine (NAC). MSM may be effective for the treatment of allergy, pain syndromes, athletic injuries, and bladder disorders. Other sulfur compounds such as SAMe, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), taurine, glucosamine or chondroitin sulfate, and reduced glutathione may also have clinical applications in the treatment of a number of conditions such as depression, fibromyalgia, arthritis, interstitial cystitis, athletic injuries, congestive heart failure, diabetes, cancer, and AIDS. Dosages, mechanisms of action, and rationales for use are discussed. The low toxicological profiles of these sulfur compounds, combined with promising therapeutic effects, warrant continued human clinical trails.

### chocolate — 2012-04-16T03:26:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-15456>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20597983](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20597983)

Functional genes as markers for sulfur cycling and CO2 fixation in microbial communities of hydrothermal vents of the Logatchev field.

HÃ¼gler M, GÃ¤rtner A, Imhoff JF.

Source

Leibniz Institut fÃ¼r Meereswissenschaften, IFM-GEOMAR, Kiel, Germany. [michael.huegler@tzw.de](mailto:michael.huegler@tzw.de)

Abstract

Life at deep-sea hydrothermal vents depends on chemolithoautotrophic microorganisms as primary producers mediating the transfer of energy from hydrothermal fluids to higher trophic levels. A comprehensive molecular survey was performed with microbial communities in a mussel patch at the Irina II site of the Logatchev hydrothermal field by combining the analysis of 16S rRNA gene sequences with studies of functional key genes involved in biochemical pathways of sulfur oxidation-reduction (soxB, aprA) and autotrophic carbon fixation (aclB, cbbM, cbbL). Most significantly, major groups of chemoautotrophic sulfur oxidizers in the diffuse fluids differed in their biosynthetic pathways of both carbon fixation and sulfur oxidation. One important component of the community, the Epsilonproteobacteria, has the potential to grow chemoautotrophically by means of the reductive tricarboxylic acid cycle and to gain energy through the oxidation of reduced sulfur compounds using the Sox pathway. The majority of soxB and all retrieved aclB gene sequences were assigned to this group. Another important group in this habitat, the Gammaproteobacteria, may use the adenosine 5'-phosphosulfate pathway and the Calvin-Benson-Bassham cycle, deduced from the presence of aprA and cbbM genes. Hence, two important groups of primary producers at the investigated site might use different pathways for sulfur oxidation and carbon fixation.

I think we might be a microbial community.

Sulfur eating bacteria

[http://phys.org/news182798965.html](http://http://phys.org/news182798965.html)

(PhysOrg.com) -- Acid Mine Drainage (AMD) is caused when sulphur in mine tailings reacts with water and oxygen in the environment to produce sulphuric acid. It is a major environmental issue, with AMD a concern for lake acidification and water quality. AMD is also implicated as a climate change culprit - the sulphuric acid dissolves carbonate minerals in the underlying rock, liberating carbon dioxide in a process known as acid rock weathering.

### chocolate — 2012-04-28T17:47:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19581>

[http://www.healthhype.com/fructose-malabsorption-cause-symptoms-and-diagnosis.html](http://http://www.healthhype.com/fructose-malabsorption-cause-symptoms-and-diagnosis.html) fructose somehow shuts down glutathione, its an either/or I guess.... of course, that just MY opinion

Laboratory Findings in FM

Fructose malabsorption was found to be correlated with low levels of folic acid in blood plasma in some cases, supposedly due to reduced amount of intestinal bacteria that produce folic acid (3). Plasma levels of zinc, iron, calcium, vitamin C and E, triptophane and glutathione may also be lowered, and amylase, **lipase and triglicerides may be elevated (4,7). ‘Fructose deficiency’, on the other hand, does n0t exist, since fructose is not an essential nutrient in a human.

[http://www.healthhype.com/small-intestinal-bacterial-overgrowth-symptoms-treatment.html](http://http://www.healthhype.com/small-intestinal-bacterial-overgrowth-symptoms-treatment.html)

Symptoms of SIBO

Symptoms of small intestinal bacterial overgrowth may include:

Early satiety (feeling full after only “few bites”), bloating and nausea, which forces many patients to avoid food, resulting in weight loss.

**Sulphur burps**

Some patients with SIBO crave sugar.

Chronic diarrhea - may appear weeks or even years after the causing event, like surgery (1).

Whitish, floating, foamy and sticky stool due to unabsorbed fats.

Muscle weakness and bone pains due to vitamin D deficiency.

Anemia with pale skin, weakness or tingling due to vitamin B12 deficiency.

Impaired night vision due to vitamin A deficiency.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-28T17:59:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19583>

[http://www.springboard4health.com/notebook/min\_sulfur.html](http://http://www.springboard4health.com/notebook/min_sulfur.html)

Description

Sulfur occurs principally in the body as a constituent of the cysteine and methionine. As a constituent of these amino acids it plays an important role in the structural conformation of almost all proteins and enzymes found in the body.

Sulfur is also important in the structure of antibodies, and the sulfide group of cysteine is important in the activity of various enzymes. The disulfide linkage, which is formed between two cysteine residues, tends to bind up adjacent parallel peptide chains, thereby adding to the structural rigidity of many proteins.

Sulfur is an important constituent of **thiamine (vitamin B-1) and biotin, and is found in **insulin, keratin, and various glycoproteins. It is important in the molecule chondroitin sulfate in bone and cartilage.

Method of Action

Sulfur in the body exists primarily as a constituent of cysteine and methionine, although it also exists in smaller amounts in various inorganic sulfate compounds.

Sulfate, which contains sulfur, is an important constituent of the compound **serotonin; serotonin acts as a vasoconstrictor** and aids in blood coagulation.

Disulfide bridges, or sulfur to sulfur bonding of two cysteine residues, function to link together the various peptide constituents of all immunoglobulin molecules. Disulfide bridges also aid in the maintenance of structural rigidity in keratin protein. Keratin is composed of many peptide chains, which are linked together by hydrogen and disulfide bonds. Skin is made of a "softer" keratin which contains less disulfide bonds and therefore, less structural rigidity. "Hard" keratin, as is found in hair and nails, has a greater amount of structural rigidity.

Disulfide bonds are essential in maintaining the three dimensional structures of proteins and enzymes. Enzymatic activity, in many enzymes, depends upon the protein structure. For example, pancreatic ribonuclease has four disulfide bonds within its protein structure and does not function efficiently in the absence of malformation of these bonds.

Sulfur is a constituent of the molecule coenzyme A (CoA), which is involved in a wide variety of metabolic reactions. CoA plays a role in glycolysis, metabolism of fatty acids, degradation of various amino acids, and the formation of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine, as well as numerous other metabolic processes.

Return to top

Properties and Uses

Sulfur-containing drugs have been used in a wide variety of clinical treatments. Among these are the sulfonylureas, which enhance glucose utilization in diabetic patients; sulfonamides, used in combatting infection; salicylazosulfapyradine, and anti-inflammatory agent; sulfisoxazole, and antibiotic; sulfinpyrazone, which helps treat chronic gout and recurrent heart attacks**; and sulfasalazine, which is used in the treatment of ulcers.

Consequences of Deficiency

Cysteine renal calculi arise from a hereditary defect in renal tubular resorption of cysteine, which results in cystinuria (an abnormally high urinary excretion of cysteine) and production of cysteine crystal kidney stones. It should be noted that adequate protein intake almost always assures adequate organic sulfur intake.

Toxicity Levels

Ingestion of organically-complexed sulfur (e.g., in amino acids) in excess has not been shown to be toxic.** High sulfate levels have been observed in infants fed exclusively on formulae with abnormally high cysteine and methionine contents.

Recommended Dietary Allowances

There has been no RDA established for sulfur. It is assumed that an individual takes in sufficient amounts of sulfur when the protein intake is adequate.

The major food sources are proteins containing methionine and cysteine. The body synthesizes cysteine from its precursor methionine.

It's hard to believe li'l ol' fructose can stop all of this.

### chocolate — 2012-04-28T23:04:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19649>

[http://www.nature.com/nrm/journal/v6/n4/full/nrm1620.html](http://http://www.nature.com/nrm/journal/v6/n4/full/nrm1620.html)

Perspectives

Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology 6, 345-351 (April 2005) | doi:10.1038/nrm1620

Iron–sulphur cluster biogenesis and mitochondrial iron homeostasis

Tracey A. Rouault & Wing-Hang Tong

Abstract

Iron–sulphur clusters are important cofactors for proteins that are involved in many cellular processes, including electron transport, enzymatic catalysis and regulation. The enzymes that catalyse the formation of iron–sulphur clusters are widely conserved from bacteria to humans. Recent studies in model systems and humans reveal that iron–sulphur proteins have important roles in mitochondrial iron homeostasis and in the pathogenesis of the human disease Friedreich ataxia.

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T03:41:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19712>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12971554](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12971554)

Synergistic enhancement of a copper chelator, bathocuproine disulphonate, and cysteine on in vitro growth of Plasmodium falciparum in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase-deficient erythrocytes.

Tantular IS, Jalloh A, Pusarawati S, Azuno Y, Lin K, Kerong H, Dachlan YP, Ishii A, Kawamoto F.

Source

Tropical Disease Center, Department of Parasitology, Faculty of Medicine, Airlangga University, Surabaya, Indonesia.

Abstract

In vitro growth of Plasmodium falciparum is restricted in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD)-deficient erythrocytes (RBC), as a result of oxidative stress. **Bathocuproine disulphonate (BCS), a copper chelator, as well as cysteine have been shown to synergistically stimulate the in vitro growth of various mammalian cells and Trypanosoma under oxygenated conditions.** We examined the effects of these two chemicals on the in vitro growth of P. falciparum in G6PD-deficient RBC, and found that addition of BCS and cysteine synergistically enhanced the growth of the P. falciparum FCR-3 strain in these RBC to the same level as in normal RBC. However, BCS or cysteine alone had no stimulatory effect. To explain this synergistic enhancement, changes in thiol, NADPH and glutathione contents were investigated. After addition of cysteine alone, thiol content in the medium decreased rapidly, but when BCS was added, it was maintained at about 35% at 24 hours after incubation, suggesting that BCS stimulates parasite growth in G6PD-deficient RBC by inhibiting copper-mediated oxidation of cysteine in the medium. In these RBC, no increase in NADPH level, but a slight increase in glutathione, was observed in the presence of both BCS and cysteine. The slight increase of glutathione, was probably due to incorporation of cysteine from the medium, although this could not fully explain the synergistic growth enhancement. These findings taken together suggest that cysteine incorporated into G6PD-deficient RBC may help maintain the thiol groups in many proteins, such as membrane proteins, hemoglobin and enzymes, and plays an important role in maintaining an appropriate culture state necessary for parasite growth. We also examined the effects of BCS and cysteine on adaptation of wild isolates of P. falciparum to in vitro cultivation using the candle jar method. Although there was no drastic effect on growth enhancement, the presence of BCS and cysteine accelerated the appearance of schizonts in many isolates.

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T03:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19713>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22010944](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22010944)

Mycobacterium tuberculosis WhiB3: a novel iron-sulfur cluster protein that regulates redox homeostasis and virulence.

Saini V, Farhana A, Steyn AJ.

Source

Department of Microbiology, University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham, Alabama, USA.

Abstract

SIGNIFICANCE:

Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), the causative agent of tuberculosis (TB), can persist in a latent state for decades without causing overt disease. Since latent Mtb is refractory to current antimycobacterial drugs, the discovery and characterization of the biological mechanisms controlling the entry, maintenance, and emergence from latent infection is critical to the development of novel clinical therapies. RECENT ADVANCES: **Recently, Mtb WhiB3, a member of the family of intracellular iron-sulfur (Fe-S) cluster proteins has emerged as a redox sensor and effector molecule controlling several aspects of Mtb virulence. WhiB3 was shown to contain a 4Fe-4S cluster that specifically reacts with important host gases (O(2) and NO), and exogenous and endogenous metabolic signals to maintain redox balance. Notably, the concept of reductive stress emerged from studies on WhiB3**. CRITICAL

ISSUES:

The detailed mechanism of how WhiB3 functions as an intracellular redox sensor is unknown. Sustaining Mtb redox balance is particularly important since the bacilli encounter a large number of redox stressors during infection, and because several antimycobacterial prodrugs are effective only upon bioreductive activation in the mycobacterial cytoplasm.

FUTURE DIRECTIONS:

How Mtb WhiB3 monitors its internal and external surroundings and modulates endogenous oxido-reductive pathways which in turn alter Mtb signal transduction, nucleic acid and protein synthesis, and enzymatic activation, is mostly unexplored. Modern expression, metabolomic and proteomic technologies should provide fresh insights into these yet unanswered questions.

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T03:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19715>

What Happens If You Get Too Much Sulfur in Your Diet? Photo Credit health supplement pills image by weim from Fotolia.com

Two forms of sulfur supplements exist in the market: dimethyl sulfoxide, or DMSO, and methylsulfonylmethane, or MSM. Fruits, vegetables, grains, milk, meat and eggs also contain MSM. **MSM has important health benefits -- they help form cartilage, tendons and ligaments -- and it can be used for medicinal purposes to decrease pain. DMSO, in addition to having painkilling effects, can reduce swelling. Despite the wide range of health benefits of dietary sulfur, you should not overload on it as research on sulfur overdose is still limited.

Limited Research

Due to limited research on possible toxicity effects of dietary fiber, the Food and Nutrition Board at the Institute of Medicine could not establish the recommended intake or the safe upper limit intake of sulfur. However, the World Health Organization and the Food and Agriculture Organization have established the recommended dietary allowance for sulfur-containing amino acid, SAA, at 13 mg per kg per day for healthy adults. Of the studies available, the majority of the findings have pointed to no toxicity effects when participants consumed a large amount of dietary sulfur.**

Read more: [http://www.livestrong.com/article/430390-what-happens-if-you-get-too-much-sulfur-in-your-diet/#ixzz1tO0tKxke](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/430390-what-happens-if-you-get-too-much-sulfur-in-your-diet/#ixzz1tO0tKxke)

Read more: [http://www.livestrong.com/article/430390-what-happens-if-you-get-too-much-sulfur-in-your-diet/#ixzz1tO0dEEql](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/430390-what-happens-if-you-get-too-much-sulfur-in-your-diet/#ixzz1tO0dEEql)

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T17:23:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-19778>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tyrosine\_sulfation](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tyrosine_sulfation)

Function

Sulfation plays role in strengthening protein-protein interactions. Types of human proteins known to undergo tyrosine sulfation include adhesion molecules, G-protein-coupled receptors, coagulation factors, serine protease inhibitors, extracellular matrix proteins, and hormones. Tyrosine O-sulfate is a stable molecule and is excreted in urine in animals. No enzymatic mechanism of tyrosine sulfate desulfation is known to exist.

By knock-out of TPST genes in mice, it may be observed that tyrosine sulfation has effects on the growth of the mice, such as body weight, fecundity, and postnatal viability.

[http://robinsonlab.stanford.edu/publications/crespo\_j\_clin\_imm\_2011.pdf](http://http://robinsonlab.stanford.edu/publications/crespo_j_clin_imm_2011.pdf)

Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors Ameliorate Autoimmune

Encephalomyelitis in a Mouse Model of Multiple Sclerosis

Conclusion

We show that the administration of sorafenib, imatinib,

or GW2580 ameliorates EAE, raising the possibility that

these TKI—two of which are already FDA-approved

drugs—could serve as novel therapeutics for the

treatment of MS. Further elucidation of the specific

pathways targeted by these TKI could shed light on the

pathogenesis of MS and allow the development of more

specific drugs that may have fewer side effects than the

current therapies for MS.

### chocolate — 2012-05-02T18:06:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-20688>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/4081634](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/4081634)

Influence of the bacterial flora of the gut on sulfur amino acid degradation. A study of patients with bacterial overgrowth before and during treatment with oxytetracycline or metronidazole.

MÃ¥rtensson J, Svensson H, Tobiasson P.

Abstract

The urinary excretion of sulfur amino acids and their main degradation products was determined in 10 patients with bacterial overgrowth during treatment with oxytetracycline or metronidazole. A normal excretion of total sulfur and inorganic sulfate was found during treatment with both antibiotics, indicating a normal intake, uptake, and oxidation of sulfur amino acids to inorganic sulfate. Irrespective of type of antibiotic given, a normalization of the increased ester sulfate excretion was found, favoring the opinion that both the aerobic and anaerobic flora of the gut contribute to the formation of sulfate esters. **The cystathioninuria found in patients with bacterial overgrowth was normalized only by treatment with metronidazole. Both antibiotics resulted in a reduced excretion of taurine and thiosulfate, but especially for thiosulfate, the effect of metronidazole treatment was more pronounced. The results suggest that the bacterial flora of the gut, such as anaerobes, may be of importance in the formation of thiosulfate in the human body.**

Can anyone translate what this means....is excretion good, like lots is made or bad like lots is dumped?

### chocolate — 2012-05-08T22:08:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-22336>

Sulfite Sensitivity

— Unrecognized Threat:

— Is Molybdenum Deficiency

the Cause?

Rhoda Papaioannou, M.S.1 and Carl C. Pfeiffer, M.D., Ph.D.1

[http://www.orthomolecular.org/library/jom/1984/pdf/1984-v13n02-p105.pdf](http://http://www.orthomolecular.org/library/jom/1984/pdf/1984-v13n02-p105.pdf)

**As far back as 1913, Rost and Franz

reported that ingestion of 1 g of sodium sulfite

per day decreased utilization of protein and fat in

humans. Such studies have apparently not been

repeated; hence we must question what the

biochemical technology for discerning more

subtle metabolic changes would reveal 70 years

later about the effects of chronic, albeit lowlevel,

exposure to sulfites. Sidorenkov (1957)

has reported that inhaled sulfur dioxide readily

penetrates into the blood stream from the

lungs; therefore adverse findings on inhaled

sulfur dioxide must carry over to ingested

sulfites. He also finds a marked alteration in

carbohydrate metabolism, perhaps due to the

destruction of thiol groups of biologically

active substances such as insulin. Reduction in

liver glycogenesis and an increase in protein

and non-protein nitrogen in the blood is also

noted.**

Perhaps the heartburn or indigestion many of

us suffer after fancy wining and dining out may

not be mere gluttony (surely we'd eaten that

much at home before without consequence!)

but due rather to the 0.007 oz (200 mg) of

sulfite we may have ingested. 200 mg sulfite can

make almost anybody vomit, according to

Lafontaine and Goblet (1955) who induced the

vomiting reflex in man consistently at such

doses. And there are known additive effects

when bisulfite is ingested together with other

chemicals such as benzoic acid (Prenner and

Stevens, 1976), another widely used

preservative which also occurs naturally in

some foods such as cranberries, cinammon

and cloves.

Possible Cause of Sulfite Sensitivity

A possible explanation for sulfite sensitivity

might be the widespread molybdenum deficiency

which we find in a majority of our

patients (Pfeiffer, 1983; Sohler, 1983). Many

have no detectable blood molybdenum and

most have levels below 5 ppb (normal 10 to 100

ppb). Molybdenum is the trace element

contained in the enzyme sulfite oxidase which

detoxifies sulfite to the inert and harmless

sulfate.

Sulfite oxidase deficiency has been identified

since 1967 as a rare inborn error of

metabolism, and recently a new inherited

disease due to deficiency of the "molybdenum

cofactor", a constituent of the three enzymes xanthine dehydrogenase, sulfite

oxidase and aldehyde oxidase, has been

recognized (Wadman et al., 1983). Neither of these

genetic diseases responds to molybdenum

supplementation, for here it is either the enzyme

which is deficient or the metal in association with

another moiety which is absent. Nonetheless,

part of the patients' symptomatology is

considered due to high accumulated toxic sulfite

and insufficient sulfate. It is not inconceivable

that some of the same symptoms may be evoked

in normal individuals by simple molybdenum

deficiency in the face of high environmental

sulfite. Indeed Abumrad et al. (1981) have

demonstrated that a molybdenum deficient diet

would result in a sulfur handling defect at the level of

transformation of sulfite to sulfate.

**The main duty of the mineral molybdenum inside the body is to break apart sulfite toxins, thus making molybdenum an antioxidant.** The mineral also helps the body to process iron better and helps to metabolize fats and carbohydrates within the body. Molybdenum is stored in the brain, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, bones and skin, and needs to be replenished regularly as it is absorbed and eliminated on a routine basis.

### chocolate — 2012-05-14T17:32:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-23907>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sulforaphane](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sulforaphane)

Sulforaphane is an organosulfur compound that exhibits anticancer, antidiabetic, and antimicrobial properties in experimental models. It is obtained from cruciferous vegetables such as broccoli, Brussels sprouts or cabbages. The enzyme myrosinase transforms glucoraphanin, a glucosinolate, into sulforaphane upon damage to the plant (such as from chewing). Young sprouts of broccoli and cauliflower are particularly rich in glucoraphanin.

Possible medicinal properties

Consumption of broccoli sprouts has shown to be potentially effective at inhibiting Helicobacter pylori growth,\[2\]\[3\] with sulforaphane being at least one of the active agents.\[4\]\[5\]

Sulforaphane and dietary consumption of cruciferous vegetables are known to affect the action of drug-metabolizing enzymes in vitro and in preliminary human studies.\[6\] Although no side effects or direct drug interactions have been reported as of 2008, people taking prescription drugs are advised to consult a doctor before taking sulforaphane or broccoli-sprout extracts.

The possible anticancer activity of sulforaphane may be related to the induction of phase-II enzymes of xenobiotic transformation (such as quinone reductase and glutathione S-transferase), and enhancing the transcription of tumor suppressor proteins, possibly via inhibitory effects on histone deacetylase.\[7\]

Sulforaphane and diindolylmethane (another compound from Brassica vegetables) inhibit cancer growth in vitro and in experimental animals.\[8\] Sulforaphane downregulated the Wnt/beta-catenin self-renewal pathway in breast cancer stem cells.\[9\]

When applied topically, sulforaphane may protect skin against UV radiation damage, and thus potentially against cancer.\[10\] Sulforaphane may inhibit histone deacetylase (HDAC) activity.\[11\]\[12\]

Preliminary experiments indicate sulforaphane may protect the heart from vascular inflammation and atherosclerosis.\[13\]

Just saw this article....... [http://nourishingplot.com/2014/06/20/the-deficiency-thats-making-you-suffer-a-sunburn/](http://nourishingplot.com/2014/06/20/the-deficiency-thats-making-you-suffer-a-sunburn/)

### Ken23 — 2014-07-20T20:39:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-134995>

I think about the food I ate for the first 26 years of my life and it's scary. In high school I'd wake up and have an eggo waffle with aunt jemima syrup with conventional butter. And Id pat myself on the back for eating, because some days I would skip breakfast. When starting epi paleo I began eating pasture raised eggs for breakfast (cooked) and more seafood (cooked), more organic vegetables (cooked) giant steps in the right direction when compared to the eggo waffle breakfast but still cooked food. What does cooking do to the food? Loses the sulfur. Iv never eaten a raw onion in my life until last week. Raw\* onions= one of the richest sources of sulfur

Hypoglycemia, cognitive functioning, skin problems (acne, dry skin, poor color, inability to tan, hair loss), bipolar disorder (anxiety).....Sulfur is implicated in a big way in all of these. Dr. K's mentioning of neuroectoderm comes to mind. Bad skin= not optimal brain

Look at the anecdotal evidence of people preventing sunburn by adding huge amounts of organic sulfur into their diet with MSM...... [http://immortalhair.forumandco.com/t10347-msm-prevents-sunburn](http://immortalhair.forumandco.com/t10347-msm-prevents-sunburn)

When I made the connection a few days ago I started 2g of MSM.... today 3g. Part of my brain seems to have been turned on.

Could sulfur be this big of a factor?

### Queen of Elves — 2017-11-03T15:13:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulfur-is-huge.909/post-227267>

I came across a treatment with **inorganic** sulfur to get rid of bad gut bacteria. In an interview with a German MD and physicist (Prof. Dr. Probst) it is said, that the oral intake of inorganic sulfur (3 tsp per day with meals) will shift the milieu of the gut by bringing in high amounts of electrons, so that only friendly bacteria can survive, when done for a while. Supposedly the Navy Seals used to take sulfur with every meal.
He says MSM is also good (for example for bone health) but inorganic sulfur has much greater impact especially on gut health.

Has anybody taken inorganic sulfur for gut health and can tell me about the results?

" The microbiome bacteria release a lot of light naturally, along with molecular H2; it is the [absence of light, reduction of H2 from the gut, that stimulates the production of melatonin](http://physics.fau.edu/observatory/lightpol-melatonin.html) from serotonin. " All from Ubi 4 .

### admin — 2012-04-24T17:05:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-topic-requests.911/post-18358>

> **Coriander;18290 wrote:** Looking at the 'Member' page, I think that you need to subscribe to either Karnivores or Kruse Klub. Once you've done that, the webinars should be available to you. There's no longer any link to buy the webinar as a one off.

Correct. And keep in mind, for future webinars, if you paid for the webinar as a standalone ticket, the recordings will not be available for you after the event. We made an exception for the first go around but the availability of the recordings will only be available to Klub and Karnivore members going forward. Thanks!

# Thread: Can't figure out where else to post this cauli-mash hack that was awesome.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-16T03:17:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15452>

I've tried a whole lot of different recipes for cauli-mash. Hit the motherlode tonight. Steamed lots of garlic right along with the cauliflower. Stuck it into a processer with a bunch of the kerrygold garlic and herb butter and lots of salt. SUPER EASY and tasted just as good as the much more complex versions.

Sure it would be better for ya with bone broth etc etc - but for a total hands on time of less than 5 mins I count it as a win. I also sprinkle kelp granules in anything and everything for the iodine but I don't think that effects the taste.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-16T05:14:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15497>

Brilliant stuff. Gonna try that tonight!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-16T15:33:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15551>

When garlic is in season I buy it by the bulbs! Toss a whole bulb in to the toaster oven in tinfoil, w/salt and ghee - roast til done then squeeze the whole damn thing into my smashers... w/HWC and more ghee... I've had ppl ask me for my mashed potato recipe at family dinners - and they are SHOCKED when I tell them its smashed cauliflower - no potatoes.... gasp!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-16T18:56:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15617>

I've always been shy about trying to dupe people with my cauli mashed potatoes, but the non-paleo hubby loves them. I will add more garlic to mine next time -- sounds amazing! I like to sprinkle parsley on top when I serve. I also like to make mine sweet sometimes...I roast cauliflower and sprinkle with cinnamon and salt. Yum!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-18T20:04:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-16265>

I like cauliflower and I like mashed taters, but I never was much of a fan of mashed cauliflower. I figured it was because I was hoping too much it would taste like potatoes and was disappointed when t didn't. I'm going to try doing really garlicky mashed cauliflower and see if it makes the difference. Thanks for the tip!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-19T03:24:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-16423>

I've had good luck making radishes taste like potatoes using the same concept. Works like a charm!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-20T04:58:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-16896>

Made them tonight and they were very yum! The whole fam liked them. Thanks for the tip!

### Mud Flinger — 2012-04-21T00:42:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-17237>

I have learned to love caulifower now that I avoid night shades. I have found that it can do whatever the potato used to. Mock potato salad anyone? Really good. It has all the same flavors (mayo, pickle, mustard, egg) and even my oh so picky son will eat it (funny, he is a Micheal, so the old add of "hey, Mikey likes it" applies!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T16:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26311>

Breakfast choice was liver wurst and a cup o bone broth. Proudmama!

### BJK77 — 2012-05-22T16:22:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26317>

> **Cavemam;26402 wrote:**
>
> Breakfast choice was liver wurst and a cup o bone broth. Proudmama!

Wow! You rock Cavemam!! I feel like my kids overall eat a very healthy, paleo diet, but I don't ever see them having liver and broth for breakfast! Raw egg yolks, kefir and sausage are usually what's on the menu for them.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T20:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26411>

Ok, so raw egg yolks sound way yuckier than liverwurst. I got the roll of it from uswellness, and I'm making myself eat it. I find it goes better if I fry the beejeezus out of it. My son however just started eating it and liked it! he says. . It's like ham except hammier.

So why just yolk? Is it an allergy thing? My son also does yogurt/kefir as I'm not worried about carbs with him. Also, got lucky and some other kid brought seaweed to school for a snack - now he thinks its cool.

The whole blanket "no beans or corn or grains" is the hardest part to stick to. I guess I don't feel a little now and then is going to be negative - but I never want to let him have it as I've been putting so much effort into getting him to understand GRAINS ARE BAD - I just don't think 6 yo boys have a moderation button.

### ealachan — 2012-05-22T21:10:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26438>

I couldn't eat raw egg yolks straight, but whizzed up into a beverage, I could handle them...and I LURVE runny yolks when I cook fried eggs. There's nothing quite like a lightly fried egg over a bed of braised kale, with the yolk running all through it....YUUUUM!

re: liverwurst - if you like mustard, try it with some good hot German mustard. I wasn't a believer until I added the mustard. Now I could eat the stuff all day long.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T21:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26448>

> **ealachan;26529 wrote:** I couldn't eat raw egg yolks straight, but whizzed up into a beverage, I could handle them...and I LURVE runny yolks when I cook fried eggs. There's nothing quite like a lightly fried egg over a bed of braised kale, with the yolk running all through it....YUUUUM!
>
>
>
> re: liverwurst - if you like mustard, try it with some good hot German mustard. I wasn't a believer until I added the mustard. Now I could eat the stuff all day long.

Ok, so why raw yolks? Is that better than a whole egg?

### BJK77 — 2012-05-23T00:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26501>

> **Cavemam;26539 wrote:** Ok, so why raw yolks? Is that better than a whole egg?

The yolks are actually much more nutrient dense than the whites, which are the more allergenic part. Fortunately my kids don't have an egg allergy and my son does often eat the whole egg, but my daughter doesn't like the whites. Heat destroys a lot of the enzymes and nutrients in the yolks, and although they are still super nutritious even when cooked, our bodies can assimilate the nutrients better when raw.

### David — 2012-05-23T00:54:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26504>

I recently tried an alternative to cauliflower that tasted pretty good:

Mashed Celery Root (Celeriac)

Ingredients

1 lb Celery root (approx.)

1 Tbs butter

dash of salt & pepper.

Directions

Peel the celery root and chop into 1" cubes. Put into a medium saucepan and cover with water. Boil for 10 minutes, or until soft. Drain thoroughly and mash (preferably with a stick blender) until smooth. Stir in butter and season with salt & pepper.

Celery root has 50mg potassium per oz.

Makes four Servings

>
> Makes four Servings

Thanks for this, David! I like celery root better than cauliflower.

# Thread: Optimal Supplement Timing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### wyldeman — 2012-04-16T06:15:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/post-15512>

Hey Y'all !

Just looking for a simple guide to taking some of the common supplements (for those of us supplementing on the Leptin Rx, or for AI, Leaky Gut and/or Cortisol issues) in regards to timing and any optimal absorption (vs competing for absorption) issues.

For example, B Complex vitamins should not be taken before bed. correct?

Thanks !

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-16T08:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/post-15530>

I've heard the same about Vitamin D as August! I like to take mine with breakfast. I also take my Cod Liver Oil and Vitamin C at breakfast too.

Between meals, I take L-Glutamine between meals now. Originally I was taking it to fight carb cravings, but those have kinda died down just doing the Leptin RX. However, I think I may have a leaky gut, probably not as severe as others on the forum but... Yeah.  Usually a couple of hours after each meal I take my L-Glutamine. It's so it won't have to compete with other amino acids you got during your meals.

At night before bed I take my magnesium citrate before bed because I've heard it can make you sleepy. I haven't noticed that with myself, personally, but I figure it can't hurt to just take that at the end of the day.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T13:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/post-15536>

Many people have told me that vitamin B also should be taken in morning, except my homeopath tells me to take it right before bed. He said that is when the B vitamin works, so I have been doing it ever since and it doesn't mess with my sleep.

# Thread: DE

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-16T07:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15520>

For those of you taking DE, do you take it for maintenance (daily) or just to deal with gut issues and then stop? If the latter, how long have you taken it before stopping? I've read the benefits of specific use for treatment of parasites, etc but I've also read about the benefits of ongoing use. Anyone have experience/expertise to share?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-17T03:27:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15786>

Diatomaceous Earth (fossilized fresh water algae deposits)... typically used for gut health and other health benefits.

### shilohman — 2012-04-17T03:33:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15789>

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-17T03:35:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15790>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15810>

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-17T15:58:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15867>

You're welcome. I should add that I have parasites, candida and leaky gut, and I feel it has helped -- not cured -- but helped in all areas. Also, I felt a UTI coming on, and that's why I started DE, and it cleared right up. The benefits are numerous...I'm just not sure if it should be taken long-term or not. Would love if others can chime in on this.

### tweety — 2012-04-17T16:22:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15872>

> **PaleoCowgirl;15937 wrote:** You're welcome. I should add that I have parasites, candida and leaky gut, and I feel it has helped -- not cured -- but helped in all areas. Also, I felt a UTI coming on, and that's why I started DE, and it cleared right up. The benefits are numerous...I'm just not sure if it should be taken long-term or not. Would love if others can chime in on this.

Can I ask how you feel it has helped with the candida and parasites? I am working on candida too, and probably parasites if my stool test ever comes back in! I am trying to figure out how I know I am on the right path!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-17T16:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15880>

[http://www.candida-yeast-infection-relief.com/](http://http://www.candida-yeast-infection-relief.com/)

### tweety — 2012-04-17T23:06:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15978>

> **PaleoCowgirl;15950 wrote:** Here's an article I found very helpful on candida:
>
>
>
> [http://www.candida-yeast-infection-relief.com/](http://http://www.candida-yeast-infection-relief.com/)

Thanks! So are you using CandElim? I checked it out and its a little pricey? Or are you making your own with DE and other things?

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-17T23:10:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15979>

I'm only taking DE. Some people make a leaky gut mix with DE in it, but I just mix it with enough water to shoot it down. The one gem I took from that is too often people think they are fixed and stop taking whatever they are taking to fix candida, and then it returns even worse than before. That's why I'm of the mind that I should continue it for maintenance. To me, DE is cheap, easy and not too bad to take, so why not?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-18T01:15:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-16028>

> **tweety;15942 wrote:** Can I ask how you feel it has helped with the candida and parasites? I am working on candida too, and probably parasites if my stool test ever comes back in! I am trying to figure out how I know I am on the right path!

The only confirmed parasites we had in our household was our dog (who is 100% paleo/raw). We started giving him the DE and the parasites were gone within 48 hours.

We all take it maintenance right now, as we are trying to heal leaky guts and make sure any parasites/candida are dealt too. Like PaleoCowgirl, I find it cheap and easy to take (I put it in my children's smoothies and they can't taste it).

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-18T04:05:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-16107>

I need to get back to taking this regularly.

# Thread: I feel nauseated.....is it the dreaded Carb Flu?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-04-16T08:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15525>

I feel nauseated...is it the carb flu? I've been on the LR for a week....could it be that or is the timing wrong? I wish I'd taken enzymes with my dinner, but forgot....and guess I can't take them now....cuz I don't know if the food is in the stomach...but maybe I should take one anyway...gonna take one.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T13:39:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15534>

Janagram,

I never heard of the carb flu. I have been nauseated for a time now, but I believe it's from CO and detoxing sugar/carbs. Is that the carb flu? I have read here that some people were nauseated for a couple of weeks when they first went keto-paleo. All that garbage coming out or getting starved out. I knew there would be consequences for me, since I wasn't restricting anything for the better part of a year.

I hope you start to feel better soon.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-16T16:26:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15559>

Janagram, definitely the carb flu. Keep trucking along, and it will get better. Also, if you have added in coconut oil, take it slow. Too much too quick can also cause nausea! Good luck!

### nonchalant — 2012-04-16T16:58:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15567>

I felt nauseated when I first started upping my intake of animal fat. I think it took a while for my body to learn to digest so much of it.

Definitely not a problem any more.

### janagram — 2012-04-16T20:38:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15653>

Thanks....feeling better this morning and remembering to take enzymes and betaine.

# Thread: How Long Will hCG Shots Keep in Freezer?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MaggieC — 2012-04-16T08:51:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/post-15529>

Every round since my first I've kept my back up shots in the freezer after mixing. This round, for the last 5 days I've been incredibly hungry. I checked, and this batch of shots was mixed during the second week of January. Apparently 3 months in the freezer is too long. Does anyone here have a better idea how long they will last? Has anyone else kept track?

I mixed up a new batch this morning and took my first new batch shot, and I'm cold! So I know that there's a big difference between my old shots and this new batch. The other big clue was that my hunger disappeared.

Thanks!

### thomasina838@gmail.com — 2012-04-26T17:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/post-19040>

> **MaggieC;15598 wrote:** Every round since my first I've kept my back up shots in the freezer after mixing. This round, for the last 5 days I've been incredibly hungry. I checked, and this batch of shots was mixed during the second week of January. Apparently 3 months in the freezer is too long. Does anyone here have a better idea how long they will last? Has anyone else kept track?
>
>
>
> I mixed up a new batch this morning and took my first new batch shot, and I'm cold! So I know that there's a big difference between my old shots and this new batch. The other big clue was that my hunger disappeared.
>
>
>
> Thanks!

i am sorry no one answered you about this. but i think you already know the answer. 3 months is too long. i have used previously frozen hcg successfully after 6 weeks. but your body will know right away. so if i were using frozen hcg i would be sure to have some new i could mix. there are people who will tell you not to freeze it because the hcg molecule breaks down. maybe. but the fragments still work. but i dont think it works indefinitely. you already know that. i like the leptin stuff better than hcg. i tried my last round to do both so that meant a lot more protein and no fruit. no hunger but precious little weight loss as well. so no more hcg for me.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-26T17:55:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/post-19044>

Sorry it didn't work out but it sounds like you figured out the problem and are back on track.

Thomasina, you are losing on the leptin reset? Lots of women just don't. Glad that worked for you! Nothing works for me except hcg.

# Thread: My doctor - you need to go on cholesterol diet!

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-04-16T19:25:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15628>

I just got a call about my labs - everything is OK but you need to go on a cholesterol diet and watch your sugar!

I will get a copy of my all my labs on Wednesday when I go to see her. I am sure she will push a low fat!

Total 269

HDL 132

LDL 137

Trig 55

Ac1 5.8

From what I googled, my numbers are not so bad, right?

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-16T19:43:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15635>

At least he didn't try to put you on statins like mine did. I don't have any real issues now so I'm not too worried about my TC levels.

### Destiny — 2012-04-16T20:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15644>

> **donkjellberg;15705 wrote:** At least he didn't try to put you on statins like mine did. I don't have any real issues now so I'm not too worried about my TC levels.

I'll see on Wednesday what that cholesterol diet includes or not!

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-16T20:46:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15657>

I have looked at some of the long term observational studies. They seem to indicate that the sweet spot for TC is between 200-240 when correlating with lowest cause all mortality. When people dropped below or above their risks jumped. With your HDL so high that confounds things a bit.

Something to consider...

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-16T22:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15687>

Your numbes are incredible and your doctor is an idiot. Many recent studies have found that lower cholesterol for women corresponds with higher rates of death, and same results for men under a certain age (I think 60 or 70).

HDL is fantastic; Trigs are fantastic. Calculate that ratio and ask him about what it indicates (it indicates that your LDL is almost exclusively large & fluffy, which means absolutely no study has ever found it is linked with heart disease, and if he doesn't know that, he's still getting all his continuing education from statin reps).

When I went to my doctor 4 years ago, he looked at my numbers and told me to go on a low-fat, high-whole-grain diet and start an exercise program. I told him I'd been doing exactly that for the last fifteen years - OBSESSIVELY.

Sorry, I'm not feeling very tolerant today.

### Destiny — 2012-04-17T02:18:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15758>

>
>
>
> Sorry, I'm not feeling very tolerant today.

I know, it is frustrating.

Total: 249

HDL: 89

LDL:130

Trig: 94

### Destiny — 2012-04-19T03:45:30+0300

> **Souldanzer;16486 wrote:** Cholesterol is fine, your A1c is something to pay attention to, though... too high.

I know, I have been trying to lower it since last fall. My FBG started going up and up (between 99-114) when I started with LC and then with Leptin RX. When I started supplementing with estrogen in mid of Feb, my FBG started going down to 80th. Some days it goes up to low 90th, not sure why. I am hoping that next test will be better. From what I read, I should wait for 3 months before testing again.

### Destiny — 2012-04-19T03:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16433>

I actually got a copy of my tests today and I am mad. First of all, the cholesterol numbers I got over the phone were only partially correct. My HDL is 71 not 132 and my total is 219 not 269.

In addition, they ran a regular CRP instead of hsCRP! Is that any good? Or is it totally useless? In addition, my thyroid labs have stuff in it that I did not see - some T3 uptake, etc. but it is missing free T3, rT3 and antibodies. The TSH is marked as high. So that test is probably useless too.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-19T18:43:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16616>

sigh. cholesterol has NO relation to heart disease. WHEN are the docs going to get the word? Evidently never if big pharma has its say.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T18:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16621>

> **DarleenMB;16686 wrote:** sigh. cholesterol has NO relation to heart disease. WHEN are the docs going to get the word? Evidently never if big pharma has its say.

Cholesterol does have a relation to heart disease. It is the causality that is at issue. Realizing the mechanisms that trigger this relationship to heart disease is the challenging part to understand. Three things off the top of my head that can interact in this relationship are oxidation, inflammation, and pathogenic expression. They all affect cholesterol and its role in heart disease.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-19T19:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16644>

> **donkjellberg;16691 wrote:** Cholesterol does have a relation to heart disease. It is the causality that is at issue. Realizing the mechanisms that trigger this relationship to heart disease is the challenging part to understand. Three things off the top of my head that can interact in this relationship are oxidation, inflammation, and pathogenic expression. They all affect cholesterol and its role in heart disease.

THis is very similar to comments on my pictures of the Keto pizza I posted on my FB page - had a friend tell me that cholesterol was a marker for CVD. when in reality you HAVE to look at the Whole picture. As I have serious inflammation going on in my arteries- there's cause to be concerned; however my hsCRP is low.18 & my HbA1C is all over the place, and my TC is pretty damn high as is my LDL...oh and I'm not making the hormones I need - which is one reason why my LDL is high but it's the light fluffy pattern A...am I concerned no. Is my PCP - no. What critical is looking at the whole picture of Cholesterol, hsCRP, HbA1C, homocystine, hormones, Thyroid. its all connected. where cholesterol becomes an issues - is when you go to the ER w/chest pains, and you have high LDL. they automatically admit you and put you on statins to protect themselves. - incase you have a heart attack, especially as you move beyond the age of 60.

I'm interested to seeing what my labs look like in May - after being on BHRT, & CT for the past 2mths. and seeing what's changed. I would anticpate that the longer I'm on BHRT, and have enough hormonal support, my adrenal glands will stop telling my body to produce more LDL, and It will drop... especially as I continue forward w/LS., CT, keto and cyclical eating once I've reversed disease... I've got a long way to go - as my arterial age is 51 - tomorrow I will be 39.

### Destiny — 2012-04-20T02:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16803>

> **Souldanzer;16560 wrote:** hsCRP = cardiac CRP

Unfortunately, they did not do any of these, just the regular CRP.

### Dextery — 2012-04-21T06:44:23+0300

> See this video about targeting cholesterol...especially LDL. This should be seen by every PCP.
>
> [http://www.cardioexchange.org/voices/why-we-should-abandon-ldl-cholesterol-targets/](http://http://www.cardioexchange.org/voices/why-we-should-abandon-ldl-cholesterol-targets/)
>
>
>
> Edit: Even with your new numbers, you are doing just fine.

I believe my HDL will go up again and my LDL a bit down as soon as I optimize my hormones (steroid and thyroid).

The video is great! Tx!

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-21T18:34:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-17445>

> **Shijin13;16714 wrote:** THis is very similar to comments on my pictures of the Keto pizza I posted on my FB page - had a friend tell me that cholesterol was a marker for CVD. when in reality you HAVE to look at the Whole picture. As I have serious inflammation going on in my arteries- there's cause to be concerned; however my hsCRP is low.18 & my HbA1C is all over the place, and my TC is pretty damn high as is my LDL...oh and I'm not making the hormones I need - which is one reason why my LDL is high but it's the light fluffy pattern A...am I concerned no. Is my PCP - no. What critical is looking at the whole picture of Cholesterol, hsCRP, HbA1C, homocystine, hormones, Thyroid. its all connected. where cholesterol becomes an issues - is when you go to the ER w/chest pains, and you have high LDL. they automatically admit you and put you on statins to protect themselves. - incase you have a heart attack, especially as you move beyond the age of 60.
>
>
>
> I'm interested to seeing what my labs look like in May - after being on BHRT, & CT for the past 2mths. and seeing what's changed. I would anticpate that the longer I'm on BHRT, and have enough hormonal support, my adrenal glands will stop telling my body to produce more LDL, and It will drop... especially as I continue forward w/LS., CT, keto and cyclical eating once I've reversed disease... I've got a long way to go - as my arterial age is 51 - tomorrow I will be 39.

Excellent points. When I lost my weight, my doctor wanted to do the same . . . put me on statins. Having hormone issues can complicate things. I don't seem to have that issue and do not worry about my CHO since it is in the sweet spot putting me at the lowest risk statistically for all cause mortality. Cholesterol is not an issue per se but to say it has no relation to heart disease is a potentially dangerous assumption when not considering inflammation and other factors such as the hormone and metabolic dysfunction that precipitate the cascade which cholesterol plays a role . . . as I eat four eggs, bacon, and chicken meatballs for breakfast.

### Rick — 2012-04-20T15:12:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-all.927/post-16969>

Thank you Shilohman, nice to meet you too. In 1971 I moved off campus my sophomore year, joined the Fredonia food co-op and armed with my copies of Adele Davis and Atkins Diet Revolution, I just went for it...been cooking ever since. About 3 years ago I went strict Paleo to make one last effort to overcome a 30 year struggle with IBS/IBD and UC. So now I'm feeling better but I have no customers at all because I can't/won't cook grains, beans, potatoes etc. for clients. I've been a LC advocate since the 70's but experimented thru the 80's and 90's with every other type diet under the sun to alleviate my GI problems (of course I made things much worse). When I just cooked gourmet world cuisines I had some big clients and have cooked for Bruce Cakebread and Rene Schlatter (Merryvale vineyards) but I was putting on weight and making myself sicker and sicker. The jury is still out on whether my Paleo business model will work for personal cheffing, so far very difficult to sell customers on what I now know to be the truly healthiest cuisine. I do have a day gig at Consumer Reports testing computers and I am picking up alot more work training folks in gung-fu/kuntao combat for self-defense so we'll see how this all shakes out!
